stem: You are architecting a multi-agent research orchestrator on the Messages API. A lead Claude agent runs a tool-use loop: it calls search and fetch tools, you execute them, append the tool_result blocks, and re-call the model until the research turn completes. During load testing on a 40-source synthesis task, two failure modes surface. First, the loop sometimes halts early: the assistant writes "I now have enough to finish my summary" in a text block but the SAME response also contains a tool_use block requesting one more fetch — and your orchestrator stops there, dropping the final source. Second, on other runs the loop never stops even after the model has clearly produced its final answer, because the agent's prose never happens to contain a "done"-style phrase. You also notice that occasionally a server-side tool returns stop_reason "pause_turn". Which termination strategy correctly fixes both failure modes?
anti_pattern: Deciding when an agent loop is finished by scanning the model's natural-language output for words like "done" or "finished" (or by relying on a fixed iteration cap as the primary stop), instead of inspecting the API's structured stop_reason field. NL signals are unreliable: the model may say "done" mid-paragraph while still emitting tool_use, or finish cleanly without ever saying "done", and an iteration cap is only a safety backstop, not a correct terminator.
choice_a: Keep parsing the assistant's natural-language output, but make it robust: stop the loop only when the final text block contains an explicit completion phrase (e.g. "summary complete") AND the response contains no tool_use block. Add a system instruction telling the model to always emit "summary complete" when it is finished.
rationale_a: This still anchors termination on natural-language signals, which is the anti-pattern: the model can omit the phrase when genuinely finished or emit it while more tool calls remain, so the stop decision must come from the structured stop_reason, not from prose the model is merely instructed to produce.
choice_b: Set a fixed iteration cap (e.g. 12 tool rounds) as the primary stop condition: run the loop until the cap is reached and then take whatever the assistant's last response contains as the final answer, ignoring stop_reason entirely since the cap guarantees the loop always terminates.
rationale_b: A fixed iteration cap is only a safety backstop, never the primary terminator — it either truncates a turn that legitimately needed more rounds or wastes rounds after the model already returned end_turn, so correct termination must inspect stop_reason rather than rely on a hard count.
choice_c: Drive termination off stop_reason — loop while stop_reason == "tool_use" and stop on "end_turn" — and keep an iteration cap only as a safety backstop. When a server-side tool returns "pause_turn", append a user message whose content is the text "continue" so the model resumes the paused server-side tool call.
rationale_c: This gets the core stop_reason logic exactly right, but it embodies an objective mechanism error: pause_turn is resumed by re-sending the same user turn plus the assistant content, and appending a literal "continue" message is the documented anti-pattern — so the pause_turn path is handled incorrectly.
choice_d: Drive termination off the structured stop_reason: keep looping only while stop_reason == "tool_use" (execute the requested tools, append all tool_results in one user message, and re-call), and stop the loop when stop_reason == "end_turn". For a "pause_turn" result, resume by re-sending the prior user message plus the assistant content rather than appending a literal "continue". Keep a high iteration cap purely as a safety backstop, not as the primary stop condition.
rationale_d: Correct termination is determined by the API's structured stop_reason — loop on "tool_use", stop on "end_turn" — which is immune to where the model places phrases like "I now have enough"; pause_turn is resumed by re-sending context, and an iteration cap is only a backstop. This handles both the premature stop (the response still carried a tool_use block) and the runaway loop (end_turn fires regardless of prose).
guide_section: D1 §1.1 — Design and implement agentic loops for autonomous task execution

stem: At a fintech startup, you are building a coding agent on the large, most capable Claude model tier that fixes failing CI builds: it reads stack traces, edits files, and re-runs the test suite through a set of tools, looping until the build is green. The previous engineer hard-coded the harness to execute exactly 8 tool-use rounds and then return whatever the assistant last produced as the "final answer," reasoning that 8 rounds was empirically enough for the median fix. In production you observe two failure modes: large multi-file refactors get cut off mid-edit at round 8 with a broken tree, while trivial one-line fixes that the model clearly finished at round 3 still burn 5 wasted rounds before returning. A teammate proposes raising the cap to 20 to "give it room." How should you redesign the loop's termination so it correctly stops when the task is actually done?
anti_pattern: Treating a fixed iteration cap (or counting tool-use rounds) as the PRIMARY signal that an agent task is complete, rather than inspecting stop_reason. A cap stops the loop at an arbitrary point regardless of whether the model actually finished, so it both truncates legitimately long tasks and masks real completion; the correct primary stop is stop_reason == "end_turn", with the cap reserved only as a runaway-safety backstop.
choice_a: Drive the loop off stop_reason: keep looping while stop_reason == "tool_use" (execute the requested tools, append all matching tool_results, and re-call the model), and treat stop_reason == "end_turn" as the signal that the agent has finished. Keep a high iteration cap only as a runaway-safety backstop, and handle pause_turn by re-sending the accumulated messages to resume.
rationale_a: Correct termination is determined by inspecting stop_reason — the loop continues on "tool_use" and ends on "end_turn" — so the agent stops exactly when it is done, neither truncating long refactors nor wasting rounds on finished short fixes; the cap is only a safety backstop, not the primary stop.
choice_b: Set tool_choice to {type: "any"} for the whole session so the model is forced to keep calling tools, and terminate the loop only when a hard wall-clock timeout of 90 seconds is reached, returning whatever state exists at that point.
rationale_b: Forcing {type:"any"} prevents the model from ever ending its turn naturally (it can never emit end_turn), and a wall-clock timeout is just another arbitrary primary stop that ignores whether the task finished — both replace correct stop_reason inspection with an external cutoff.
choice_c: Loop while stop_reason == "tool_use" and stop on "end_turn", keeping the iteration cap purely as a backstop; and when a server-side tool returns stop_reason == "pause_turn", append a user message containing the text "continue" so the model resumes where it left off.
rationale_c: The stop_reason-driven loop is exactly right, but the pause_turn handling is objectively wrong: pause_turn is resumed by re-sending the existing user message plus the assistant's content blocks to continue the turn, NOT by appending a literal "continue" message — that injects a spurious user turn.
choice_d: Keep the fixed-iteration design but raise the cap to 20 rounds and, on every round, parse the assistant's natural-language text for phrases like "the build is now green" or "all tests pass"; stop the loop as soon as one of those phrases appears, otherwise stop at round 20.
rationale_d: This compounds two anti-patterns: a fixed cap as the primary stop still truncates or wastes rounds, and parsing free-text for completion phrases is unreliable — termination must be decided by stop_reason, not by string-matching the model's prose.
guide_section: D1 §1.1 — Design and implement agentic loops for autonomous task execution

stem: You are architecting a multi-agent research system on the Claude Agent platform for a legal-tech firm called Brightwater Counsel. A user query like "summarize our exposure across the Halloran matter" requires scanning roughly 240 documents across three repositories, where the discovery phase (finding, reading, and filtering relevant docs) produces an enormous volume of intermediate text — full document bodies, search hits, and rejected candidates — most of which the final synthesis never needs. Your lead orchestrator (running the large, most capable model tier) must produce a tight cited memo, but in early prototypes the orchestrator's 1M-token context fills with raw document dumps long before synthesis, degrading the final answer. Your team proposes several ways to restructure the workflow so the orchestrator's context stays focused on synthesis. Which approach best applies the platform's guidance for this orchestrator-worker situation?
anti_pattern: Believing that the context-preservation benefit of the orchestrator-worker pattern comes from running the discovery in the same context but compacting it afterward, or from a high iteration cap — rather than from giving the worker its own isolated context window and returning only a distilled summary to the orchestrator.
choice_a: Leave discovery in the orchestrator but raise the orchestrator's primary stop condition to a fixed iteration cap of 50 tool-calls, so the loop is forced to halt before the 1M-token context can be exhausted by document dumps.
rationale_a: An iteration cap is only a safety backstop, not a context-management mechanism — it neither keeps raw documents out of the orchestrator's context nor preserves the relevant findings, and correct termination is driven by inspecting stop_reason, not a fixed cap.
choice_b: Spawn a discovery subagent that runs in its own separate context window, performs the full 240-document scan and filtering there, and returns only a concise structured summary (the relevant findings plus citations) to the orchestrator; the orchestrator then synthesizes the memo from that summary, never ingesting the raw document bodies.
rationale_b: The orchestrator-worker pattern preserves the main context by isolating verbose discovery in a subagent with its own context window and returning only a distilled summary, so the orchestrator's context holds findings rather than raw dumps — exactly the documented purpose of subagents.
choice_c: Keep the entire discovery phase running inside the orchestrator's own context, but call /compact (or the equivalent compaction step) once the raw document dumps grow large, so the orchestrator reclaims context space before it begins synthesizing the memo.
rationale_c: This still ingests all the raw discovery output into the orchestrator's context first, and compaction is reactive and lossy — it summarizes after the fact and can drop detail — so it is not a substitute for proactively isolating the verbose work in a separate subagent context.
choice_d: Register all of the document-search, read, and filter operations as additional dedicated tools (about 20 of them) on the orchestrator agent so it can discover and prune documents more granularly within its single context.
rationale_d: Adding ~20 tools both degrades tool selection (overlapping, rarely-used tools make it harder for the model to pick the right one) and does nothing to keep the verbose intermediate document text out of the orchestrator's single context window, so it fails to address the context-saturation problem.
guide_section: D5 §5.4 — Manage context effectively in large codebase exploration

stem: At Meridian Claims, you are architecting a pipeline that turns each incoming insurance-claim PDF into a structured record. Every claim follows the same fixed sequence with no branching: extract 11 named fields from the PDF, validate each against a JSON schema, look up the policy number in an internal REST API, and write the result to a database. The field set, the validation rules, and the lookup call are all fully specified up front and identical for every document; the only variation is the document contents. The team processes about 40,000 claims a day and needs the per-document control flow to be auditable and the cost predictable. You are choosing the architecture for a single claim's processing on the Claude API (using the large, most capable model tier). Which approach best fits public platform guidance on choosing between a single API call, a code-orchestrated workflow, and an open-ended agent?
anti_pattern: Reaching for an open-ended, model-driven agent loop and relying on a fixed iteration cap as the PRIMARY termination and cost control for a task whose step sequence is fully known and specifiable in advance. Public platform guidance says to default to the simplest tier that meets the need, to use an agent only when the task is multi-step AND hard to fully specify up front, and to treat a fixed iteration count as a safety backstop only — never the primary control that guarantees an auditable, predictable per-document control flow.
choice_a: Use a single Claude API call per document: pass the PDF and instruct the model to extract the 11 fields, validate them, perform the policy-number lookup, and return the final database record, all in one request with structured output.
rationale_a: A single API call is the right tier only for a one-shot task like pure extraction or classification. One model turn cannot itself execute the external REST policy-lookup or the database write — those are code-executed side effects that must happen between turns — so collapsing this four-step pipeline into one request is not viable regardless of how the prompt is worded.
choice_b: Build an agent loop: give Claude an extract tool, a validate tool, and a policy-lookup tool, let the model decide the trajectory each run, and cap it at 6 agentic iterations per document as the primary mechanism to guarantee the loop terminates and stays within a predictable budget.
rationale_b: Wrong because of one specific, checkable flaw: it makes a fixed iteration cap (6 iterations) the PRIMARY termination and cost control. Public agent-loop guidance is that you terminate correctly by inspecting stop_reason — the loop runs while stop_reason=="tool_use" and stops on end_turn — and that a fixed iteration count is only a safety backstop, never the primary control. As a backstop the cap is fine; as the primary guarantee it is the wrong mechanism, and it does not deliver the auditable, deterministic per-document flow the team needs (the same number of iterations can run a different trajectory each time). The tier itself is also more than this task requires, but the decisive, objective error is treating the cap as the primary control rather than a backstop.
choice_c: Build a Managed Agent: create a persisted agent with the prebuilt toolset, start a session per claim so the platform runs the loop and hosts a container, and let the agent work the document to completion autonomously in its workspace.
rationale_c: Managed Agents fit server-managed, stateful, open-ended work where you want the platform to run the agent loop and host the tool-execution sandbox. Spinning up a hosted container and an autonomous loop per claim for a fixed, high-volume, fully-specified pipeline adds latency, cost, and trajectory variability — the opposite of the auditable, predictable control flow a code-orchestrated workflow gives you for free.
choice_d: Build a code-orchestrated workflow: your code drives the fixed sequence and owns the loop, calling Claude for the structured field extraction (with output_config.format / strict tool use) and invoking the schema validator and the policy-lookup tool as deterministic steps you control. Reserve a Claude call only for the extraction step that genuinely needs the model.
rationale_d: Public platform guidance is to default to the simplest tier that meets the need and to use a code-orchestrated workflow when you have a multi-step pipeline whose logic is code-controlled — exactly this case, since the step sequence is fixed and fully specifiable in advance. With your code owning the loop, termination is structural (the sequence ends when the four steps complete), giving the auditable per-document control flow and predictable cost the team requires, while Claude is invoked only for the one step (extraction) that needs a model.
guide_section: Appendix — Out of scope for the exam (drill-only)

stem: At Meridian Analytics, you're designing a "research desk" agent that compiles competitive-intelligence briefs. Over three sprints the team kept bolting capabilities onto one agent, and it now exposes 22 tools spanning web search, an internal SQL warehouse, a PDF extractor, three CRM connectors, a Slack poster, and several charting utilities. QA reports that on multi-step briefs the agent increasingly picks the wrong tool — calling the CRM lookup when it should query the warehouse, or invoking the PDF extractor on a web URL — and accuracy has dropped versus the early two-tool prototype. Leadership wants the full capability set preserved but the wrong-tool errors fixed. Which approach best follows the platform's published guidance on agent and tool design?
anti_pattern: Loading one agent with a very large, undifferentiated tool set (here 22 tools) so that tool-selection accuracy degrades, instead of keeping each agent to a focused handful of tools and using mechanisms (subagents, tool search) that add capability without bloating the live tool list.
choice_a: Decompose the work so the orchestrator keeps ~4–5 focused tools and delegates specialized work to subagents (e.g. a warehouse-query subagent, a document-extraction subagent), each scoped to its own small tool set; capabilities the orchestrator only occasionally needs are surfaced on demand via tool search, which appends the matching tool schemas to the request rather than keeping them permanently in the live tool list.
rationale_a: The platform's guidance is to keep ~4–5 focused tools per agent because exposing ~18+ tools degrades tool selection. Subagents isolate specialized tool sets in separate contexts, and tool search appends schemas on demand — preserving both selection accuracy and the cached tools+system prefix — so the full capability set is kept without bloating any one agent's live tool list.
choice_b: Decompose the work into specialized subagents (warehouse, document-extraction, CRM) each with a small tool set, and for the few tools the orchestrator needs only occasionally, dynamically swap them into and out of the orchestrator's tools array per request so its live list stays around 4–5 tools at any moment.
rationale_b: The subagent decomposition and the ~4–5-tool target are exactly right, which makes this tempting — but the mechanism for occasional tools is wrong. Changing the tools array between requests invalidates the cached tools→system prefix every time (any byte change in the prefix invalidates everything after it), forcing a full cache write. The published mechanism is tool SEARCH, which appends schemas on demand without mutating the stable prefix, so it adds capability while keeping the cache intact.
choice_c: Leave all 22 tools on the one agent and instead raise the effort level to "xhigh" in output_config so the model spends more thinking on each call and reliably reasons its way to the correct tool.
rationale_c: Higher effort increases thinking, tool-calls, and cost but is not the remedy for selection degradation caused by an oversized tool list; the published fix is to reduce the number of live tools per agent, not to think harder over 22 of them.
choice_d: Keep all 22 tools defined on the single agent but set tool_choice to {type:"any"} so the model is always forced to select one of the existing tools, which constrains it to the valid tool set and prevents the wrong-tool calls.
rationale_d: tool_choice:{type:"any"} only forces the model to use SOME tool rather than answering in plain text — it does not reduce the 22-tool count or improve WHICH tool is chosen, so the selection-degradation root cause from exposing ~18+ tools remains unaddressed.
guide_section: D2 §2.3 — Distribute tools appropriately across agents and configure tool choice

stem: You are architecting a litigation-research agent for the fictional firm Vance & Roe. A single research task fans out across roughly 40 sequential tool calls: a `fetch_filing` tool pulls full court filings (each 30K-80K tokens of raw text), an `extract_holdings` tool distills each filing to a short structured summary (~400 tokens), and a `tally_citations` tool aggregates citation counts across all summaries to produce one final 12-row table. In early prototypes the conversation context ballooned past 600K tokens and per-task cost spiked, because every raw filing and every intermediate summary was being appended to the message history as tool_results before the next call. The partners only ever need the final 12-row table; the raw filings and per-filing summaries are disposable scratch work. Which approach best controls the context growth while preserving correctness?
anti_pattern: Routing large or disposable intermediate tool outputs back through the model's context. The near-miss correctly isolates the verbose raw-filing work in subagents but still collects every per-filing summary into the main model's context to run the aggregation, so context grows linearly with the number of filings instead of staying flat.
choice_a: Switch the main model to the large, most capable tier for its 1M-token context window so the 600K tokens of filings and summaries fit comfortably, and rely on the larger window to hold all intermediate results.
rationale_a: A larger context window accommodates the bloat rather than preventing it. The cost spike persists because every appended raw filing is still billed as input tokens each turn, and the design ignores the documented technique of keeping disposable intermediates out of context entirely. Window size is a capacity ceiling, not a cost-control mechanism.
choice_b: Keep the normal tool-use loop but, for each filing, spawn a small fast-tier subagent that runs `fetch_filing` then `extract_holdings` in its own isolated context and returns only its ~400-token structured summary; the main agent collects all ~40 summaries into its context and then calls `tally_citations` over them.
rationale_b: This is genuinely close and gets the hard part right: pushing each `fetch_filing`/`extract_holdings` pair into a subagent keeps the 30K-80K-token raw filings out of the main context entirely, which is the correct documented use of subagents for isolating verbose discovery. The single objective flaw is that the main agent still collects all ~40 summaries into its own context to run the final tally, so context and cost still grow linearly with the number of filings instead of staying flat. The remedy for an aggregation whose only output is a small final value is to keep that aggregation in code too, which programmatic tool calling does and routing every summary back through the model does not.
choice_c: Use programmatic tool calling: have Claude write a single orchestration script that itself invokes `fetch_filing`, `extract_holdings`, and `tally_citations` in sequence, so the 40 raw filings and all intermediate summaries are held in the script's execution environment and never appended to the conversation; only the final 12-row table is returned into the model's context.
rationale_c: Programmatic tool calling lets Claude orchestrate many tool calls from a script it writes, so large and disposable intermediate results are processed by code and never appended to the conversation. Only the small final value (the 12-row table) re-enters context, so context stays roughly flat whether there are 40 filings or 400. This is exactly the documented remedy for pipelines that produce big intermediate payloads needed only transiently.
choice_d: Leave the architecture unchanged but enable prompt caching with a 1-hour TTL and a cache_control breakpoint after the growing message history, so the accumulated filings and summaries are read back at the ~0.1x cache-read rate.
rationale_d: Caching only lowers the read price of tokens already in context; it does nothing to keep the 600K+ tokens out of the window. Worse, appending a new tool_result each turn changes the prefix, and any byte change in a cached prefix invalidates everything after it, so this neither bounds context growth nor maintains a stable cacheable prefix.
guide_section: Appendix — Out of scope for the exam (drill-only)

stem: You are building a multi-agent research orchestrator named "Atlas" on the Claude Messages API. For a market-sizing query, the lead agent, running a balanced mid-tier model, emits a single assistant message containing four parallel tool_use blocks — search_filings, fetch_pricing, query_competitors, and read_internal_memo — each with its own tool_use_id. Your harness dispatches all four concurrently across a worker pool; read_internal_memo fails with a 404 because the document was archived, while the other three return normally. A teammate proposes that the harness send each tool_result back as it completes — appending a separate user message per result in completion order, and simply omitting read_internal_memo's result since it errored — so the lead agent can "start reasoning on the fast results sooner." The next assistant turn then continues the loop. How should Atlas return these results to preserve correct parallel tool use, and why?
anti_pattern: Mishandling a failed tool call in a parallel batch: omitting the failed tool's result, or fabricating a stub success, instead of returning a tool_result with is_error=true plus structured error context. Compounded by splitting the parallel tool_results across multiple user messages rather than returning them together. Both break the protocol contract that all tool_results for one assistant turn arrive in a single following user message, each carrying its matching tool_use_id and a truthful per-tool status.
choice_a: Discard this assistant turn and re-issue the original research request with disable_parallel_tool_use:true so the agent emits one tool_use at a time, avoiding the need to coordinate multiple results entirely.
rationale_a: Throwing away a valid assistant turn and forcing serial tool calls needlessly sacrifices the latency benefit of the four concurrent calls and does not address how to correctly return results — the protocol already supports returning all parallel results together in one message, each with its tool_use_id and status.
choice_b: Return all four tool_results in one user message keyed by their tool_use_ids and re-call the API, but for read_internal_memo omit the block entirely since the tool failed — the three successful results are enough for the model to proceed.
rationale_b: The single-message batching and tool_use_id keying are exactly right, so this tempts a prepared candidate. The one objective flaw is omitting the failed tool's result: a failed tool must return a tool_result with is_error=true (plus context) and never be silently dropped, otherwise the assistant turn has an unanswered tool_use_id and the model loses the signal that the memo lookup 404'd — it cannot reason about or recover from a failure it never sees.
choice_c: Send each tool_result as a separate user message in completion order as the teammate suggests, so the lead agent can begin reasoning on the fast results before the slower ones land, and include a stub success result for read_internal_memo so no tool_use_id is left unanswered.
rationale_c: Splitting parallel tool_results across multiple user messages degrades parallel tool use, and fabricating a stub success for a tool that actually 404'd hides the failure from the model — both violate the protocol that all results return together in one message with a truthful per-tool status (is_error=true for the failure).
choice_d: Return all four tool_results in ONE user message immediately following the assistant turn, each block carrying its matching tool_use_id; include read_internal_memo's result with is_error=true and the 404 context rather than omitting it. Then re-call the API to get the next assistant turn.
rationale_d: When an assistant message contains multiple parallel tool_use blocks, all corresponding tool_results must be returned together in the single user message that follows, each keyed by its matching tool_use_id; splitting them across messages degrades parallel tool use. A tool that failed must be returned with is_error=true plus structured error context (not silently dropped and not faked), so the model can see the failure and decide how to recover.
guide_section: D1 §1.1 — Design and implement agentic loops for autonomous task execution

stem: You are the architect for a Tier-1 support agent at a fintech firm, Larkspur Pay, that handles ~14,000 chat sessions a day across billing, account-recovery, and dispute workflows. The agent has tools for refunds (capped at $50 without a human), KYC lookups, and dispute filing, plus a documented policy covering those three flows; anything outside them is meant to go to a human specialist queue. During the pilot, the product team notices two recurring patterns: (1) customers who use angry or frantic language about routine, in-policy billing questions, and (2) calm, polite customers asking for a $4,000 chargeback reversal — a flow the agent has no tool or policy for. Leadership wants you to define the single rule that decides when the agent hands off to a human. The team proposes several designs and asks you to choose the one most consistent with the platform's guidance on agent escalation.
anti_pattern: Wiring the escalation/handoff trigger to detected user emotion (frustration, anger, urgency) or to a self-reported model confidence score, rather than to objective signals — genuine task complexity or an explicit gap in the agent's policy/tool coverage. Sentiment- and confidence-driven escalation produces unreliable, gameable handoffs that fire on tone instead of on whether the agent can actually complete the task.
choice_a: Trigger handoff when the request falls outside the agent's documented policy/tool coverage or exceeds a hard limit it cannot satisfy (e.g., the $4,000 chargeback flow it has no tool or policy for, or a refund above the $50 cap) — i.e., escalate on task complexity and explicit policy gaps, and keep handling in-policy billing questions regardless of how angry or frantic the customer's language is.
rationale_a: The platform's agent-design guidance is to escalate on task complexity and explicit policy/coverage gaps, not on user sentiment: the objective signal of whether the agent can complete the task (it has the tool and the request is within policy/limits) is what should gate the handoff, so the unsupported $4,000 chargeback escalates while the in-policy billing question is handled even when phrased angrily.
choice_b: Escalate whenever the request is outside documented policy or over a hard tool limit, OR whenever the agent's self-reported confidence score for handling the request drops below 0.7 — combining the policy-gap check with a confidence threshold so low-certainty cases also reach a human.
rationale_b: The policy-gap half is exactly right, but adding a self-reported confidence-score threshold as a co-trigger is the objective flaw: the platform's guidance says to escalate on task complexity and explicit policy gaps, NOT on a self-reported confidence score, which is unreliable and gameable and is explicitly not a recommended escalation signal.
choice_c: Let the agent attempt every request and only hand off after it has looped on the task without resolving it for a fixed cap of 8 tool-calling turns, treating that turn cap as the primary escalation signal.
rationale_c: A fixed iteration cap is only a safety backstop, not the primary control: making it the main escalation trigger means the agent burns turns attempting out-of-policy work like the $4,000 chargeback it can never complete, instead of recognizing the explicit policy/tool gap up front and handing off immediately.
choice_d: Run a sentiment classifier on every message and hand off to a human the moment a customer's detected frustration or anger crosses a threshold, since emotionally charged sessions are the ones most likely to need a human's empathy.
rationale_d: This is the canonical sentiment-escalation anti-pattern: it gates handoff on detected user emotion rather than on whether the agent can actually complete the task, so it would needlessly escalate angry-but-in-policy billing questions while potentially keeping the calm, genuinely-unsupported $4,000 chargeback in the agent's hands.
guide_section: D5 §5.2 — Design effective escalation and ambiguity resolution patterns

stem: You are architecting a Claude-powered support agent for Meridian Telecom that resolves billing disputes autonomously and hands off to a human when it should not act alone. The agent has tools to read accounts, issue credits up to a policy ceiling, and look up the refund-policy knowledge base. During a design review, a teammate proposes the escalation rule: "After drafting its resolution, the agent emits a self_confidence field between 0.0 and 1.0; if it returns below 0.75 we route the conversation to a human, otherwise it executes the credit." In a 4,000-conversation shadow run, several cases where the agent confidently issued an out-of-policy credit had self-reported confidence above 0.9, while many correctly-handled routine refunds scored near 0.6 and were needlessly escalated. The lead asks you to redesign the escalation trigger so it is reliable for this hard-to-reverse action. Which approach best reflects the platform's published agent-design guidance?
anti_pattern: Routing the human-escalation decision on a number the model emits about itself (a self-reported confidence score) rather than on objective, programmatically-checkable conditions like task complexity or an explicit policy gap. Self-reported confidence is not a calibrated signal — the model can be fluently wrong with high stated confidence — so it makes an unreliable trigger for hard-to-reverse actions like handing off to a human.
choice_a: Keep the self_confidence field but recalibrate the rule: have the agent also emit a structured complexity assessment, then escalate whenever self_confidence is below 0.85 OR the agent flags the case as complex, so both the model's confidence and a complexity signal must agree before it acts autonomously.
rationale_a: The objective flaw is that the rule still keys the autonomous-action decision on the model's self-reported confidence score, which the platform guidance explicitly says not to escalate on. Under the proposed OR rule the agent acts alone only when confidence is at or above 0.85 AND it does not self-flag complexity — but the shadow run showed out-of-policy actions scoring above 0.9, exactly the high-confidence band that this rule lets execute. Adding the complexity signal is the correct half of the fix, yet because the model can be fluently wrong with high stated confidence on a case it also fails to flag as complex, retaining the confidence number as a trigger leaves the irreversible credit unreliable.
choice_b: Remove the escalation branch and instead cap the agent at a fixed maximum of 8 reasoning-and-tool iterations per conversation; if it hasn't produced a resolution within that many turns, abandon autonomy and hand the case to a human.
rationale_b: A fixed iteration cap is only a safety backstop, not a correctness signal — an agent can confidently issue an out-of-policy credit on turn 2, well under the cap, so this neither prevents the irreversible error nor escalates on complexity or a policy gap.
choice_c: Route the escalation decision on conversation sentiment: run a sentiment classifier on the customer's messages and hand off to a human whenever the detected frustration or anger crosses a threshold, since upset customers are the ones most likely to need human judgment.
rationale_c: Platform guidance explicitly says to escalate on task complexity or an explicit policy gap, NOT on sentiment; a calm customer can still trigger an out-of-policy action and an angry one can have a trivially-resolvable request, so sentiment does not track whether the agent should act alone.
choice_d: Escalate based on objective, programmatically-checkable conditions — e.g., the requested credit exceeds the policy ceiling, the dispute type isn't covered by a matched knowledge-base policy (an explicit policy gap), or required account fields are missing — evaluated by deterministic code outside the model's free text, and gate the irreversible credit action behind a dedicated tool that enforces those checks rather than leaving it as an open bash-style action.
rationale_d: Platform guidance is to escalate on task complexity or an explicit policy gap rather than on sentiment or a self-reported confidence score, and to promote hard-to-reverse actions to dedicated, gated tools; deterministic checks over objective conditions are calibrated and auditable in a way a model's stated confidence is not.
guide_section: D5 §5.2 — Design effective escalation and ambiguity resolution patterns

stem: At Meridian Capital Research, an analyst team runs a multi-agent literature-synthesis pipeline: a lead agent on the large, most capable model tier dispatches four subagents to gather sources, then drafts a 30-page market report citing roughly 180 references. Leadership requires that every report pass a factual-grounding check — each claim must trace to a real cited source — before it goes to clients. In the first version of the pipeline the same lead agent re-read its own draft at the end of its turn and emitted a pass/fail grade against a rubric, but auditors found it rubber-stamped several claims that were not actually supported by the cited sources. The architect now wants the grounding check to be a genuinely independent verification step, consistent with the platform's published guidance on agent verification. Which redesign should the architect choose?
anti_pattern: Trusting an agent's judgment of its own output when the verifier shares the reasoning that produced that output (self-review bias) — including the subtler failure of spinning up a fresh verifier session but feeding it the producer's chain-of-thought, which re-imports the very bias separate-context verification exists to remove.
choice_a: Run the grounding check in a brand-new verifier session (separate from the lead agent), and to give it the full picture, pass it the finished report, the cited sources, AND the lead agent's complete chain-of-thought from drafting so it can see why each claim was made before grading it.
rationale_a: This correctly moves verification into a separate session — the headline fix — but commits one specific, checkable flaw: feeding the verifier the producer's chain-of-thought re-imports the exact reasoning bias that separate-context verification is meant to remove. The platform's guidance is that the fresh session should receive only the artifact and the sources; the drafting reasoning must be withheld, so this design is independent in name only.
choice_b: Drop the grounding check entirely and instead enforce a fixed iteration cap so the lead agent stops drafting after 12 turns, treating a clean stop as evidence that the citations are sound.
rationale_b: A fixed iteration cap is only a safety backstop, not a correctness signal, and a clean stop says nothing about whether claims are grounded in their sources; this both removes the required check and misuses termination as a proxy for verification.
choice_c: Run the grounding check in a fresh verification session that is given ONLY the finished report and the cited sources — not the lead agent's drafting reasoning — and grades each claim against the rubric, so the verifier carries none of the reasoning bias from having produced the draft.
rationale_c: The platform's agent-design guidance holds that verification in a separate session/context beats same-session self-review precisely because the verifier carries no reasoning bias from producing the output. Independence is preserved only when the fresh context receives the artifact and the sources alone — the drafting reasoning is deliberately withheld.
choice_d: Keep the same-session self-review, but raise the lead agent's effort to max and instruct it to escalate to a human reviewer whenever its self-reported confidence in the grounding grade falls below 0.9.
rationale_d: This retains the biased same-session review and adds a self-reported-confidence escalation trigger, which the platform's guidance specifically warns against — escalation should be driven by task complexity or an explicit policy gap, not by a model's confidence score, which is not a reliable signal.
guide_section: D4 §4.6 — Design multi-instance and multi-pass review architectures

stem: At a SaaS company called Brightline, the support team wants to use Claude to handle ~4,000 inbound tickets per day. The tickets fall into three clearly distinct, non-overlapping categories — billing disputes, password resets, and multi-step bug reports — and each category needs a different system prompt, a different tool set, and (to control cost) a different model. Billing disputes require reconciling charges across several invoices and bug reports require reasoning over stack traces and repro steps, so both go to the balanced mid-tier model; password resets are a scripted lookup-and-send flow, so they go to the small, fast model tier. The first step must reliably read each incoming ticket and direct it to exactly one of the three category-specific handlers, and each handler then composes a single reply. There is no requirement to iteratively refine or re-grade any reply. Which workflow shape best fits this first step?
anti_pattern: Choosing the right workflow shape (routing) but mis-wiring the model tiers — sending the cheapest/fastest model to the categories that need the most capability and the most capable model to the trivial category. The trap is that the surrounding structure is correct, so the flaw hides in one inverted assignment that contradicts the cost/capability rationale routing exists to exploit.
choice_a: A prompt-chaining workflow: every ticket passes sequentially through the billing handler, then the password-reset handler, then the bug-report handler, with each step adding to the reply before the final response is sent.
rationale_a: Prompt chaining decomposes one task into fixed sequential subtasks where every step runs on every input. Here the categories are mutually exclusive, so running all three handlers on each ticket is wasteful and produces incoherent replies — the categories are alternatives to choose between, not stages to run in order.
choice_b: A routing workflow: an initial classification step dispatches each ticket to one of three specialized handlers, but routes the billing disputes and bug reports to the small, fast tier to minimize cost on the two highest-volume categories, and sends the scripted password resets to the mid-tier model.
rationale_b: The shape is correct — this is genuinely routing with per-category handlers — but the model tiers are inverted. It hands the cheapest, lowest-capability tier to the two categories the stem says need reasoning (multi-invoice reconciliation, stack-trace analysis) and spends the more capable tier on the trivial scripted lookup. That contradicts the whole point of tiered routing: match the cheap model to the easy case and the capable model to the hard case. The single objective flaw is the swapped assignment, checkable against the stem's stated difficulty per category.
choice_c: A routing workflow: an initial classification step inspects each ticket and dispatches it to one of the three specialized handlers — the small, fast tier for the scripted password resets, and the balanced mid-tier model for both the billing disputes and the multi-step bug reports — each handler using its own prompt and tools.
rationale_c: Routing is the right shape when inputs fall into distinct categories each better served by a separate, specialized path. It lets you send the simple, scripted case to the cheaper, faster small tier and reserve the more capable mid-tier model for the cases that actually need reasoning, which is exactly the cost/capability separation the scenario describes.
choice_d: An evaluator-optimizer workflow: one handler drafts a reply for every ticket, then a separate evaluator step critiques it and sends it back for revision in a loop until the reply is judged good enough, regardless of category.
rationale_d: Evaluator-optimizer fits tasks with a clear quality signal to iterate against (e.g., refining a translation or a search until it meets a rubric). Here the stem explicitly states there is no requirement to iteratively refine or re-grade replies, and the real need is to dispatch each ticket to the correct category handler — that is routing, not a refinement loop.
guide_section: D1 §1.6 — Design task decomposition strategies for complex workflows

stem: Meridian Atlas runs a long-lived research orchestration agent on the large, most capable Claude model tier. Across a single 40-minute session it accumulates a large cached prefix: a frozen 9,000-token system prompt, a deterministically sorted toolset of 11 tools, and roughly 60 turns of conversation history, all protected by cache_control breakpoints and confirmed via usage.cache_read_input_tokens on every call. Midway through, the orchestrator needs to perform ~200 cheap, high-volume "does this paragraph mention a named competitor?" classifications over fetched documents — work that clearly does not need large-tier reasoning. A teammate proposes switching the main loop to the small, fast model tier for the classification stretch, then switching back to the large tier afterward, arguing this keeps the existing conversation and cache intact while cutting cost on the cheap part. The team's hard constraint is that the orchestrator's accumulated cached prefix must keep getting cache hits before and after the cheap work. Which approach satisfies the architecture goals while respecting how prompt caching works?
anti_pattern: Treating a mid-session model switch on the main agent loop as a free cost optimization, ignoring that changing the model invalidates the entire prompt cache (prefix match) — and conflating "use a cheaper model for one sub-task" with "swap the model the long-lived cached loop runs on." The correct move is to isolate the cheap-model sub-task in a subagent with its own context so the main loop's cached prefix (tools + frozen system prompt + accumulated history) is never touched.
choice_a: Switch the main loop from the large tier to the small, fast tier for the classification stretch and then switch back to the large tier, since the conversation history, system prompt, and tool definitions are all kept byte-for-byte identical across the switch and are protected by cache_control, so the cached prefix is preserved.
rationale_a: The objective flaw: changing the model invalidates the entire prompt cache regardless of whether the bytes of the prefix are unchanged, because the cache is keyed per-model — so the switch to the small tier (and back) forces full cache writes, not the cache_read hits the constraint requires.
choice_b: Keep everything on the large tier and simply lower the effort to "low" and disable thinking for the classification turns; toggling effort and thinking does not invalidate the tools+system cache, so this gets the cost down without any model change.
rationale_b: While it is true that toggling effort/thinking does not invalidate the tools+system cache, this does not satisfy the goal of running the high-volume work on a cheaper model — it leaves all 200 classifications on the large tier, so the per-token cost of the cheap work is not actually reduced to the cheaper tier.
choice_c: Remove the cache_control breakpoints before the classification stretch so the prefix is no longer cached, run the classifications on the main large-tier loop, then re-add the breakpoints afterward to rebuild the cache cheaply.
rationale_c: Removing cache breakpoints abandons the cache hits the team explicitly requires and does nothing to move the cheap work to a cheaper model; re-adding breakpoints just forces a fresh cache write (≈1.25× cost), making this strictly worse than leaving the cache intact.
choice_d: Keep the main orchestration loop on the large tier untouched, and dispatch the 200 classifications to a subagent running the small, fast model in its own isolated context; the subagent returns a concise summary of results. The main loop's cached prefix is never altered, so it continues to get cache_read hits, while the cheap high-volume work runs on the cheaper model in a separate context.
rationale_d: A subagent isolates the cheap-model sub-task in a separate context and returns a concise summary, so the main loop's tools + frozen system prompt + history prefix is never modified and keeps hitting the cache; running a cheaper model for a high-volume sub-task is exactly the documented use of a subagent.
guide_section: Appendix — Out of scope for the exam (drill-only)

stem: You maintain a long-running research agent at Helibren Labs built on the Messages API with the large, most capable Claude model tier. Each work session it produces ~3-4 KB of findings (decisions made, open questions, source URLs) that the NEXT session must be able to read back. The current implementation keeps a single frozen system prompt (the agent's role, ~40 stable tool definitions) and, at the end of each session, appends that session's findings to the END of the system prompt before saving it for next time. After ~30 sessions the system prompt has grown past 150 KB, every run is slow and expensive, and a teammate notices `usage.cache_read_input_tokens` is near zero on every call even though nothing about the role or tools changed. You need cross-session continuity that stays cheap. What is the best fix?
anti_pattern: Persisting long-horizon agent state by appending every prior session's notes into the system prompt, which inflates per-request cost, invalidates the prompt cache, and eventually overruns the context window.
choice_a: Keep appending all prior findings into the system prompt, but switch the agent from the large tier to the small, fast tier so the larger carried history is cheaper per token and fits more cheaply in context.
rationale_a: This keeps the root cause — a growing, prefix-mutating system prompt that invalidates the cache every session — and only lowers the per-token rate. It does not restore cache reads (switching the model itself also invalidates the cache), does not stop the prompt from eventually overrunning even the small tier's smaller 200K context, and trades away large-tier capability the research task may need. It addresses neither the continuity mechanism nor predictable cost.
choice_b: Drop the saved findings entirely and rely on Claude's training knowledge plus a fresh web search at the start of each session to reconstruct what prior sessions decided.
rationale_b: This abandons cross-session continuity rather than making it cheap. The model cannot reconstruct session-specific decisions, open questions, or chosen source URLs from training data or a generic search — that state was produced by the agent and exists nowhere on the public web. It also reruns expensive discovery every session. The requirement is to persist and read back concrete prior state cheaply, which external file-based memory does and this does not.
choice_c: Keep the system prompt frozen (role + tools, in stable order) so its cached prefix is reused, write each session's findings to an external file (e.g. memory/state.md), and at the start of each session load that file's current contents into a user/tool message so the model reads prior state without ever mutating the cached prefix.
rationale_c: Prompt caching is a prefix match: any byte change in the prefix invalidates everything after it, and tools+system render before messages. Appending findings to the system prompt mutates the prefix every session, which is exactly why cache_read is near zero and cost keeps climbing. Moving the volatile, growing state OUT of the system prompt and into file-based memory loaded via a message keeps the system+tools prefix byte-identical (so it caches and is read at ~0.1x), while still giving the next session full continuity. This is the file-based / external-memory pattern for long-horizon agents.
choice_d: Keep the system prompt frozen, write each session's findings to an external file, and at the start of each session load that file's contents by appending them to the END of the system prompt after the frozen role and tool definitions.
rationale_d: This correctly externalizes state into a file and keeps the role/tools text itself unchanged, which is most of the right idea. It is wrong by one specific, checkable detail: it still injects the loaded state into the system prompt, after the frozen prefix. Because caching is a strict prefix match and the appended state changes every session, the bytes that follow are different each run, so the system+tools prefix is still re-billed as a cache write rather than a read. The fix must place the volatile state in a message (which renders AFTER system and does not move the cached system/tools prefix), not anywhere inside the system block.
guide_section: D5 §5.4 — Manage context effectively in large codebase exploration

stem: A research orchestrator agent named "Helix" runs an 80-step literature-survey loop. At each step it calls a `fetch_source` tool that returns a 30–60K-token raw HTML/PDF dump, immediately extracts a 3–4 sentence finding plus the citation URL into a running notes structure, and then never re-reads the raw dump again. After ~25 steps the context window is dominated by dozens of these consumed raw dumps, even though the distilled findings and citations they produced are small and must remain verbatim through step 80 so the final report can cite exact URLs and quoted passages. The architecture lead must keep the window under control without corrupting any citation. Which approach best fits Helix's history-management needs?
anti_pattern: Treating reactive, lossy summarization (compaction) as interchangeable with deterministic removal of stale, fully-consumed tool output (context editing / clear). The trap is choosing the mechanism that discards recoverable raw data the agent still needs to cite, or that destroys the reasoning chain the agent still depends on, rather than matching the mechanism to which bytes are stale versus still-load-bearing.
choice_a: Use compaction to summarize the entire running history—including the raw `fetch_source` dumps, the distilled findings, and the citation URLs—into a single condensed digest every 25 steps, so the window stays small while the agent retains a summary of all sources gathered so far.
rationale_a: Compaction is lossy summarization; folding the verbatim citation URLs and quoted passages into a summary risks paraphrasing or dropping the exact strings the final report must cite, so it is the wrong mechanism for data that must survive verbatim.
choice_b: Use context editing to clear the large, already-consumed `fetch_source` tool-result blocks once their findings have been extracted, while leaving the distilled findings and citation URLs untouched in context; this deterministically removes the stale raw bytes that are the actual source of bloat without summarizing or altering the small load-bearing data.
rationale_b: Context editing (clear) deterministically removes specific stale, fully-consumed tool-result content while preserving everything else verbatim, which is exactly right when the bloat is recoverable raw data the agent no longer reads but the derived findings/citations must survive exactly. Unlike compaction it does not summarize, so no exact string is paraphrased away.
choice_c: Use context editing to clear the bulky consumed `fetch_source` tool-result blocks once their findings are extracted, but to save even more, also strip out each step's originating assistant tool_use request block while leaving that step's tool_result in place, since the request itself is just the call arguments and the extracted finding already captures what mattered.
rationale_c: This correctly identifies that the consumed raw tool-result content is the real source of bloat and that clearing it is the right move — but it adds one objectively wrong step. Every tool_result is bound to its originating assistant tool_use block by a shared tool_use_id; removing the tool_use request while leaving the tool_result that references it orphans that id and produces an invalid message structure (a 400-class error), because a tool_result must always have a matching tool_use in the turn. Clear the bulky result content, but never delete a tool_use while a tool_result still references it.
choice_d: Do nothing to the history and instead rely on prompt caching with a cache_control breakpoint after the system prompt, since cached tokens read at roughly 0.1x cost will keep the long history affordable as it grows.
rationale_d: Prompt caching reduces the cost of re-reading tokens but does not shrink the context window or stop it from filling, and appended per-step content sits after the breakpoint anyway, so it does not solve the window-pressure problem at all.
guide_section: Appendix — Out of scope for the exam (drill-only)

stem: A research firm builds a multi-agent system on Claude Code to compile competitive-intelligence dossiers. A coordinator agent receives a request like "profile the 9 vendors in the 2026 observability market" and fans out to per-vendor subagents, each of which reads scraped data and writes a `vendors/<name>.md` file to a shared working directory, then returns a short summary. The team observes two problems: (1) the coordinator's final synthesis sometimes omits nuances that a subagent clearly worked through but that didn't fit in the few-line summary it returned, and (2) when a subagent discovers a vendor is actually two merged companies, it tries to spin up its own child subagents to profile each — and the coordinator loses track of how many threads exist. The lead architect wants a design that fixes both issues while respecting how Claude Code subagents actually share state. Which approach is correct?
anti_pattern: Treating isolated subagent contexts as if they share working memory: assuming the coordinator can "see" everything a subagent reasoned about, or that subagents can pass findings to each other implicitly, when in fact each thread has its own isolated context and the only durable shared channels are the filesystem and the concise summary each subagent returns. Related trap: allowing subagents to recursively delegate (multi-level fan-out), which makes the tree unbounded and the coordinator's view incomplete.
choice_a: Merge everything into one long-running agent that profiles all 9 vendors sequentially in a single context, eliminating subagents entirely so there is no context isolation to manage and nothing can be lost between threads.
rationale_a: Collapsing to one context discards the core benefit of subagents — isolating verbose per-vendor discovery so it doesn't consume the coordinator's context window — and will exhaust or degrade the single context as the 9 vendors' raw data accumulates, which is the very problem the multi-agent design existed to solve.
choice_b: Keep delegation flat at one level and have the coordinator synthesize purely from the structured summary each subagent returns. Since you control the summary format, just require each subagent to emit a richer, fully structured summary that captures every nuance — then the coordinator never needs to open the `vendors/<name>.md` files, because the returned summary is a complete representation of the subagent's work.
rationale_b: The flat-delegation decision is correct, and tightening the summary schema genuinely helps. The single objective flaw is treating the returned summary as a complete representation that lets the coordinator skip the files. A summary returned across the thread boundary is inherently a lossy compression of the subagent's isolated context — no schema makes it lossless — so the filesystem remains the authoritative channel and the coordinator must still read the files for detail, or the lost-nuance problem returns.
choice_c: Keep delegation to a single level: the coordinator spawns the per-vendor subagents directly and does not let subagents spawn their own children. Each subagent's reasoning lives in its own isolated context, so it must persist its full findings to the shared filesystem (`vendors/<name>.md`) and return a concise summary; the coordinator synthesizes from the returned summaries and reads the underlying files directly wherever it needs the detail the summary dropped. If a vendor needs to be split, the subagent records that fact in its file and summary, and the coordinator decides whether to launch additional first-level subagents.
rationale_c: Claude Code subagents have isolated per-thread contexts: the coordinator cannot see a subagent's internal reasoning, and the only things that cross the thread boundary are files the subagent writes and the concise summary it returns. Because that summary is deliberately compact, the authoritative, complete channel is the filesystem — so the coordinator must read the `vendors/<name>.md` files when it needs detail the summary omitted. Keeping delegation flat (one level) keeps the thread count and the coordinator's view bounded, fixing the runaway-threads problem; routing detail through files fixes the lost-nuance problem.
choice_d: Let subagents recursively spawn their own child subagents whenever a vendor turns out to be more complex (e.g., a merged company), and give every agent at every level write access to a single shared `state.json` that each one overwrites with its current findings so the whole tree stays in sync.
rationale_d: Claude Code subagents can spawn nested subagents (the platform bounds nesting to a fixed depth), so deeper delegation is possible — but making it the default here reintroduces the runaway thread-count problem the coordinator already hit, and a single overwritten shared-state file means concurrent subagents clobber each other's findings instead of coordinating safely through per-vendor files. It worsens both reported problems rather than fixing them.
guide_section: D1 §1.2 — Orchestrate multi-agent systems with coordinator-subagent patterns

stem: You are architecting a multi-agent research system on the Messages API. A lead agent fans out to four subagents, each of which calls a shared `fetch_source(url)` tool to pull and parse documents. In one run the lead agent emits a single assistant message containing four parallel `tool_use` blocks; three sources return cleanly, but the fourth call to `fetch_source` raises a 504 from the upstream archive service after the parser's timeout. Your harness code catches the exception around that one call. A teammate proposes that, since the other three succeeded, the cleanest behavior is to return only the three successful `tool_result` blocks in the next user message and let the agent proceed with what it has. You need to decide how the harness should hand the failed fourth call back to the model so the loop stays correct and the agent can recover. Which approach is correct?
anti_pattern: When a tool invocation fails, the harness must still return a tool_result block keyed to the matching tool_use_id with is_error set to true, so the model can see the failure and adapt. Dropping the failed result entirely (leaving an unanswered tool_use), or swallowing it and substituting plausible fabricated content, breaks the loop contract: the model either stalls waiting on a missing tool_result or hallucinates over an error it never saw. A related trap is returning a result with is_error true but a generic "Operation failed" string that gives the model nothing actionable to recover from.
choice_a: Return all four `tool_result` blocks in a single user message; for the failed fourth call, set `is_error: true` on its `tool_result` (keyed to that call's `tool_use_id`) and include the upstream 504 / timeout detail in the content so the agent can see the failure and decide to retry, try another source, or report the gap.
rationale_a: Every `tool_use` block must receive a matching `tool_result` keyed by `tool_use_id`, and all results for one assistant message belong in one user message; a failed tool must come back with `is_error: true` and useful context rather than being dropped, so the model can adapt instead of stalling or hallucinating.
choice_b: Return all four `tool_result` blocks together in the next user message, each keyed to its `tool_use_id`, and for the failed fourth call set `is_error: true` with the content string "Operation failed" so the agent knows that call did not succeed.
rationale_b: The structure is right — all four results in one message, correct `tool_use_id` keying, and `is_error: true` on the failure — but the objective flaw is the opaque "Operation failed" content: a failed tool_result should carry actionable detail (status, error category, retryability) so the model can recover, not a generic message that leaves it nothing to act on.
choice_c: Have the harness retry the failed call silently, and if it still fails, substitute a `tool_result` with `is_error: false` containing a placeholder summary like "source unavailable, content omitted" so the response stays uniformly successful and the agent isn't distracted by an error state.
rationale_c: Marking a genuine failure as `is_error: false` with fabricated placeholder content disguises an access failure as a real (empty) result, so the model treats missing data as valid and may hallucinate over it — the empty-as-success anti-pattern.
choice_d: Drop the failed fourth `tool_result` entirely and return only the three successful results, since the lead agent still has enough successful sources to make progress and an absent result naturally signals that source was unavailable.
rationale_d: This violates the loop contract: every `tool_use` block requires a matching `tool_result`, so omitting the fourth leaves an unanswered tool call that stalls or errors the turn rather than letting the agent recover.
guide_section: D2 §2.2 — Implement structured error responses for MCP tools

stem: At a market-intelligence firm, an architect is designing a research system on the Claude managed-agents platform. A "Lead Analyst" capability must run for hours across roughly 40 turns per investigation, accumulating findings as it goes, and it authenticates to an internal MCP server (a licensed filings database) that requires OAuth credentials. Separately, each investigation fans out into about a dozen short, independent "Section Researcher" tasks — each pulls one data slice, summarizes it, and returns; these are stateless, throwaway, and benefit from running in isolated contexts so their verbose intermediate retrieval does not pollute the Lead Analyst's window. The team also worries about cost and operations: they run ~300 investigations a week, want prompt caching to stay efficient, and want a single place to rotate the filings-DB credential without touching the agent definition. Which approach correctly maps these two workload shapes onto the platform's primitives?
anti_pattern: Conflating a long-lived agent that retains its own context across many turns with a fresh spawn-and-block subagent created per task; in particular, calling agents.create on every dispatch instead of creating the agent once and referencing it by ID from many sessions, and misplacing MCP auth on the agent definition rather than in vaults.
choice_a: Run both the multi-hour Lead Analyst work and all dozen Section Researcher tasks inside one shared long-lived Session so that the section researchers' raw retrieval is always visible to the lead and nothing needs summarizing between them.
rationale_a: Pouring verbose stateless fan-out retrieval into the lead's shared session defeats context isolation, balloons the window, and churns the cacheable prefix the team is trying to keep efficient. Isolated subagents should absorb that verbose discovery and return concise summaries, preserving the lead's context window.
choice_b: Call agents.create at the start of every one of the ~300 weekly investigations to provision a fresh Lead Analyst, and also call agents.create for each of the dozen Section Researcher tasks, so every unit of work gets a cleanly provisioned agent with no carried-over state.
rationale_b: This contradicts the create-once, reference-by-ID model: the agent (model/system/tools) is defined a single time and reused, while per-investigation state belongs in sessions, not in newly provisioned agents. Re-creating agents per run is wasteful, complicates lifecycle management, and is unnecessary — a fresh Session already gives clean per-task state without a new agent.
choice_c: Define the Lead Analyst agent once and drive each investigation through a long-lived Session that retains context, run the dozen Section Researcher tasks as isolated spawn-and-block subagents, but place the filings-DB OAuth credentials directly in the Lead Analyst agent definition so the configuration is self-contained.
rationale_c: Every structural decision here is correct — create-once agent, long-lived retaining session, isolated spawn-and-block subagents — which is what makes it tempting. It is wrong only on one objective, checkable detail: MCP auth must live in a vault attached via vault_ids, never in the agent definition. Embedding the OAuth credential in the agent definition contradicts that rule and also couples credential rotation to redefining the agent, defeating the team's stated goal of a single rotation point.
choice_d: Create the Lead Analyst as a single managed Agent once (model, system prompt, and tools defined on it), attach the filings-DB MCP auth via a vault referenced by vault_ids, and drive each multi-hour investigation through its own long-lived Session that retains context across the ~40 turns; implement the dozen Section Researcher tasks as fresh spawn-and-block subagents in isolated contexts that return concise summaries to the Lead Analyst.
rationale_d: A managed agent is created once and referenced by ID from many sessions, with the long-lived session (not a new agent) holding the per-investigation state that accumulates across turns. MCP auth lives in vaults attached via vault_ids, not in the agent definition — which also gives the team the single rotation point they want, since rotating the vault credential never requires editing the agent. Stateless fan-out work is best handled by isolated spawn-and-block subagents that absorb verbose retrieval and return concise summaries, preserving the lead's context window (and keeping its cacheable prefix stable).
guide_section: D1 §1.2 — Orchestrate multi-agent systems with coordinator-subagent patterns

stem: Meridian Analytics runs an overnight "competitive-landscape" research agent built on the Managed Agents API: a lead large-tier agent fans out to four mid-tier subagents, each researching one competitor, and the lead synthesizes a brief that an analyst reads at 8 a.m. Over the last 30 nightly runs the agent produced 30 briefs and never errored, yet analysts rejected 11 of them for missing a required pricing-changes section or citing stale sources. The current harness runs the lead agent's loop until the model emits a closing paragraph containing the phrase "Research complete and verified," then stops and ships the brief unattended. The architecture lead must redefine what "done" means for this autonomous, unattended workload so that low-quality briefs stop shipping. Which approach is the best fit for the platform's guidance on autonomous agents?
anti_pattern: Treating an autonomous agent as "done" based on the model's own natural-language self-report (it says it is "finished" / "confident") or on a fixed iteration budget, rather than on an explicitly defined Outcome scored against a gradeable rubric in a separate evaluation step.
choice_a: Stop relying on the closing phrase and instead cap the lead agent's loop at a fixed number of iterations (e.g., 12), treating reaching the cap as "done" and shipping whatever brief exists at that point.
rationale_a: A fixed iteration cap is only a safety backstop, not a definition of correct termination or quality; shipping whatever exists at the cap addresses neither the missing-section nor the stale-source failures and embodies the iteration-cap anti-pattern.
choice_b: Keep shipping unattended, but redefine "done" as a gradeable rubric (required sections present, all claims cited, sources within the run window) and have the lead agent score its own finished brief against that rubric in the same session, shipping only when it passes and re-running otherwise.
rationale_b: It correctly adopts an Outcome-plus-gradeable-rubric definition of done, but the objective flaw is that the rubric check runs as same-session self-review by the agent that wrote the brief; platform guidance is that verification in a separate session/context beats same-session self-review because it avoids the author's reasoning bias, so the grading step must be isolated from the producing context.
choice_c: Have the lead agent append a self-reported confidence score to each brief and ship automatically when confidence is at or above 0.9, routing only lower-confidence briefs to an analyst.
rationale_c: Platform guidance says to escalate on task complexity or an explicit policy gap, not on a self-reported confidence score, which is unreliable and would still ship the same defective briefs the model already believes are complete.
choice_d: Define "done" as an explicit Outcome with a gradeable rubric — e.g., required sections present (including pricing-changes), every claim cited, and no source older than the run window — and have a separate verification step score each brief against that rubric before it ships, escalating any brief that fails to an analyst.
rationale_d: The platform's managed-agents guidance says to define "done" for autonomous work via an Outcome plus a gradeable rubric, and that verification in a separate context beats same-session self-review; scoring the brief against objective criteria before shipping is exactly what catches the missing-section and stale-source failures the self-report misses.
guide_section: D1 §1.3 — Configure subagent invocation, context passing, and spawning

stem: You are architecting a multi-agent research assistant in Claude Code for the Heliotrope legal-discovery team. The lead/orchestrator agent (running the large, most capable model tier) coordinates document review across a 4,200-file corpus. Most steps are genuinely open-ended — "find every contract that mentions an indemnification carve-out" can touch hundreds of files and produce thousands of lines of intermediate grep output — and for those you already delegate to Explore subagents so the verbose discovery stays out of the lead's 1M-token context. A new step has been added: at the start of each run, the lead must read a single known config file, `review_policy.yaml` (about 60 lines), whose exact path is fixed and whose contents it needs verbatim to set thresholds. A teammate proposes wrapping this read in its own dedicated general-purpose subagent too, arguing "every step should be a subagent so the architecture is uniform and the lead's context stays pristine." Reviewers are split. Which approach best reflects the platform's stated guidance on when to use subagents?
anti_pattern: Reflexively spawning a fresh subagent for every step — including a trivial, bounded single-file read — on the belief that "more agents = more capable," when subagents exist to isolate VERBOSE/open-ended discovery from the main context. A cheap, deterministic, low-token operation gains nothing from isolation and pays the full overhead of a separate context (its own system prompt, tool set, and a round-trip summary), so the orchestrator should just do it inline.
choice_a: Have the lead agent read `review_policy.yaml` directly (inline) rather than spawning a subagent. Subagents exist to isolate VERBOSE or open-ended discovery from the orchestrator's context and return a concise summary; a fixed-path, ~60-line read is small, deterministic, and bounded, so isolation buys nothing and a separate context only adds overhead (its own prompt/tool setup plus a summarization round-trip). Keep delegating the genuinely large grep/scan steps to Explore subagents.
rationale_a: Public Claude Code guidance frames subagents as a tool to isolate verbose discovery in a separate context and return a concise summary, preserving the main context window; that benefit only materializes when the work is large or open-ended, so a small bounded read should be done inline and only the heavy scans delegated.
choice_b: Skip the dedicated subagent but have the lead reactively run `/compact` immediately after reading the file, so any context the 60-line read consumes is reclaimed before the heavy grep steps begin.
rationale_b: `/compact` is reactive and lossy and is explicitly not a substitute for proactively isolating verbose work; invoking it to reclaim a trivial 60-line read is both unnecessary (the read is tiny) and risks discarding the verbatim thresholds the step depends on.
choice_c: Spawn a dedicated general-purpose subagent for the read so the architecture stays uniform — every step is a subagent — which maximizes the lead's capability and guarantees the 60 lines never touch the orchestrator's context.
rationale_c: This embodies the overeager-spawning anti-pattern: uniformity is not a design goal, and isolating a tiny deterministic read provides no context savings while adding a full extra context plus a summarization round-trip; subagents are justified by verbose/open-ended work, not by a rule that "every step is a subagent."
choice_d: Read `review_policy.yaml` inline rather than via a subagent, because the file is small and the path is fixed — and additionally promote the read to a dedicated single-purpose tool so its summarized result, not the raw 60 lines, is what enters the lead's context and keeps the prefix cache-clean.
rationale_d: The lead decision (read inline, not as a subagent) is correct and well-justified, but the stated reason for promoting it to a dedicated tool is wrong: per the platform's agent-design guidance you promote an action to a dedicated tool to GATE hard-to-reverse actions, enforce a staleness check, render custom UI, or mark it parallel-safe — not to summarize away a cheap read. The config must be used verbatim to set thresholds, so summarizing it would discard the exact data the step needs, and a one-time fixed read does not benefit from tool promotion.
guide_section: D1 §1.3 — Configure subagent invocation, context passing, and spawning

stem: You are architecting a multi-agent research orchestrator on the Messages API. A "lead" agent fans work out to specialist subagents; one specialist, the Citation Verifier, is invoked on the very first turn of its session and must ALWAYS begin by calling the tool `fetch_source(url)` exactly once to pull the primary document before doing anything else — never answering from memory and never calling two tools in the same response.
