The Verifier's tool set contains four tools: `fetch_source`, `extract_quote`, `check_archive`, and `flag_discrepancy`. During testing you observe that under your current config the Verifier sometimes responds with prose instead of calling `fetch_source`, and occasionally emits both `fetch_source` and `check_archive` as parallel tool_use blocks in its first response. You need the first turn to deterministically produce a single `fetch_source` call. Which configuration for that first request is correct?
anti_pattern: Conflating tool_choice (which controls WHETHER/WHICH tool is called) with disable_parallel_tool_use (which controls HOW MANY tools per turn), and assuming forcing a tool also pins WHICH tool — when in fact {type:any} forces some tool but not a specific one, {type:tool,name:X} forces exactly that one named tool (inherently a single call), and disable_parallel_tool_use only matters when you want at-most-one tool under auto/any.
choice_a: Set tool_choice to {type:"any"} on the first request together with disable_parallel_tool_use:true. {type:"any"} guarantees the model must call some tool rather than answering in prose, and disable_parallel_tool_use:true caps it at one tool per response, so the first turn produces a single tool call.
rationale_a: This correctly eliminates two of the three failure modes — {type:"any"} forbids a prose/end_turn answer and disable_parallel_tool_use:true forbids parallel tool_use — but its single objective flaw is that {type:"any"} forces a tool call without pinning WHICH tool. The lone first call could be extract_quote or check_archive instead of fetch_source. Only {type:"tool",name:"fetch_source"} guarantees the specific tool.
choice_b: Set tool_choice to {type:"tool", name:"fetch_source"} on the first request. This forces the model to emit exactly that one tool call, so it cannot answer with prose and cannot also emit check_archive in the same response; on subsequent turns switch tool_choice back to {type:"auto"} so the Verifier can choose freely among all four tools.
rationale_b: tool_choice:{type:tool,name:X} compels the model to call exactly the named tool. Because it pins one specific tool, the response is inherently a single forced tool_use block for fetch_source — you do not even need disable_parallel_tool_use here. This satisfies "always fetch_source, exactly once, no parallel tool" for the first turn while leaving later turns under auto for free selection among all four tools.
choice_c: Set tool_choice to {type:"none"} on the first request so the model is forced to route through fetch_source, then parse its text to confirm the fetch happened before continuing.
rationale_c: {type:"none"} does the opposite of what is needed: it forbids the model from calling any tool, so fetch_source can never fire on that turn; it is used to suppress tool use, not to force a specific tool.
choice_d: Keep tool_choice at {type:"auto"} and instead add a strong system instruction ("On your first turn you MUST call fetch_source and only fetch_source"). Auto plus an emphatic instruction is sufficient because the model reliably follows clear directives, and it keeps the config simple.
rationale_d: A system instruction under {type:"auto"} is advisory — the model may still answer in prose or call a different/second tool, which is exactly the nondeterminism observed; auto leaves the decision to the model rather than enforcing it, so it cannot deterministically guarantee a single fetch_source call.
guide_section: D2 §2.3 — Distribute tools appropriately across agents and configure tool choice

stem: You are architecting a multi-agent research assistant at Meridian Legal Analytics. An orchestrator dispatches subagents to query three internal corpora through a `search_corpus` tool that wraps separate vendor APIs. During a pilot, a subagent investigating "indemnification clause precedents" called `search_corpus(corpus="caselaw_v2")`, received a 200 response whose body was `{"results": []}`, and the orchestrator's final report stated with high confidence: "No indemnification precedents exist in the case-law corpus." A partner later confirmed the corpus contains hundreds of such precedents; the pilot's API key had simply not been granted scope on `caselaw_v2`, and that vendor returns an empty results array (not an HTTP error) for unauthorized scopes. Leadership wants the tool and agent loop redesigned so a finding of "nothing exists" is trustworthy. Which redesign best addresses the root cause per the platform's agent-design guidance?
anti_pattern: Treating a tool call that returned zero items as authoritative confirmation that no matching data exists, when the zero could instead be an access/permission/scope failure. The agent then reports a confident "nothing found" conclusion built on an unverified negative, hiding the failure inside a normal-looking empty payload.
choice_a: Raise the orchestrator's effort to "max" and add a system instruction telling it to be skeptical of empty results, so the model reasons more carefully about whether an empty array truly means no data exists before writing the final report.
rationale_a: Higher effort and an advisory system instruction add cost and remain skippable guidance; they cannot make the model see authorization state that the tool never reported, so the unverified-negative root cause in the tool boundary is untouched.
choice_b: Change `search_corpus` so its tool result explicitly distinguishes a genuinely-empty result from an access failure: have the wrapper verify scope/authorization for the requested corpus and, when the empty array stems from a missing scope, return a structured tool_result with is_error=true carrying an errorCategory like "access_denied" and isRetryable metadata — reserving the empty-but-successful payload only for a confirmed authorized query that legitimately matched nothing.
rationale_b: The platform's agent-design guidance states an agent must distinguish a genuinely-empty result from an access failure, and that tool errors should surface structured isError/errorCategory/isRetryable context rather than being silently swallowed; encoding the authorization failure into the tool result is what lets the agent stop treating an unauthorized empty array as a confirmed negative.
choice_c: Add an iteration cap so that whenever any corpus returns zero results the orchestrator automatically re-dispatches the query two more times, and only accepts "nothing found" if all three attempts return empty.
rationale_c: Retrying a deterministically unauthorized query just reproduces the same empty array three times, and an iteration cap is a safety backstop rather than a correctness mechanism — it still treats the empty payload as the signal instead of distinguishing access failure from a true empty result.
choice_d: Have `search_corpus` detect the unauthorized case and, when scope is missing, return a normal (non-error) tool_result whose results array is empty but with an added `"note": "access may have been denied"` string field, so the agent can read the note and reconsider before concluding nothing exists.
rationale_d: The objective flaw is returning the access failure as a successful (non-error) tool_result instead of setting is_error=true with a structured errorCategory; a failed tool call must be returned as an error result and never disguised as a normal empty payload, so a free-text note inside a success response still conflates the two cases the design must separate.
guide_section: D2 §2.2 — Implement structured error responses for MCP tools

stem: You are architecting a document-extraction agent at a fictional firm, Marlowe Title & Escrow, that pulls structured fields (parcel ID, grantor, grantee, recording date) out of property deeds. The deeds and a custom retrieval service live behind a remote MCP server named "deed-vault" exposed over HTTPS, and your team wants a large-tier Claude model to call that server's search and fetch tools directly through the Messages API's MCP connector (not via a separate self-hosted MCP client). A junior engineer adds `mcp_servers=[{type:"url", url:"https://mcp.marlowe.example/deed-vault", name:"deed-vault"}]` to the request, runs it, and gets a 400 error before any extraction happens. The pipeline must process roughly 4,000 deeds per night, so you need the wiring correct on the first call rather than discovered per-batch. What is the correct way to wire this request so Claude can actually invoke the deed-vault tools?
anti_pattern: Treating the MCP connector as configured once the remote server is declared, while omitting the matching mcp_toolset tool entry (or mismatching its name) — so Claude can "see" the server connection but has no tool surface wired into the request, which the Messages API rejects as a 400. The trap rewards partial knowledge of one of the two required pieces.
choice_a: Add the toolset and reference the server by its exact name — `tools=[{type:"mcp_toolset", mcp_server_name:"deed-vault"}]` — but trim the `mcp_servers` entry to `mcp_servers=[{url:"https://mcp.marlowe.example/deed-vault", name:"deed-vault"}]`, dropping the `type` field as redundant since the URL already implies a remote server.
rationale_a: This gets the two-part shape right — a server declaration plus a matching mcp_toolset whose `mcp_server_name` equals the server `name` — so it looks complete. The single objective flaw is dropping `type:"url"` from the `mcp_servers` entry: that discriminator is a required field on the server object, not redundant decoration, and omitting it leaves the entry malformed, so the request still 400s before the (correctly named) toolset can take effect.
choice_b: Keep only the `mcp_servers` entry as written, but add `output_config` with `effort:"max"` so the model invests enough reasoning to discover and connect to the server on its own.
rationale_b: Effort controls how much thinking/tool-calling the model does, not whether the MCP tools are wired into the request; with no `mcp_toolset` the 400 persists regardless of effort, since the server-only declaration is rejected at request validation, before any reasoning occurs.
choice_c: Remove the `mcp_servers` field entirely and instead list each deed-vault tool individually under `tools` with a normal `input_schema`, since the MCP connector is just shorthand for hand-declared custom tools.
rationale_c: This abandons the MCP connector mechanism: the connector exists precisely so you do not hand-author each remote tool's schema, and removing `mcp_servers` means there is no managed connection to the remote server at all, so Claude cannot reach deed-vault. Hand-declared custom tools have no transport to the remote service.
choice_d: Keep the `mcp_servers` entry and ALSO add a matching toolset to `tools`: `tools=[{type:"mcp_toolset", mcp_server_name:"deed-vault"}]`, where `mcp_server_name` exactly equals the server's `name`. The MCP connector requires both the server declaration and a matching mcp_toolset referencing it by name; declaring the server alone is a 400.
rationale_d: The Messages API MCP connector is two-part: `mcp_servers` declares the connection and a sibling `tools` entry of type `mcp_toolset` whose `mcp_server_name` matches the server `name` actually exposes those tools to the model. Declaring the server without the matching toolset is documented to return a 400 — which is exactly the error the engineer hit, because no tool surface was wired into the request.
guide_section: Appendix — Out of scope for the exam (drill-only)

stem: At Northwind Returns, a customer-support agent built on Claude triages refund tickets. Its current design routes everything through one general-purpose bash tool: the agent greps the order DB, computes the refund amount, and then runs `python send_refund_email.py --to <customer> --amount <n>` to dispatch an irreversible refund-confirmation email through the billing provider. Over 4,200 tickets last month, three incidents occurred where the agent emailed a refund confirmation for the wrong amount, and post-mortems showed the harness had no way to require human confirmation before the send because the dispatch was buried inside an opaque bash invocation. The architecture team wants the send step to become something the harness can reliably gate (require approval), mark as not parallel-safe, and surface structured errors for — while keeping cheap, high-volume read operations (DB lookups, amount math) fast and unceremonious. Which change best fits the platform's published agent-design guidance?
anti_pattern: Treating a hard-to-reverse, externally-visible action (sending a refund email to a customer) as just another opaque shell command, so the harness has no seam to gate, confirm, or render it — and conflating "give it a dedicated tool" with the separate, optional choices of permission gating, staleness checks, and structured error surfaces.
choice_a: Split the agent into many fine-grained tools — one each for `lookup_order`, `compute_refund`, `validate_customer`, `format_email`, `dispatch_email`, `log_refund`, and a dozen similar steps — so every operation including the send is individually visible and controllable by the harness.
rationale_a: Decomposing the cheap read/compute steps into a dozen-plus tools pushes the agent toward the ~18+ tools range that degrades tool selection; guidance favors ~4–5 focused tools and promoting only the action that genuinely needs gating, not exploding every step into its own tool.
choice_b: Promote the refund send to its own dedicated tool (e.g. `send_refund_email`) with an explicit input schema, leaving the read/compute steps in bash; because it is a discrete, hard-to-reverse, externally-visible action, the dedicated tool gives the harness a seam to gate it behind approval, mark it not parallel-safe, and return structured errors — none of which is possible while it is hidden inside an opaque bash call.
rationale_b: Public guidance says to promote an action from bash to a dedicated tool precisely when you must gate hard-to-reverse actions, mark it parallel-safe (or not), or render/handle it specially; bash gives breadth but is opaque to the harness, so it cannot gate or specially handle a step buried inside it.
choice_c: Keep the send inside the general-purpose bash tool, but add a CLAUDE.md instruction telling the agent to always ask the user for confirmation before running the refund-email command, since CLAUDE.md persists project conventions across every turn.
rationale_c: A CLAUDE.md instruction is advisory and can be skipped by the model, so it cannot reliably gate a hard-to-reverse action; deterministic enforcement requires a harness seam (a dedicated tool the harness can gate, or a hook), not a prompt convention.
choice_d: Promote the refund send to a dedicated `send_refund_email` tool and have its handler, on any billing-provider failure, return a tool_result whose text is "Operation failed" so the agent can retry, keeping the read/compute steps in bash.
rationale_d: Promoting the irreversible send to a dedicated gated tool is correct, but the failure handler is wrong: public guidance says tool errors should be a structured payload (isError / errorCategory / isRetryable plus context), not a generic "Operation failed" string, which is the generic-error anti-pattern the scenario explicitly wants to avoid.
guide_section: D2 §2.1 — Design effective tool interfaces with clear descriptions and boundaries

stem: At Meridian Filings, an extraction agent ingests roughly 4,200 regulatory PDFs per night. For each one it reads the existing JSON record from a shared records/ tree, merges newly parsed fields, and writes the record back. Analysts edit the same records/ tree by hand during the evening, so a record can change on disk between the moment the agent reads it and the moment it writes — and an overwrite silently destroys an analyst's edits. The team wants the agent to NEVER overwrite a record that changed since it was last read by this agent, and they want that guarantee to hold even if a future prompt revision forgets to mention it. Which design enforces the staleness guarantee most reliably?
anti_pattern: Trying to enforce a hard, hard-to-reverse safety condition (don't overwrite a file modified since you last read it) through advisory prompt text or an opaque shell command, instead of making it a structural property of a dedicated, harness-visible tool that can deterministically reject the call before any write occurs.
choice_a: Keep the write in a bash command and run the whole extraction in a separate verification session that re-checks each record after the nightly run and reverts any record the agent overwrote that an analyst had edited.
rationale_a: Verification in a separate session is a documented platform pattern, but it is a detective/after-the-fact control: the overwrite still happens and the analyst's edit is destroyed before the second session can notice and revert it. The requirement is to NEVER overwrite a changed record, which demands a preventive gate at write time, not post-hoc reconciliation.
choice_b: Promote the write to a dedicated edit_record tool whose input includes the path and the content-hash (or mtime) the agent observed at read time; inside the tool, re-read the file, compare against the supplied hash, and if they differ return a tool_result with is_error=true (errorCategory like 'stale_read', isRetryable=true) so the write is rejected before it happens and the agent can re-read and retry.
rationale_b: The platform's agent-design guidance says to promote an action to a dedicated tool precisely when you must GATE it — hard-to-reverse actions and staleness checks are the canonical examples — because the harness can enforce the precondition deterministically. The tool owns the check (re-read + hash compare) and the contract is structural: every call goes through it regardless of what the prompt says, and the failure is surfaced as a structured is_error result the agent can act on.
choice_c: Promote the write to a dedicated edit_record tool that, on each call, re-reads the file, compares it against the content the agent last read, and when they differ SKIPS the write and returns a normal (non-error) tool_result whose text reads 'write skipped: file changed since last read.'
rationale_c: This correctly promotes the action to a dedicated, harness-visible tool that owns the staleness check — structurally the right move — but it reports the rejected write as a SUCCESS (a non-error tool_result). Public guidance is that a failed or rejected tool call must return is_error=true with a structured category, never a normal result; signaling skip-as-success means the agent's loop sees a successful write, won't reliably re-read and retry, and the stale records are silently left un-updated. One objective flaw: wrong tool_result status for a rejected action.
choice_d: Keep the write in a bash command and add a strongly-worded line to the system prompt instructing the agent to re-read each file and abort the write if it changed since the last read.
rationale_d: A system-prompt instruction is advisory — the model can skip it, and bash is opaque to the harness, so nothing structurally prevents the overwrite. Public guidance is explicit that prompt text is advisory while deterministic enforcement (a gating tool or a hook) is what guarantees a critical rule; this fails the team's 'must hold even if a prompt revision forgets it' requirement.
guide_section: D2 §2.1 — Design effective tool interfaces with clear descriptions and boundaries

stem: You maintain an MCP server, doc-vault, that exposes a `search_contracts` tool to a Claude agent running on the large, most capable model tier. The tool runs a full-text query against a contracts index and returns matching contract sections. Two distinct conditions keep getting confused downstream. Condition A: the query is well-formed and the index is reachable, but zero contracts match (a real, valid empty result). Condition B: the underlying search backend is unreachable because an upstream auth token expired, so the query could not run at all. Today the tool handles both the same way: it returns the JSON string `{"result": "Operation failed"}` with `isError: true`. As a result, the agent treats 'no contracts exist for this client' as a failure and retries pointlessly, while genuine auth outages get silently lumped in with empty searches and never surface for remediation. You are redesigning the tool's return contract to follow the platform's guidance on tool/MCP error design. Which redesign is the best?
anti_pattern: Collapsing a genuinely-empty-but-valid result into an error, and returning a bare generic failure string instead of a structured, machine-readable tool error.
choice_a: On Condition A, return a normal (non-error) success result whose payload is an explicitly empty match list (e.g. `{"matches": []}`), so the agent can distinguish 'genuinely empty' from 'failed'. On Condition B, return a structured tool error that sets `isError: true` and includes machine-readable fields such as an error category (e.g. `errorCategory: "auth"`), an `isRetryable` flag, and human-readable context naming the expired upstream token.
rationale_a: The platform's tool-design guidance says to distinguish a genuinely-empty result from an access failure, and to return structured errors (isError plus fields like errorCategory and isRetryable, with context) rather than a generic 'Operation failed'. A valid zero-match search is a successful result and must NOT be flagged isError; an auth outage is a real failure and should carry a non-retryable-without-fix, categorized signal the agent and operators can act on.
choice_b: On Condition A, return a normal success result with `{"matches": []}`. On Condition B, return `isError: true` with a structured error that includes `errorCategory: "auth"` and human-readable context naming the expired token, and set `isRetryable: true` so the agent automatically retries until the backend recovers.
rationale_b: This correctly separates empty-from-failure and returns a structured, categorized error with context — the hard part of the guidance. Its single objective flaw is `isRetryable: true` for an expired-auth-token outage: that failure cannot be resolved by retrying the same call (the token must be refreshed out of band), so retryable should be false. Mislabeling a fix-required failure as retryable reproduces the pointless-retry loop the redesign is meant to eliminate.
choice_c: Keep returning `isError: true` for both conditions, but replace the message string: use `{"result": "No contracts found"}` for Condition A and `{"result": "Backend unreachable"}` for Condition B, so the agent can tell them apart by reading the message text.
rationale_c: This still flags a valid empty search as an error (violating distinguish-empty-from-failure), and it forces the agent to raw-string-match free-text messages instead of reading machine-readable fields. The platform's guidance is to return structured errors with categories/flags, not to encode meaning in a human prose string the consumer must parse.
choice_d: Leave the tool returning `isError: true` for both, and instead add a system-prompt instruction telling the agent: 'If a search tool reports a failure, assume it just means no results were found and do not retry.'
rationale_d: A prompt instruction is advisory and unreliable, and here it is also wrong: it would make the agent treat genuine auth outages (Condition B) as empty results, hiding real failures that need remediation. The fix belongs in the tool's structured return contract, not in a prompt that papers over conflated states.
guide_section: D2 §2.2 — Implement structured error responses for MCP tools

stem: You are designing a multi-agent research assistant for a fictional firm, Brightmoor Analytics. A lead agent on the large, most capable model tier orchestrates two retrieval tools that its subagents share: `search_internal_memos` (queries ~48,000 indexed company memos) and `search_public_filings` (queries an external EDGAR-style corpus of regulatory filings). In testing, the agent frequently calls `search_public_filings` for questions that should hit internal memos and vice-versa, and sometimes calls both redundantly in a single turn before answering. Both tool descriptions currently read like "Searches the [internal memo / public filings] corpus and returns the top matching documents." The orchestration prompt already explains, at length, the difference between the two corpora and when each should be used. An architect proposes a fix focused on the tool definitions themselves. Which change is the most effective and correctly-implemented way to reduce both the wrong-tool and the redundant-call behavior?
anti_pattern: Writing a tool description that only restates the tool's mechanical action ("what it does") and relying on the system prompt or the model's judgment to decide when to invoke it, instead of encoding the selection criteria — when to use it and when NOT to — into the tool description itself, where the model actually reads them at selection time. A related trap is treating tool overlap as something to fix by adding more enforcement (tool_choice, more tools) rather than by disambiguating the descriptions.
choice_a: Keep the descriptions as purely "what it does" text, but set `tool_choice` to `{type: "any"}` on the orchestration call so the model is forced to pick exactly one of the two retrieval tools, eliminating the redundant double-calls.
rationale_a: `tool_choice:{type:"any"}` only forces the model to use SOME tool on that turn — it does not restrict it to a single tool call, so it does not actually stop the model from emitting two retrieval calls; the redundant-call symptom is suppressed by `disable_parallel_tool_use:true`, not by `any`. The choice's claim that `any` means "exactly one" is the single objective error, and as a bonus the wrong-tool problem is left entirely untouched because the disambiguating criteria still aren't in the descriptions where selection happens.
choice_b: Rewrite each tool's `description` field to state when to call it and when not to — e.g., `search_internal_memos`: "Use for questions about Brightmoor's own decisions, strategy, or internal discussion. Do NOT use for external regulatory data — use search_public_filings for that." — and apply the symmetric guidance to `search_public_filings`, so the selection criteria live where the model reads them at tool-selection time.
rationale_b: A tool's description is the primary signal the model uses to decide whether to call it, so usage criteria ("when to use / when not to use," including a cross-reference to the sibling tool) belong in the description itself, not only in the orchestration prompt. Sharpening the descriptions so each corpus owns a clear scope is the canonical way to disambiguate overlapping tools, which both routes each question to the right tool and removes the ambiguity that drives the model to hedge by calling both.
choice_c: Leave both tool descriptions unchanged and instead expand the orchestration system prompt with even more detailed rules about which corpus maps to which question type, since the system prompt is the authoritative place to encode routing logic.
rationale_c: The orchestration prompt already contains this guidance and the wrong-tool behavior persists, because selection logic placed only in the system prompt competes with everything else there and is not consulted as directly as the description at tool-selection time; the criteria need to be in the tool descriptions the model reads when choosing a tool.
choice_d: Merge the two tools into a single `search_documents` tool with a `corpus` enum parameter, and add a third tool, a `corpus_router`, that the agent must call first to decide which corpus to query.
rationale_d: This adds tools and an extra mandatory hop rather than clarifying selection criteria; a router tool just relocates the same ambiguous "which corpus" decision into another call, and growing the tool count works against keeping a small, well-described tool set.
guide_section: D2 §2.1 — Design effective tool interfaces with clear descriptions and boundaries

stem: You are architecting a document-extraction pipeline at a fictional firm, Brightleaf Records, that processes roughly 40,000 scanned invoices per night. A single tool named `record_invoice` receives the extracted fields (vendor, invoice_number, total_cents, due_date) and writes them to a downstream ledger. Last week the on-call engineer found 312 invoices were rejected by the ledger because Claude occasionally emitted an extra `notes` field and sometimes omitted `due_date`, producing tool inputs that drifted from the agreed schema. You want the API to guarantee every `record_invoice` call conforms exactly to the schema — no extra keys, all four fields present — without adding a post-hoc validation layer. Using a frontier-tier model via the Messages API, where and how should you configure strict schema enforcement?
anti_pattern: Treating strict schema validation as a request-level routing control (placing it on tool_choice) rather than a property of the tool's own schema, and omitting the JSON Schema constraints (additionalProperties:false + required) that strict mode requires to actually validate inputs.
choice_a: Leave the tool definition unchanged and instead set `output_config.format` to a JSON schema matching the four fields, which forces all model output to conform to the contract.
rationale_a: output_config.format constrains the assistant's structured text output, not the inputs to a tool call, so it does not guarantee that `record_invoice` tool inputs conform to the schema.
choice_b: Set `strict: true` as a sibling of `name`/`description`/`input_schema` on the `record_invoice` tool definition, and make the tool's `input_schema` enforce the contract by adding `additionalProperties: false` and listing all four fields in `required`.
rationale_b: strict mode is a property of the tool definition itself (sibling of name/description/input_schema) and only validates as intended when the schema also declares additionalProperties:false and required, which together forbid the extra `notes` key and force `due_date` to be present.
choice_c: Set `tool_choice: {type: "tool", name: "record_invoice", strict: true}` so the forced tool selection also enforces the input schema on every call.
rationale_c: strict is not a field on tool_choice; tool_choice only controls which tool is selected or whether a tool must be used, so placing strict there does not enable schema validation — schema enforcement lives on the tool definition.
choice_d: Add `strict: true` to the `record_invoice` tool definition alongside `name` and `input_schema`, but leave the schema as-is with only the four field types declared, since marking the tool strict is what activates exact-conformance validation.
rationale_d: The placement is correct (strict belongs on the tool definition), but the objective flaw is that strict mode only enforces what the schema itself declares — without additionalProperties:false the extra `notes` key is still allowed, and without listing the fields in required the missing `due_date` is not caught.
guide_section: D4 §4.3 — Enforce structured output using tool use and JSON schemas

stem: Meridian Labs runs a managed-agent research pipeline: a single "LitScout" Agent (created once via agents.create with the configured large-tier model) is referenced by roughly 240 Sessions/day, each fanning out across three remote MCP servers — an internal patents index, a licensed journals API, and a clinical-trials registry — all reached via the Messages API MCP connector. The journals provider rotates its OAuth bearer token every 14 days, and Meridian's security team requires that (a) a credential rotation never force a redeploy of agent logic, (b) secrets never appear in the agent's stored configuration, and (c) the rotation be a single in-place change rather than a fan-out across call sites. A new architect proposes encoding all three MCP servers' tokens directly in the Agent's mcp_servers configuration at agents.create time so every Session inherits them automatically. The team asks you to choose the design that satisfies all three requirements while keeping the agent definition stable and the agent ID unchanged. Which approach is correct?
anti_pattern: Treating the managed Agent object as the place to hold MCP server authentication. Because the agent definition (model/system/tools/mcp_servers/skills) is created once and referenced from every session, baking a credential into it couples a rotating secret to an immutable, shared, permanent-on-archive object — instead of attaching auth through a vault that can be swapped or updated in place without touching the agent.
choice_a: Keep the Agent definition free of credentials; store the three MCP servers' auth in one or more Vaults and attach them to each Session via vault_ids. When the journals token rotates every 14 days, update the secret inside the existing vault in place — the shared Agent definition and its ID are never touched, the vault_ids the Sessions reference stay the same, and no redeploy of agent logic occurs.
rationale_a: For managed agents, MCP auth lives in vaults attached at the Session level via vault_ids and never in the agent definition. The agent is created once and referenced by ID, so rotation is a single in-place secret update inside the already-attached vault: the immutable shared agent definition, its ID, and every Session's vault_ids binding all stay stable, and no agent logic is redeployed — satisfying all three requirements.
choice_b: Adopt the architect's proposal: encode all three MCP tokens directly in the Agent's mcp_servers configuration at agents.create time so every Session inherits them, and re-run agents.create with the new token whenever the journals provider rotates.
rationale_b: Embedding the tokens in the Agent's mcp_servers configuration puts secrets in the stored agent definition (violating requirement b) and ties rotation to recreating the create-once shared agent — churning the agent ID and effectively redeploying agent logic (violating a). MCP auth is meant to live in vaults, not the agent definition.
choice_c: Keep credentials out of the Agent definition and store the MCP auth in a Vault attached to each Session via vault_ids. On each 14-day rotation, create a fresh Vault holding the new token and update the Sessions to reference the new vault_ids, retiring the old vault.
rationale_c: Vault storage and Session-level vault_ids attachment are both correct, and the agent definition and ID stay untouched — this is very close. The single objective flaw is rotation mechanics: the secret should be updated in place inside the existing vault, so the vault_ids binding never changes. Minting a new vault and rewriting vault_ids on every rotation churns the binding the Sessions reference and violates requirement (c) that rotation be a single in-place change, gaining nothing over a plain secret update.
choice_d: Leave vaults out of it: have each Session pass the three bearer tokens inline in the mcp_servers array on every Messages API request, so rotation just means sending the new token on the next call.
rationale_d: Passing raw bearer tokens inline in the mcp_servers array on every request scatters the secret across all ~240 daily call sites (the opposite of the single in-place change in requirement c) and bypasses the vault mechanism designed to hold MCP auth, making centralized rotation and secret handling harder, not easier.
guide_section: D2 §2.4 — Integrate MCP servers into Claude Code and agent workflows

stem: You are architecting a multi-agent research system on the large, most capable model tier. A lead orchestrator agent dispatches a `fetch_corpus` MCP tool that pulls full-text filings from an internal document service; for a typical query it returns roughly 140K tokens of raw filings across ~60 documents, but the agent usually only needs two or three specific sections to answer. With the raw result streamed straight back into the orchestrator's message history, every subsequent turn re-sends the entire payload, cache_read input balloons, and the orchestrator's reasoning quality visibly degrades as the window fills. The team needs the orchestrator to still be able to consult any part of the corpus when a follow-up demands it, without the full text ever sitting in its context. Which design best fits the platform's published guidance for handling large tool results?
anti_pattern: Routing an oversized tool payload back through the model's context window (or trying to "fix" it with a lossy summarization pass) instead of keeping the bulk out of context entirely and letting the agent read only the slice it needs on demand. Treating /compact-style summarization, a bigger context model, or returning everything-and-letting-the-model-cope as substitutes for never putting the 100K+ payload in context in the first place.
choice_a: Have `fetch_corpus` return all 60 documents but split the 140K tokens across multiple sequential tool_result messages so no single message exceeds a size limit, letting the orchestrator process them one message at a time.
rationale_a: Chunking the payload across messages still places the entire 140K tokens into the orchestrator's context history and re-sends it on subsequent turns; it solves a non-existent per-message limit while leaving the actual context-bloat and cache-cost problem fully intact.
choice_b: Leave `fetch_corpus` unchanged and switch the orchestrator to the large tier's 1M-token context window so all ~140K tokens fit comfortably with room to spare, eliminating the degradation without changing the tool.
rationale_b: A larger context window does not address the core problem: the full payload is re-sent every turn (inflating cache_read cost) and a filling window still degrades reasoning, so "it fits" is not the same as "it belongs in context." The platform's guidance is to keep large intermediate results out of context, not to grow the window to absorb them.
choice_c: Have `fetch_corpus` write the full 140K-token result to a file and return only a path reference, but instead of reading slices on demand, fire a hook after the tool result that runs `/compact` whenever the window grows large, so the orchestrator keeps a compacted summary of the corpus available for later follow-ups.
rationale_c: The file-offload-plus-path-reference half is correct, which is what makes this tempting — but the design then relies on `/compact` to preserve later access, and `/compact` is explicitly reactive and lossy summarization, not a faithful index. The one disqualifying detail: the rarely-needed sections a follow-up will require are precisely the low-salience text compaction drops, so the compacted summary cannot satisfy the "consult any part later" requirement. The correct design instead reads exact slices from the offloaded file on demand, with no summarization in the path.
choice_d: Have `fetch_corpus` write the full 140K-token result to a file in the agent's workspace and return only a short reference (path plus a manifest of section headers/offsets); the orchestrator then reads just the specific slices it needs on demand — ideally via programmatic tool calling, where Claude writes a script that calls the tools so the large intermediate data stays out of the model's context entirely.
rationale_d: The platform's agent-design guidance is to keep large intermediate results out of context: offload the payload to a file and read only the needed slice on demand, and use programmatic tool calling so the bulk of the data never enters the model's context window. This preserves both the prompt cache prefix and reasoning quality while still allowing any section to be retrieved later, satisfying the "consult any part on demand" requirement.
guide_section: D5 §5.1 — Manage conversation context to preserve critical information across long interactions

stem: Helios Research runs a multi-agent literature-review system on the large, most capable model tier. The orchestrator agent has access to a registry of 240 specialized retrieval and citation tools, but any single research turn typically needs only 4-6 of them. The team's frozen system prompt (a 9,000-token methodology brief) and a stable, alphabetically-sorted tool list are placed at the front of every request with a cache_control breakpoint, and they have verified healthy cache_read_input_tokens across turns. An engineer proposes reducing input cost further by having the orchestrator compute, on each turn, exactly which 4-6 tool schemas that turn needs and sending ONLY those in the tools array — arguing that a smaller tools block means fewer input tokens. The lead architect objects that this will hurt, not help. Which approach correctly reduces the per-turn schema cost while preserving the cache, and why?
anti_pattern: Mutating the live tool set per request to "load only what's needed" — swapping or rewriting the tools block on every call — which sits at the front of the cache prefix and so invalidates the entire tools→system→messages cache, the opposite of the intended savings. Tool search is the documented mechanism that appends schemas on demand while leaving the cached tool definitions untouched.
choice_a: Have the orchestrator send only the 4-6 needed tool schemas per turn, but keep them alphabetically sorted and move the cache_control breakpoint to sit after the system prompt so the system brief still benefits from caching even as the tools change.
rationale_a: Genuinely tempting because it preserves stable sorting and tries to protect the expensive 9,000-token brief by repositioning the breakpoint. The single objective flaw: cache matching is prefix-based and tools render BEFORE system, so changing the SET of tools each turn invalidates everything after the mutated tools block — including the system prompt — no matter where the breakpoint sits. You cannot cache content that lives behind a mutated prefix, and sorting a subset does not help when the subset itself differs each turn.
choice_b: Keep the full, stably-sorted tool definitions in the cached prefix and enable tool search, which appends only the relevant tool schemas on demand at request time; because the cached tools block is never mutated, the tools→system→messages prefix stays cache-eligible and cache_read_input_tokens remains high.
rationale_b: Tool search appends only the relevant schemas on demand while leaving the cached tool definitions in place — in contrast to swapping the tool set, which rewrites the front of the prefix. Because the cached tools block is unchanged byte-for-byte, the whole tools→system→messages prefix survives and is billed at the cheap cache-read rate; this is the documented way to scale a large tool registry without re-caching it every turn.
choice_c: Switch the orchestrator to the small, fast model tier for tool-selection turns to cut cost, sending the full 240-tool array each time since the cheaper tier absorbs the larger input token price.
rationale_c: The cache is keyed on the model, so switching models mid-session invalidates the entire cache and forces a full re-cache write while still paying to re-send all 240 schemas. It addresses neither the schema cost nor the caching goal, and the small tier is also less capable at selecting among 240 tools.
choice_d: Move the volatile per-turn tool subset to the very front of the request and place the frozen system brief last, so the smallest, most-changing content is isolated at the start of the prefix.
rationale_d: This inverts the caching rule: stable content (frozen system, sorted tools) must come first and volatile content last, because any byte change in the prefix invalidates everything after it. Putting the changing tool subset first guarantees the cache is busted every turn and re-caches the 9,000-token brief behind it.
guide_section: Appendix — Out of scope for the exam (drill-only)

stem: You are architecting a document-extraction pipeline at a fictional firm, Larchmont Title, that parses ~4,200 scanned property deeds per night and must emit each result as a strictly-shaped JSON object (fixed keys: grantor, grantee, parcel_id, recording_date). The original pipeline ran on earlier-generation models and guaranteed well-formed JSON by prefilling the assistant turn with an opening brace (`{`) so the model could only continue a JSON object. After migrating the calls to a current-generation frontier-tier model, the team finds that every request now returns an HTTP 400 error before any tokens are generated, and the nightly batch fails wholesale. The lead engineer wants the most direct, supported fix that preserves the hard guarantee of schema-conformant JSON without relying on post-hoc string repair. Which change should the architect make?
anti_pattern: The trap is reaching for assistant-message prefill (a trailing `{"role":"assistant"}` turn opening with `{`) to coerce JSON — a once-idiomatic technique that is now REMOVED on current-generation models and returns a 400 before any tokens are generated. The near-miss embodies a subtler version: knowing prefill is gone and correctly choosing schema-enforced output, but reaching for the deprecated top-level `output_format` field instead of the current `output_config.format` carrying a `json_schema`.
choice_a: Set `strict: true` inside `tool_choice` and define a single extraction tool, forcing the model to return arguments matching the schema as the guaranteed-JSON path.
rationale_a: `strict` belongs on the tool DEFINITION (a sibling of name/description/input_schema), not inside `tool_choice`, so this configuration is invalid and would itself fail validation. For direct JSON output the intended mechanism is `output_config.format` with a `json_schema`, not a forced tool call.
choice_b: Remove the now-unsupported assistant prefill and instead set `output_config.format` to `type: "json_object"` (plain JSON mode, without a schema), which restores guaranteed structured output on the migrated model.
rationale_b: Deleting the prefill correctly clears the 400, and `output_config` is the right home — so this tempts a well-prepared candidate. The single objective flaw is `type: "json_object"`: plain JSON mode guarantees only that the output is syntactically valid JSON, not that it contains the required fixed keys (grantor, grantee, parcel_id, recording_date). The hard shape guarantee the architect required needs `type: "json_schema"` with the four-field schema; `json_object` abandons it.
choice_c: Keep the assistant-turn prefill but move it into a `system` instruction that says "begin your reply with {", since the migrated model only rejects prefill when it appears in the messages array, and add a regex post-processor to repair any malformed JSON.
rationale_c: This misstates the mechanism: prefill is removed on the migrated model outright, not merely restricted to the messages array, so phrasing it as a system hint does not restore it. Relying on regex repair also abandons the hard guarantee the architect required — it is exactly the fragile post-hoc approach to be avoided.
choice_d: Remove the assistant-turn prefill entirely (it is no longer supported on the migrated model and is what triggers the 400), and instead set `output_config.format` to a `json_schema` describing the four fields, so the platform enforces the structured shape natively.
rationale_d: Assistant-turn prefill was removed across the current model generation and now returns a 400, so it must be deleted rather than re-phrased. The current supported mechanism for guaranteed JSON is `output_config.format` set to a `json_schema`, which enforces the shape at the platform level instead of via fragile string continuation; this both clears the 400 and preserves the hard schema guarantee with no post-hoc repair.
guide_section: D4 §4.3 — Enforce structured output using tool use and JSON schemas

stem: You are architecting a multi-agent research assistant on the large, most capable model tier. Each research subagent answers a sub-question by reading from three independent read-only sources: a 40,000-document vector index, a corporate wiki, and a SQL warehouse of 1,200 tables. In the current design all three reads go through a single general-purpose `run_shell` tool that shells out to different scripts, and runs are noticeably slow because the subagent issues the three reads strictly one after another even though none depends on the others. The lead architect wants the harness to dispatch the three independent reads concurrently within a single assistant turn, while keeping a hard-to-reverse `write_report` action gated and sequential. Which change best lets the harness schedule the three reads in parallel?
anti_pattern: The traps confuse WHERE concurrent dispatch is actually governed. One steers scheduling through advisory system-prompt text on top of an opaque bash tool (a prompt instruction is advisory and skippable; the harness can only schedule actions it can see individually, and a single shell tool hides its sub-actions). Another flips on disable_parallel_tool_use, which caps the assistant at one tool_use block per turn and silently re-serializes the reads even after the tools are correctly promoted. A third reaches for thinking-effort knobs, which change how much the model reasons, not whether the harness treats an action as parallel-safe.
choice_a: Leave the tools unchanged and instead raise the subagent's effort to `xhigh` and enable adaptive thinking, so the model reasons hard enough to overlap the three reads on its own.
rationale_a: Effort and adaptive thinking control how much the model thinks and how many tool calls it is willing to make, not whether the harness treats individual actions as parallel-safe; concurrency comes from dedicated parallel-safe tool definitions plus parallel-enabled tool_choice, not from thinking budget.
choice_b: Promote each read into its own dedicated tool (`search_vector_index`, `read_wiki`, `query_warehouse`), each defined as a discrete read-only/parallel-safe action, and leave `tool_choice` at its default (`auto`, parallel tool use enabled); the harness can then emit all three tool_use blocks in one assistant message and you return all three tool_results in a single following user message.
rationale_b: A single bash/`run_shell` tool is opaque to the harness, which can only schedule actions it can see individually; promoting each read into its own dedicated, parallel-safe tool exposes three distinct invocable actions. With `tool_choice` left at its default (`auto`, parallel tool use enabled), the model can emit all three independent tool_use blocks in one assistant message, and you return all three tool_results in one following user message — which is exactly how concurrent dispatch works. The hard-to-reverse `write_report` action stays a separate gated tool and is unaffected, so it remains sequential.
choice_c: Promote each read into its own dedicated read-only tool, then set `tool_choice` to `{type: "any", disable_parallel_tool_use: true}` so the subagent is forced to keep calling the new read tools until all three sources are covered.
rationale_c: Promoting the reads to dedicated tools is exactly right, and `tool_choice: any` does force a tool call — but `disable_parallel_tool_use: true` caps the assistant at one tool_use block per response, so the three reads are pushed back into separate sequential turns and the promotion's parallelism is silently defeated. The single subtle flaw is that one flag; drop it (or leave `tool_choice` at default `auto`) and the same answer becomes correct.
choice_d: Keep the single `run_shell` tool but add a system-prompt instruction telling the subagent to always fire the vector, wiki, and warehouse reads at the same time before doing anything else.
rationale_d: A single bash tool stays opaque to the harness, so it cannot schedule its invocations as distinct parallel-safe actions, and a system-prompt instruction is only advisory and can be skipped by the model — it does not change how the harness dispatches tool calls. Concurrency is governed by the tool definitions and tool_choice, not by prompt text.
guide_section: D2 §2.1 — Design effective tool interfaces with clear descriptions and boundaries

stem: A research agent at Halcyon Labs uses the Claude Messages API with the `web_search_20260209` server tool (configured with `max_uses: 5`) to gather sources on niche regulatory topics, running against a frontier-tier model. For most queries the run completes normally, but on one broad regulatory query the model fires six searches in a single turn and the sixth exhausts the `max_uses` budget. The engineer wrapped the entire `client.messages.create(...)` call in a try/except (try/catch), expecting a failed web search to raise an exception they could log and retry. Instead no exception fires — the request returns normally with `stop_reason` `end_turn` — yet downstream code that iterates the search results throws a `TypeError` when it reaches that block. The engineer also notices that other queries which simply found nothing did NOT throw. How should the engineer detect and handle a web-search tool failure correctly?
anti_pattern: Treating a platform server-side tool (web search / web fetch) failure as a thrown/HTTP exception, so the handler wraps the API call in try/except and never inspects the returned result block — meaning genuine tool errors (which arrive as a normal 200 with an error object inside the tool-result content) slip through as if the call succeeded, or the code crashes trying to index an error object as a results list.
choice_a: Keep the try/except but catch the SDK's `APIStatusError` specifically; a web-search budget overrun returns an HTTP 429, so catching the status error lets you read the `retry-after` header and back off before retrying the turn.
rationale_a: A `max_uses_exceeded` server-tool failure is not an HTTP-level error and produces no 429 or `APIStatusError` — the request succeeds with status 200 and `stop_reason` `end_turn`, so no exception is ever raised to catch and `retry-after` is irrelevant. The failure lives in the result block, not the HTTP layer.
choice_b: Add `strict: true` to `tool_choice` as `{"type": "tool", "name": "web_search", "strict": true}` so the API validates the tool call and raises a 400 when the search cannot complete, converting the failure into a catchable exception.
rationale_b: `strict` belongs on a tool DEFINITION (for input-schema validation, alongside `name`/`input_schema`), not on `tool_choice`, and neither mechanism converts a server-tool runtime failure into a raised exception. `tool_choice` only constrains whether/which tool is selected; server-tool errors are always returned as result-block data, never as a 400.
choice_c: The web-search failure does surface inside the response as a `web_search_tool_result` block, so stop relying on try/except and inspect the block instead — and detect the failure by checking whether the block's `content` list is empty, treating a zero-length results list as the error signal.
rationale_c: This correctly abandons the try/except and inspects the result block, but its failure detector is objectively wrong on one checkable point: on error the block's `content` is an error object (e.g. `{"error_code": "max_uses_exceeded"}`), not an empty list — iterating it is exactly what raised the `TypeError`. An empty list is a legitimate "no results found" success (the queries that found nothing and did NOT throw), so this both misses real errors and flags successful empty searches as failures — collapsing empty-as-success into error.
choice_d: The over-budget search returns a successful HTTP 200 whose assistant content includes a `web_search_tool_result` block whose `content` is a single error object (e.g. `{"type": "web_search_tool_result_error", "error_code": "max_uses_exceeded"}`) rather than a list of result items. Iterate the `web_search_tool_result` blocks and branch on the shape of each block's `content` — an error object means the tool failed, a (possibly empty) list means it succeeded — since the failure is returned as data and is never raised.
rationale_d: The platform's server-side tools run inside the model turn and report failures as data: a normal 200 response carrying an error object inside the tool-result block, with `stop_reason` still `end_turn`. The try/except never fires because nothing is raised, and the `TypeError` comes from iterating an error object as if it were a results list. Correct handling inspects the block's `content` shape (object = error, list = success) — the same principle that says distinguish a genuinely-empty result from a failure rather than collapsing both.
guide_section: D2 §2.2 — Implement structured error responses for MCP tools

stem: You are the architect for "LedgerLift," a batch invoice-extraction agent that classifies each of ~40,000 scanned invoices into exactly one of nine accounting buckets (e.g., "cogs", "opex", "payroll", "tax_withholding"). The pipeline runs on the balanced mid-tier model, and you expose a single tool, record_classification, whose input_schema declares a "bucket" property as a string with an "enum" listing the nine allowed bucket ids, plus a free-text "evidence" field. During a 2,000-invoice pilot, a downstream ledger import job occasionally rejects a row because the bucket value does not match any of the nine ids (e.g., the model emitted "cost_of_goods" instead of "cogs"). Your finance lead insists the enum should make out-of-set values "structurally impossible," and proposes simply re-emphasizing the allowed ids in the tool description. As the architect, what is the most correct way to guarantee that every recorded bucket is one of the nine ids, and how should the agent loop handle a value that still falls outside the set?
anti_pattern: Believing that an enum declared in a tool's input_schema is, by itself, hard-enforced at the API boundary — and conflating schema declaration with schema validation. The trap is assuming that listing allowed values guarantees the model can never emit anything else, and that no downstream validation or strict-mode enforcement is required.
choice_a: Set strict:true on the record_classification tool definition (as a sibling of name/description/input_schema) so the API constrains generation to the schema, and ensure the input_schema has additionalProperties:false with "bucket" marked required; additionally, parse each tool input with json.loads and, if a bucket value is ever still out of the nine-id set, return that tool_result with is_error:true so the model can self-correct rather than letting the bad value reach the ledger import.
rationale_a: A bare enum in the input_schema documents the allowed set but is not guaranteed hard-enforced; strict:true on the tool definition is what makes the API constrain generation to the schema, and it requires additionalProperties:false plus required fields — with an is_error:true tool_result as the validation backstop for any value that still escapes.
choice_b: Drop the enum from the tool schema entirely and instead detect out-of-set buckets by raw-string-matching the serialized tool-call input against the nine id strings before forwarding; if no match is found, silently substitute the closest id and continue the loop without surfacing an error.
rationale_b: Tool inputs must be parsed with json.loads/JSON.parse rather than raw-string-matched against the serialized input, and silently substituting a guessed value masks the failure (treating a bad result as a success) instead of returning an is_error:true tool_result that lets the model self-correct — so wrong buckets would still reach the ledger, just relabeled.
choice_c: Keep the enum as the sole enforcement mechanism and re-emphasize the nine allowed ids in the tool's description text; since the enum is declared in the input_schema, any value the model emits is structurally guaranteed to be one of the nine ids, so no downstream validation or error handling is needed.
rationale_c: A declared enum documents intent but is not by itself a hard guarantee at the API boundary, and prose emphasis is advisory; relying on it alone is exactly the anti-pattern that let "cost_of_goods" reach the ledger import.
choice_d: Add strict:true inside the tool_choice object ({type:"tool", name:"record_classification", strict:true}) so generation is constrained to the enum, keep additionalProperties:false and "bucket" required in the input_schema, and on any out-of-set value return a tool_result with is_error:true so the model retries.
rationale_d: The validation backstop and schema requirements are right, but the objective flaw is placement: strict:true belongs on the TOOL definition (sibling of name/description/input_schema), not inside the tool_choice object — putting it on tool_choice does not turn on strict schema enforcement.
guide_section: D4 §4.3 — Enforce structured output using tool use and JSON schemas

stem: You are architecting the orchestrator loop for "Atlas," a multi-agent research assistant built on the balanced mid-tier model via the Messages API. For a single research step, Atlas issues four read-only retrieval tools in one assistant message — `search_arxiv`, `search_pubmed`, `search_internal_kb`, and `fetch_url` — emitting four `tool_use` blocks in that one response. Your retrieval layer runs the four calls concurrently against different backends, so they complete in nondeterministic order (PubMed routinely finishes last, and `fetch_url` occasionally throws a timeout). A junior engineer's first implementation streams each result back to the model the instant its backend returns: it appends one `tool_result` to a fresh `user` message per completion, correlates each result to its call by matching the Nth `tool_result` to the Nth `tool_use` in submission order, and on the `fetch_url` timeout it simply omits that block so the conversation "stays clean." In code review you notice Atlas frequently reasons over the wrong retrieval payloads and sometimes stalls. Which change correctly fixes the tool-result protocol for this parallel step?
anti_pattern: Treating parallel tool calls as independent serial turns: splitting their tool_results across multiple user messages and/or pairing results to calls by array position instead of by tool_use_id. This breaks correlation when results arrive out of order or are batched, and degrades the model's parallel-tool-use behavior.
choice_a: Keep streaming each result back the instant its backend returns — one `tool_result` per `user` message — but fix correlation by stamping each `tool_result` with the matching `tool_use_id`, and drop the `fetch_url` block on timeout since a missing result is equivalent to an empty search.
rationale_a: Splitting the results of parallel `tool_use` blocks across multiple `user` messages degrades parallel tool use, and silently omitting the timed-out tool's `tool_result` leaves a `tool_use` unanswered (stalling the loop) while conflating an error with a genuinely empty result. Correct `tool_use_id` stamping alone does not repair these two violations.
choice_b: Wait for all four backends, then return all four `tool_result` blocks together in one `user` message — but keep correlating each `tool_result` to its call by submission position (Nth result to Nth `tool_use`), and for the `fetch_url` timeout still emit an error `tool_result` with `is_error: true`.
rationale_b: The objective flaw is correlating results to calls by array position instead of by `tool_use_id`. Even when all results are batched into one message, the API binds each `tool_result` to its `tool_use` solely via the matching `tool_use_id`; position-based pairing mis-routes payloads whenever the assembled order differs from submission order, which is exactly what produces "reasoning over the wrong payload."
choice_c: Collect all four outcomes, then return them as four `tool_result` blocks inside a SINGLE `user` message, each carrying the `tool_use_id` of the `tool_use` block it answers; for the `fetch_url` timeout, include a `tool_result` with that call's `tool_use_id` and `is_error: true` rather than omitting it.
rationale_c: Multiple parallel `tool_use` blocks in one assistant message must be answered by ALL their `tool_result` blocks in ONE following `user` message, and each result is bound to its call by `tool_use_id` (never by position); a failed tool is reported with `is_error: true`, never silently dropped. This makes correlation order-independent and keeps the loop from stalling on a missing block.
choice_d: Set `disable_parallel_tool_use: true` so the four retrievals are forced into separate turns, then answer each one with a single `tool_result` (matched by `tool_use_id`) in its own `user` message; on the `fetch_url` timeout return `is_error: true`.
rationale_d: Disabling parallel tool use sidesteps rather than fixes the protocol and discards the concurrency the architecture was built for, serializing four independent read-only retrievals into four round-trips. The real defect is how parallel results are batched and correlated, not that the model emitted parallel calls.
guide_section: D1 §1.1 — Design and implement agentic loops for autonomous task execution

stem: Northwind Intelligence runs a multi-agent competitive-research system on the Claude API. A long-lived "analyst" agent uses the client-side memory tool (`{"type": "memory_20250818", "name": "memory"}`) to carry findings across sessions; the team implements the storage backend themselves, persisting each `/memories` file to a shared S3 bucket that several analysts and an audit pipeline can later read. During research the agent routinely ingests scraped pages and internal CRM exports that contain customer names, email addresses, and the occasional leaked API key — and it has begun writing some of that raw material verbatim into memory files via `create` and `str_replace`. Security needs a guarantee that secrets and PII never land in the persisted store, even if a scraped page contains text engineered to make the agent save it. Which design gives that guarantee?
anti_pattern: Treating a system-prompt instruction ("never write secrets to memory") as if it were an enforcement control. A prompt instruction is advisory — the model can ignore or be steered past it — so it cannot guarantee that secrets and PII stay out of the persisted memory store. The durable control must live in the client-side memory backend (the tool handler the architect implements), which deterministically inspects and redacts every write before it touches disk.
choice_a: In the memory tool handler — the code that executes the `create`, `str_replace`, and `insert` commands — run every proposed file body through a deterministic secret/PII detector before writing to S3, redacting or rejecting matches; this enforcement lives in the backend the team controls, so it holds regardless of what the model decides to write.
rationale_a: The memory tool is client-side: Claude emits the command but the team's own handler performs the persistence, so a deterministic check in that handler is the only layer that can guarantee secrets/PII never reach the store — programmatic enforcement in code you control, not model goodwill, is what makes the invariant hold under adversarial input.
choice_b: Add a firmly worded instruction to the analyst agent's system prompt — "Never write API keys, passwords, tokens, or customer PII into memory files; redact them first" — and rely on the model to scrub each value before it issues a `create` or `str_replace`.
rationale_b: This correctly identifies the right content to keep out and the right commands to watch, but the flaw is objective: a system-prompt instruction is advisory, not enforced — the model can be steered past it by injected text in a scraped page, so it cannot guarantee secrets stay out of the persisted store. The guarantee requires a deterministic check in the client-side handler that actually writes the file.
choice_c: Switch the agent to summarized extended thinking (`thinking: {type: "adaptive", display: "summarized"}`) so a reviewer can read the model's reasoning and catch any moment it decides to persist a secret before the write happens.
rationale_c: Thinking display only controls whether reasoning summaries are surfaced; it does not gate or inspect the actual create/str_replace payload, happens too late, and requires a human in the loop — it provides visibility, not the deterministic guarantee that secrets never reach the persisted store.
choice_d: Enable context editing with `clear_tool_uses_20250919` so old memory tool results are cleared from the conversation, ensuring previously-seen secrets are dropped from the agent's context before they can be written to disk.
rationale_d: Context editing prunes stale tool results from the in-flight transcript to manage the context window; it does nothing to inspect or redact what the agent writes to the memory backend, so secrets and PII still flow straight into the persisted store.
guide_section: D1 §1.5 — Apply Agent SDK hooks for tool call interception and data normalization

stem: A team at a fictional firm, Larkspur Claims, is building a Claude pipeline on the large, most capable model tier to extract structured records from roughly 40,000 scanned insurance documents per night. Each extracted record must conform exactly to an `extract_claim` tool whose input_schema has six fields (claimant_name, policy_number, loss_date, amount_usd, adjuster_id, notes), of which the first four are mandatory and the last two are optional. During a pilot they observed Claude occasionally emitting extra, hallucinated keys (e.g. `confidence`, `source_page`) that broke their downstream Postgres loader, and — separately — roughly 1 in 300 records arrived with `amount_usd` missing entirely, silently inserting NULL into a NOT NULL column. They have decided to turn on strict schema enforcement so that every tool call is guaranteed to match the schema's shape. Which configuration correctly enforces the schema and eliminates BOTH the extra-key problem and the missing-`amount_usd` problem?
anti_pattern: Treating strict structured output as a single boolean flag while neglecting the schema-level constraints it actually requires (additionalProperties:false plus a complete required array that names every mandatory field), or attaching the flag to the wrong object (tool_choice instead of the tool definition).
choice_a: Leave the tool definition unchanged and instead set `tool_choice: {type: "tool", name: "extract_claim", strict: true}`, since strict enforcement is a property of how the tool is selected on each request.
rationale_a: strict is a property of the tool definition (sibling of name/description/input_schema), not of tool_choice. Placing it on tool_choice does not enable schema enforcement and would not constrain the emitted keys or required fields. tool_choice only governs WHICH tool must be used, not the SHAPE of its output — so neither observed failure is addressed.
choice_b: Keep the schema as-is and instead add `output_config.format` with a json_schema plus an assistant-turn prefill seeding `{"claimant_name":` to force Claude to start emitting only the allowed fields.
rationale_b: output_config.format governs the JSON shape of the final assistant response, not the input shape of a tool call, so it cannot enforce the extract_claim schema. Worse, assistant-turn prefill is removed on this model generation and returns a 400, so this is not even a valid request on this model. It addresses neither the extra keys nor the missing required field.
choice_c: Set `strict: true` on the `extract_claim` tool definition itself (as a sibling of name/description/input_schema), and in that input_schema set `additionalProperties: false` and list claimant_name, policy_number, loss_date, AND amount_usd in the `required` array.
rationale_c: strict:true belongs on the tool definition (a sibling of name/description/input_schema), and it only guarantees schema conformance when the schema is itself complete. additionalProperties:false forbids the hallucinated extra keys like confidence and source_page, and a required array that enumerates all four mandatory fields — critically including amount_usd — forces those fields to be present. This is exactly the combination the two observed failures demand: extra-key suppression plus required-field enforcement.
choice_d: Set `strict: true` on the `extract_claim` tool definition and add `additionalProperties: false` to its input_schema, with a `required` array listing claimant_name, policy_number, and loss_date (the optional adjuster_id and notes are deliberately omitted).
rationale_d: This is correct in mechanism and placement — strict on the tool definition, additionalProperties:false to kill the extra keys — and it correctly leaves the optional fields out of required. Its single objective flaw is that the required array omits amount_usd, the one mandatory field they actually saw missing. Because amount_usd is not enumerated as required, strict mode still permits a record without it, so the NOT-NULL loader failure they observed is NOT eliminated. The fix is to add amount_usd to required; the distinction from the correct answer is exactly that one field.
guide_section: D4 §4.3 — Enforce structured output using tool use and JSON schemas

stem: You are the architect for "LedgerMint," an internal finance-ops agent at a mid-size firm. The recurring job: each weeknight, parse 40-some vendor invoice PDFs that land in `/srv/invoices/` on the company's own on-prem Linux host, normalize them with an in-house Python CLI (`invtool extract`) that depends on a license file and a Postgres connection string that exist only on that host, and write a reconciliation CSV back to a local NFS share. The vendor portal that produced the PDFs has a clean documented REST API and a published `invtool` command line; there is no requirement to click through any GUI. The agent loop is driven through the Messages API, and the firm's security team mandates that the invoice data and the Postgres credentials never leave their network. You must pick the tool that lets Claude drive this automation. Which choice is correct, and why?
anti_pattern: Reaching for computer use or the server-side code execution sandbox when the job's real constraints (a programmatic API/CLI exists; the data and credentials live only on the customer's own host) actually point to the client-side bash tool. The traps embody the failure to match the tool surface to where execution must physically happen and what the tool can reach: computer use for something that has a clean programmatic interface, and the server-hosted code-execution sandbox for work that needs the customer's local files, network, and secrets — which that sandbox cannot reach.
choice_a: Skip a dedicated execution tool entirely and have Claude return the shell commands and Python as text in its response, so an operator can copy-paste and run them on the on-prem host each night.
rationale_a: Emitting commands as plain text abandons the agent loop: there is no tool_use/tool_result cycle, so Claude never sees stdout, exit codes, or errors and cannot iterate or recover — it turns an automatable nightly job into a manual copy-paste ritual.
choice_b: Give Claude the server-side code execution tool (`{"type": "code_execution_20250522", "name": "code_execution"}`) so it can write and run Python to call `invtool extract` and process the PDFs in a managed sandbox you don't have to operate, then have it write the reconciliation CSV.
rationale_b: Like bash, code execution can run Python and shell out to `invtool`, which is what makes it tempting — but it is fully server-side: the container runs on the model provider's infrastructure, not on the firm's host. That single fact is decisive: it sits on the wrong side of the network boundary, so the on-prem `/srv/invoices/`, the host-local license file, the Postgres connection, and the NFS share are physically unreachable from it (and routing the invoice data to it would violate the no-egress mandate). The flaw is not the capability of the tool; it is the location of the execution environment.
choice_c: Use computer use (the `computer` tool) so Claude can open the vendor portal in a browser, visually locate each invoice, and operate the `invtool` desktop workflow through screenshots and simulated mouse and keyboard actions.
rationale_c: Computer use is for driving GUIs that have no programmatic interface; here a documented REST API and a published CLI already exist, so screenshot-and-click automation is slower, far more brittle, and strictly unnecessary — the wrong surface for a job that is fully scriptable.
choice_d: Give Claude the client-side bash tool (declared as `{"type": "bash_20250124", "name": "bash"}`, schema-less), and run the tool-execution harness on the on-prem host so each `bash` command (running `invtool extract`, reading `/srv/invoices/`, writing the NFS share) executes locally where the files, license, Postgres connection, and network already are; the data and credentials never leave the firm's network.
rationale_d: The bash tool is client-side: Claude emits the command, but your own harness executes it — so it runs wherever you host that harness. Hosting it on-prem keeps all execution next to the local files, license, DB, and network, satisfying the no-egress mandate while still giving Claude broad programmatic leverage over the existing CLI and API.
guide_section: D2 §2.5 — Select and apply built-in tools (Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob) effectively

stem: At Meridian Title, an invoice-processing agent built on the balanced mid-tier model exposes a tool `extract_line_item` whose input schema has fields `vendor_id` (string), `amount_cents` (integer), and `memo` (free-text string). The harness loop is correct: it continues while `stop_reason == "tool_use"` and returns each tool_result with its matching `tool_use_id`. To dispatch the tool, a junior engineer wrote a handler that takes the assistant message's serialized tool_use block and runs a regex like `"amount_cents":\s*(\d+)` against the raw JSON string to read the amount, plus a similar pattern for `vendor_id`. In a batch of 4,200 invoices, roughly 3% produce a wrong `amount_cents`; on spot-checking, the failures cluster on invoices where the `memo` field contains text like `"ref amount_cents: 1999"`, or where Claude emitted the object's keys in a different order. What is the correct way to read the tool arguments, and why does the current approach fail?
anti_pattern: Treating the serialized JSON of a tool_use.input as plain text and pulling out argument values via substring/regex matching, rather than deserializing it into a structured object. Raw string matching is brittle to key ordering, whitespace, Unicode/escape encoding, nested objects, and values that legitimately contain the matched substring — it silently extracts wrong values rather than failing loudly.
choice_a: Set `disable_parallel_tool_use: true` so Claude can only emit one tool_use block per response; with a single tool call in the message, the regex will always match the correct `amount_cents` and the wrong-value problem disappears.
rationale_a: This conflates concurrency with parsing: disable_parallel_tool_use only limits how many tool_use blocks appear per turn. It does nothing about substring collisions inside a single block's serialized input, so the wrong-value extraction persists.
