The stable prefix (tools → system) is then byte-identical across requests and is read from cache; only the volatile per-invoice content lives after the breakpoint.
rationale_a: Caching is a prefix match over the tools→system→messages render order, and any byte change in the prefix invalidates everything after it; datetime.now() and a per-invoice document_id in the system prompt are classic silent invalidators that change the prefix every request, so the cache is written but never read. Moving stable content first and the volatile per-invoice content into the trailing user message makes the prefix byte-identical and produces high cache_read_input_tokens.
choice_b: Switch to the small, fast model tier for this workload and enable the 1-hour cache TTL (ttl:"1h") on the system block; the cheaper model plus the longer-lived cache will raise cache_read_input_tokens across the day's 120,000 invoices.
rationale_b: Neither change addresses the root cause: the prefix still changes every request because of datetime.now() and document_id, so a longer TTL caches an entry that is never reused, and switching models actually invalidates the cache (changing the model invalidates the whole cache) while doing nothing to stabilize the prefix.
choice_c: Leave the system prompt structure exactly as-is (rules + datetime.now() + document_id) but add three more cache_control breakpoints — one after the tools, one after the rules, and one after the document_id — using all four allowed breakpoints so more of the request is covered and at least the earlier segments get cache hits.
rationale_c: Adding breakpoints does not help when the issue is that volatile content (datetime.now(), document_id) sits inside the prefix: a breakpoint only marks where a cacheable prefix ends, and any byte change earlier still invalidates everything after it, so the per-request timestamp before later segments keeps them from being read regardless of how many of the four breakpoints are used.
choice_d: Keep the frozen rules + schema and the per-run `Processing run started at {datetime.now()}` line together in the system block (so each run's full context is cached as a unit), put the cache_control breakpoint at the end of that system block, and move only the document_id into the trailing user message. Within a single processing run the timestamp is constant, so the prefix stays stable for that run's invoices.
rationale_d: The objective flaw: leaving datetime.now() inside the cached system block still places a volatile value in the prefix. The reasoning that "the timestamp is constant within a run" is wrong because datetime.now() is evaluated per request, not once per run, so it changes on essentially every call and invalidates the prefix — the timestamp must be removed from the system prompt (or moved after the breakpoint), not merely grouped with the stable rules.
guide_section: Appendix — Out of scope for the exam (drill-only)

stem: Northwind Support runs a customer-support agent on the large, most capable model tier. Every request sends the same 14 tool definitions (a ~3,800-token, name-sorted, byte-stable block) followed by an ~9,000-token system prompt holding the frozen support policy and macro library; only the trailing `messages` (the live ticket thread) vary per request. Traffic is continuous — thousands of tickets an hour — and the team wants the entire tools-plus-system prefix served from cache on every request after the first, using exactly one `cache_control` breakpoint. An engineer is about to add the single breakpoint and asks you where it must go so that both the tools and the system prompt are cached together. Assume nothing else in the prefix changes between requests.
anti_pattern: Placing the cache_control breakpoint on the wrong block relative to render order (tools → system → messages), so the intended stable prefix is only partially cached — caching the tools but leaving the system prompt uncached because system renders *after* the tools.
choice_a: Put the single `cache_control: {"type": "ephemeral"}` breakpoint on the last (14th) tool definition. Anchoring the breakpoint at the end of the first stable tier caches the whole tool array, and since the system prompt and tools are both unchanging the entire stable prefix is covered by that one entry.
rationale_a: This is genuinely tempting: anchoring at the end of the first tier is the right instinct for the tools, and both tiers really are byte-stable. The single objective flaw is direction — a breakpoint caches the prefix *up to and including* its block, i.e. everything that renders *before* it, and `system` renders *after* `tools`. So a breakpoint on the last tool caches only the tool array; the ~9,000-token system prompt sits behind the breakpoint and is re-processed at full input price on every request. Stability of the system prompt is irrelevant — it is never inside the cached prefix to begin with. Caching tools+system together requires the breakpoint at the end of the *last* tier in the desired prefix, which is system, not tools.
choice_b: Put the single breakpoint on the last block of the live ticket thread (the final `messages` entry), so the whole request — tools, system, and the current ticket — is cached as one prefix.
rationale_b: Wrong because the trailing messages vary per request, and any byte change inside a cached prefix invalidates everything from that point. A breakpoint after the live ticket makes the cached prefix unique to each ticket, so every request writes a fresh entry (at ~1.25x) and none is ever read — the opposite of reusing the stable tools+system prefix.
choice_c: Put the single `cache_control: {"type": "ephemeral"}` breakpoint on the last block of the `system` prompt. Because render order is tools → system → messages, a breakpoint there caches everything before it — the entire tool array and the full system prompt — so each later request reads ~12,800 tokens from cache.
rationale_c: A cache breakpoint caches the rendered prefix up to and including the block it sits on, and the render order is tools → system → messages. A breakpoint on the last system block therefore spans both the tools and the entire system prompt in one cache entry — the documented single-breakpoint pattern for caching tools+system together. Because both tiers are byte-stable, every later request gets a cache_read for the full ~12,800-token prefix.
choice_d: Put one breakpoint on the last tool definition and a second on the last system block. You need two breakpoints — one to close the tools tier and one to close the system tier — or the system prompt won't cache.
rationale_d: Wrong because a single breakpoint on the last system block already caches everything that renders before it, including all 14 tools; cache prefixes are cumulative, not per-tier. The task explicitly asks for exactly one breakpoint, and a second one buys nothing for the tools+system goal (each of the 4 allowed breakpoints just marks an additional cacheable prefix length, not a separate required 'tier closer').
guide_section: Appendix — Out of scope for the exam (drill-only)

stem: A support-automation team at a fictional company, Larkfield Logistics, runs an agent on the large, most capable model tier that handles roughly 9,000 customer chats per day. To save cost they put their large, stable 14KB triage policy at the top of the system prompt with a cache_control:{type:"ephemeral"} breakpoint right after it, then append the customer's latest message as the user turn. After deploying, an engineer notices usage.cache_read_input_tokens is 0 on essentially every request and their bill barely changed. Reviewing the code, they find the system prompt is assembled as the 14KB policy text followed by the line "Current server time: " + datetime.now().isoformat(), and the cache_control breakpoint sits after that whole system block. What is the correct way to restore cache hits while keeping the per-request timestamp available to the model?
anti_pattern: Treating prompt caching as a switch you turn on (add cache_control / add more breakpoints) rather than a prefix-match contract: leaving a per-request volatile value (a timestamp) anywhere inside the cached prefix silently busts the entire cache on every call, and the "fix" must remove the volatility from the prefix, not just annotate around it.
choice_a: Move the datetime.now() timestamp out of the system prompt entirely and into the per-request user message (after the cached prefix), so the stable 14KB policy is the unchanging prefix and the cache_control breakpoint follows only stable content; verify the fix by confirming usage.cache_read_input_tokens is non-zero on the second and subsequent requests.
rationale_a: Prompt caching is a prefix match: any byte change before a breakpoint invalidates everything after it, so a datetime.now() value embedded in the cached prefix changes every request and guarantees a full cache miss. The fix is to keep only stable content before the breakpoint and push the volatile timestamp into the message turn (rendered last, after tools and system), then confirm reuse with usage.cache_read_input_tokens rather than assuming the annotation worked.
choice_b: Keep the timestamp in the system prompt but reorder the system block so the volatile "Current server time" line comes first and the 14KB stable policy comes after it, then place the cache_control breakpoint after the policy text.
rationale_b: This embodies the correct instinct (stable content must precede the breakpoint) but the objective flaw is that the changing timestamp now sits at the very start of the cached prefix, so the prefix's first bytes differ on every request and the prefix match fails before it ever reaches the policy bytes — the cache still misses 100% of the time. Order within the prefix does not matter; any volatile byte anywhere before the breakpoint busts the whole prefix, which is why the timestamp has to leave the prefix, not move within it.
choice_c: Switch the cache_control TTL from the default 5-minute ephemeral window to the 1-hour TTL (ttl:"1h"), since a longer cache lifetime will let the entries survive long enough to be reused across the 9,000 daily chats.
rationale_c: TTL only governs how long a written cache entry lives; it is irrelevant when the prefix itself changes every request, because a different prefix is never a hit regardless of how long the previous one is retained. The root cause is prefix invalidation by datetime.now(), not an expiring cache, and a longer TTL would only raise the write cost (1h writes cost more) while changing nothing about the miss rate.
choice_d: Leave the timestamp at the end of the system block but move the single cache_control breakpoint onto the user message instead, so that the full request (policy plus timestamp plus the customer message) is captured by the cache and reused on the next chat.
rationale_d: This caches a prefix whose tail is the per-request customer message and the per-request timestamp — both change on every chat, so the written entry can never be matched by the next request and the hit rate stays at zero. Worse, it discards the one stable thing that could have been cached: the breakpoint no longer sits after the 14KB policy at all. Caching helps only when the bytes before the breakpoint repeat; placing the breakpoint after volatile content guarantees a fresh, unreusable prefix every time. The real fix is removing the volatility from the prefix, not relocating the breakpoint over it.
guide_section: Appendix — Out of scope for the exam (drill-only)

stem: At Meridian Claims, you run a high-volume invoice-extraction pipeline on the balanced mid-tier model that processes about 40,000 documents a day. Every request shares the same 9,200-token instruction block (extraction schema, field definitions, formatting rules) which you mark with cache_control as a system-prompt prefix, and each invoice's raw OCR text is appended as the final user message. To make debugging easier, a teammate proposes folding a per-request "processed_at" UTC timestamp and the document's unique intake_id into the system prompt right after the instructions, arguing it keeps an audit trail visible at the top of every call. After deploying this, your usage.cache_read_input_tokens collapses to near zero and your token bill climbs sharply, even though extraction accuracy is unchanged. Which change best restores cache efficiency while preserving the audit data?
anti_pattern: Treating the system prompt as a convenient place to drop per-request volatile data (timestamps, document IDs, the document text itself). Because prompt caching is a prefix match, any byte that changes between requests invalidates the cache for everything after that byte — so volatile content placed in the frozen prefix silently destroys cache reuse, even though the call still returns correct results.
choice_a: Leave the timestamp and intake_id where they are in the system prompt (right after the instructions) but move the cache_control breakpoint to sit immediately AFTER those audit fields, so the cached prefix now spans the instructions plus the per-request timestamp and intake_id and you get one large cached block per call.
rationale_a: This is tempting because extending the breakpoint does cache a larger block — but the single fatal flaw is that the volatile timestamp/intake_id are now INSIDE the cached prefix, and the prefix is matched byte-for-byte. Since those bytes differ on every request, the prefix is unique every call and the cache misses every time; cache_read_input_tokens stays near zero. A breakpoint can only help when everything before it is stable. The fix is not where the breakpoint sits but removing the volatile bytes from the prefix entirely and placing them last, in the user message.
choice_b: Switch the cache_control TTL from the default 5-minute ephemeral window to the 1-hour TTL, since the cache is expiring between requests and a longer-lived cache will absorb the per-request timestamp changes.
rationale_b: TTL only controls how long an already-matched cached prefix survives while idle; it has no bearing on prefix invalidation. A changing timestamp in the prefix produces a fresh, non-matching prefix on every request, so there is nothing for the longer window to keep alive — a 1-hour TTL cannot absorb byte differences and the collapse persists.
choice_c: Remove the per-request timestamp and intake_id from the system prompt so the entire cached prefix (tools->system) is byte-identical across requests, and instead carry that volatile audit data in the per-request user message after the cache breakpoint; verify the fix by confirming usage.cache_read_input_tokens rises back toward the ~9,200-token prefix size.
rationale_c: Prompt caching is a prefix match rendered in tools->system->messages order: any byte change in the cached prefix invalidates everything after it, so a changing timestamp/intake_id placed in the system prompt is a silent invalidator that destroys every cache hit while leaving accuracy untouched. Moving the volatile audit fields into the user message after the breakpoint keeps the prefix frozen and reusable, restoring cache reads — confirmed by watching usage.cache_read_input_tokens climb back toward the ~9,200-token prefix size.
choice_d: Downgrade the pipeline to the small, fast model tier to cut per-token cost, since extraction accuracy is already unchanged and the cheaper model offsets the lost cache savings.
rationale_d: Changing the model is irrelevant to the root cause and switching models itself invalidates the entire cache; the volatile system-prompt data would still break caching on the small tier exactly as it does on the mid tier. This treats a symptom (cost) and abandons the working model instead of fixing the broken prefix.
guide_section: Appendix — Out of scope for the exam (drill-only)

stem: At Meridian Claims, a data-extraction pipeline runs the balanced mid-tier model over roughly 38,000 scanned insurance forms per night. The request prefix is heavily cache-optimized: a frozen system prompt followed by a deterministically sorted list of 6 tools, with a cache_control breakpoint after the tool block, and only the per-form image and a short instruction vary at the tail. For each form the team needs Claude to ALWAYS return its output by calling exactly one tool, extract_form_fields, and never to emit a free-text answer or call any of the other 5 tools. They also need the tool block's cache write to keep being reused (verified via usage.cache_read_input_tokens) so the per-form cost stays low. Which approach meets both requirements?
anti_pattern: Reaching for the wrong lever: editing or pruning the tool definitions (or misplacing strict/single-tool flags) to make the model emit one specific structured tool call, when tool_choice already pins the call deterministically and editing tools needlessly invalidates the prompt cache.
choice_a: Keep tool_choice at {"type":"auto"} but prepend a strong system-prompt instruction ("You MUST always call extract_form_fields and never reply in text") so the behavior is enforced by the prompt.
rationale_a: A prompt instruction is advisory — {type:auto} still lets the model choose free text or another tool, so it cannot deterministically guarantee the single forced call; editing the system prompt also changes the cached prefix and invalidates the breakpoint, defeating the cost requirement.
choice_b: Leave the tool definitions and their sort order byte-for-byte unchanged, and set tool_choice to {"type":"tool","name":"extract_form_fields"} on each per-form request. Forcing a named tool guarantees that exact single call, and because toggling tool_choice does not alter the tools or system prefix, the cached breakpoint after the tool block keeps producing cache_read hits.
rationale_b: tool_choice {type:tool,name:...} compels Claude to call exactly that one tool, satisfying the "always one specific call, never free text or another tool" requirement; and because tool_choice lives in the request controls rather than the cached tools+system prefix, changing it does not invalidate the cache, so cache_read hits continue.
choice_c: Delete the other 5 tools from the request so extract_form_fields is the only option, leave tool_choice at its default {"type":"auto"}, and set strict:true on tool_choice to force structured output.
rationale_c: This is wrong on three counts: editing the tool list changes the cached prefix and invalidates the cache (losing cache_read hits and raising cost); {type:auto} lets Claude still answer in free text instead of calling the tool; and strict:true belongs on the tool definition, not on tool_choice.
choice_d: Keep the tool prefix unchanged and set tool_choice to {"type":"any"} on each request so Claude is forced to call a tool rather than answer in free text; the cache prefix is untouched so cache_read hits continue.
rationale_d: The objective flaw: {type:any} forces the model to use SOME tool but does not pin which one, so on any given form Claude may call one of the other 5 tools — it fails the "exactly extract_form_fields and never another tool" requirement. Only {type:tool,name:"extract_form_fields"} pins the specific tool. Everything else (untouched prefix, cache hits) is right, which is exactly what makes it tempting.
guide_section: Appendix — Out of scope for the exam (drill-only)

stem: A platform team at a fintech called Larkfield runs an internal code-review assistant that has been pinned to a previous-generation balanced mid-tier model for the past year. Their request template sets thinking with a fixed budget_tokens of 8000 so reviews of large diffs get consistent deep reasoning, and they want some reviews to think even harder than others depending on diff size. They are now migrating the service to the current-generation large, most capable model tier to handle their largest monorepo PRs, and an engineer proposes simply bumping the model id and leaving the rest of the thinking configuration unchanged. During a pre-migration review you are asked which change to the thinking configuration is required for the migrated requests to succeed on the new model and to still let the team dial reasoning depth per request. Which approach is correct?
anti_pattern: Carrying forward a deprecated thinking-control mechanism (fixed budget_tokens) when migrating to a current model, instead of switching to the adaptive thinking type and the effort knob. The trap is that budget_tokens "feels" like the right migration of a thinking config, but it is removed and now returns a hard 400 on current-generation models.
choice_a: Remove the fixed budget_tokens field entirely and set thinking to {type:"adaptive"}, then control per-request reasoning depth by setting effort (low|medium|high|xhigh|max) in output_config — raising effort for larger diffs.
rationale_a: On current-generation models, fixed budget_tokens is removed and returns a 400, so the migration requires switching to thinking:{type:"adaptive"}; per-request reasoning depth is then controlled by the effort parameter, which lives in output_config and scales thinking/tool-calls/cost.
choice_b: Remove budget_tokens and set thinking to {type:"adaptive"}, then vary reasoning depth per request by adding the effort field (low|medium|high|xhigh|max) inside the thinking block alongside the type, raising it for larger diffs.
rationale_b: The migration move is right — drop the removed budget_tokens, switch to adaptive thinking, and use effort to scale depth — but effort is not a property of the thinking block; it is set in output_config (the same place as the structured-output format). Nesting effort inside thinking is the single objective flaw: the depth control will not take effect from there, so this misses the requirement to dial reasoning per request.
choice_c: No thinking-configuration change is needed: keep budget_tokens:8000 as-is, because thinking parameters are model-agnostic and the new model will simply honor the existing budget.
rationale_c: This is wrong because thinking controls are not model-agnostic across versions — fixed budget_tokens was deprecated on the previous model generation and removed (400) on the current one, so leaving it unchanged breaks the migrated requests.
choice_d: Drop the thinking block entirely and instead prefill the assistant turn with a "Let me reason step by step:" opener to force deep reasoning, varying that prefill text per diff size.
rationale_d: This is wrong on two counts: assistant-turn prefill is removed on current-generation models and returns a 400, and prefill is not the mechanism for controlling reasoning depth — adaptive thinking plus the effort parameter is.
guide_section: Appendix — Out of scope for the exam (drill-only)

stem: You are the architect for "LedgerLift," a batch pipeline at a fintech firm that extracts 18 fixed fields (date, amount, merchant, category, etc.) from roughly 240,000 scanned receipt PDFs per night. The schema is rigid and validated downstream, the documents are visually simple, and an early pilot on the large, most capable model tier at the highest available effort setting hit the per-field accuracy target but blew through the nightly token budget by 3.4x and missed the processing window. Your platform lead wants the cheapest configuration that still meets the accuracy bar, and notes that the team can fan work out to a subagent for any sub-step that genuinely needs heavier reasoning. The schema is enforced via a strict tool definition, so malformed output is already structurally prevented. Which configuration change best fits the platform's public guidance for this high-volume, low-complexity extraction workload?
anti_pattern: Specifying the reasoning-effort tradeoff through a deprecated/removed control surface (fixed budget_tokens) instead of the current `effort` field, and/or defaulting to the most expensive effort tier on simple, high-volume work where public guidance says to downgrade for cost.
choice_a: Keep the large-tier model at the highest effort tier and instead raise throughput by setting `disable_parallel_tool_use:true` so each receipt resolves in a single tool call, cutting the token cost per document.
rationale_a: `disable_parallel_tool_use` only caps how many tools run per response; it does not reduce reasoning/effort tokens, so it leaves the highest-effort cost driver untouched and does not address the budget overage.
choice_b: Move the bulk extraction to a faster, cheaper model (e.g., a balanced mid-tier or small fast-tier model) and/or set a LOW `effort` value in `output_config`, since the task is simple and high-volume; reserve a higher-effort large-tier subagent only for the rare receipts that fail validation and need heavier reasoning.
rationale_b: Public guidance is to default to the most capable model but downgrade for speed/cost on simple, high-volume work, and to use a cheaper model or lower effort for the bulk while delegating only genuinely hard sub-steps to a higher-capability subagent — exactly matching a rigid-schema, visually-simple, 240K-doc nightly batch.
choice_c: Keep the large-tier model for the bulk run but dial the reasoning down by setting a small fixed `budget_tokens` for thinking, lowering it until accuracy starts to drop, so you spend the minimum reasoning needed per receipt.
rationale_c: The instinct to reduce reasoning for a simple task is right, but the mechanism is wrong: fixed `budget_tokens` is deprecated on the previous model generation and REMOVED on the current one (it returns a 400), so you must control reasoning via the `effort` field in `output_config`, not a thinking token budget.
choice_d: Keep the large-tier model at the highest effort tier but enable prompt caching on the stable system prompt and tool definitions; the cache reads at ~0.1x will recover the 3.4x overage without changing the model or effort.
rationale_d: Caching only discounts repeated prefix tokens (system + tools), not the per-receipt thinking tokens that the highest effort tier generates, so it cannot offset reasoning-driven cost on a workload whose expense is the variable extraction output, not a shared prefix.
guide_section: Appendix — Out of scope for the exam (drill-only)

stem: At Larchmont Dynamics, a Claude Code subagent named "patchbot" handles routine dependency-bump PRs across 40+ internal repos. Months ago, after patchbot once merged without running the secret-scanner, the platform team added a line to its CLAUDE.md: "CRITICAL: YOU MUST ALWAYS run the `scan_secrets` tool before EVERY tool call, no exceptions." After upgrading patchbot to the newest large-tier model, telemetry shows `scan_secrets` is now being invoked roughly 3x more often than before — it fires before read-only `git_status` and `list_files` calls where it is pointless, inflating latency and token cost, yet the team still cannot prove it runs on the one path that matters (the pre-merge step), because the instruction is still occasionally skipped under long contexts. The platform lead wants both problems fixed: stop the over-triggering AND guarantee the scan actually gates merges. Which change best satisfies both goals?
anti_pattern: Treating an escalating imperative in the prompt ("CRITICAL: YOU MUST ALWAYS...") as a reliable enforcement mechanism for a hard requirement. A prompt instruction is advisory and can be skipped by the model; on current models such all-caps coercion also overtriggers — the model invokes the gated tool defensively even when it is irrelevant. The architecturally correct move is to make the requirement deterministic (a hook) and dial the prompt back to plain descriptive guidance.
choice_a: Soften the CLAUDE.md line to plain descriptive guidance to stop the over-triggering, and add a PostToolUse hook on the merge tool that runs the secret scan and raises a blocking error if it finds credentials — so enforcement is deterministic rather than left to the prompt, and the scan is guaranteed to run on the merge path.
rationale_a: This correctly diagnoses both halves — soften the prompt to cure over-triggering, and move enforcement into a deterministic hook rather than relying on advisory instructions. The single objective flaw is the lifecycle event: a PostToolUse hook runs AFTER the tool has already executed, so by the time it scans, the merge has already happened — it can detect a leaked credential but cannot prevent the merge it was supposed to gate. To actually block the merge the hook must be PreToolUse (runs before the tool, can deny it). One subtle, checkable mechanism error turns a real gate into an after-the-fact alarm.
choice_b: Remove the "CRITICAL: YOU MUST ALWAYS..." line and replace it with plain descriptive guidance ("scan_secrets checks staged changes for credentials; run it before a merge"), then add a PreToolUse hook that programmatically runs the secret scan and blocks the merge tool if the scan has not passed. The hook makes the pre-merge requirement deterministic, while the softened prompt stops the defensive over-triggering on irrelevant read-only calls.
rationale_b: A prompt instruction is advisory and can be skipped by the model, so a hard "must gate the merge" requirement belongs in a hook, which enforces the rule deterministically regardless of context length; a PreToolUse hook fires before the merge tool runs, so it can run the scan and block the call if it fails. Dialing the language back to neutral description removes the all-caps coercion that was causing the model to over-invoke the tool defensively. This fixes both the reliability gap and the over-triggering with one architecturally sound change.
choice_c: Leave the CLAUDE.md instruction exactly as written but lower the subagent's effort setting from high to low, since reduced thinking will make the model invoke fewer tools overall and bring the scan_secrets call count back down.
rationale_c: Effort controls how much thinking and tool-calling the model does in general; it does not target the specific over-triggering caused by the coercive all-caps instruction, and lowering it could reduce calls on the path that actually matters while still leaving enforcement advisory. This addresses neither goal correctly and risks degrading the task.
choice_d: Set the subagent's tool_choice to {type:"tool", name:"scan_secrets"} so the model is forced to call the scanner, which both guarantees it runs and standardizes the behavior across all 40 repos.
rationale_d: Forcing tool_choice to a single named tool would make the model call scan_secrets on every single turn — worsening, not fixing, the over-triggering — and it still does not deterministically block the merge tool, so the gate is not actually enforced. It misreads tool_choice as an enforcement gate rather than a per-turn selection constraint.
guide_section: D1 §1.5 — Apply Agent SDK hooks for tool call interception and data normalization

stem: You operate a customer-support assistant for a SaaS billing product on the Messages API. The agent's system prompt is a stable ~6,000-token block of policies and tool descriptions, frozen at the front of the prefix and marked with cache_control:{type:"ephemeral"}; in a typical 12-turn session you see usage.cache_read_input_tokens hitting on nearly every turn after the first. Midway through one live chat (turn 7 of an ongoing conversation), a supervisor decides that for the REMAINDER of this conversation only, the agent must stop offering refunds and instead escalate every refund request to a human. You must apply this transient, per-conversation operator instruction to the rest of the session while keeping the cache savings on the frozen system prompt intact. The tool set is unchanged and you stay on the same model. Which approach best satisfies this?
anti_pattern: Treating the top-level system prompt as the place to inject a transient, mid-conversation operator instruction. Because prompt caching is a prefix match, editing any byte of the cached system prefix invalidates the entire cache after that point, forcing an expensive cache-write on the next call — when the same effect could be achieved by appending the instruction as a new message at the end (the volatile tail) where it leaves the cached prefix byte-identical.
choice_a: Edit the system prompt to add the "escalate all refunds" rule, then move the cache_control:{type:"ephemeral"} breakpoint to sit immediately AFTER the newly added text so the updated, longer prefix is cached from the next turn onward.
rationale_a: The objective flaw is a single cache mechanic: a cache_control breakpoint can only produce a cache_read hit if the bytes BEFORE it match a previously cached prefix. Editing the system text changes those bytes, so the very next call is a guaranteed cache miss and a full cache-write of the new ~6,000-token prefix — repositioning the breakpoint preserves caching only for turns AFTER that write, not the savings you were trying to keep. The transient rule should go in the volatile message tail instead, where the frozen prefix is untouched.
choice_b: Switch this conversation to the small, fast model tier for the remaining turns so the cheaper per-token rate offsets having to re-process the refund policy each time.
rationale_b: Caches are model-specific, so switching models mid-session invalidates the whole cache and forces re-processing; it also ignores WHERE the operator instruction belongs. It trades cache savings away rather than preserving them, the opposite of the requirement.
choice_c: Add the instruction as a new tool definition describing a "refund_escalation" rule, since tools are rendered first in the prefix and the model will read it before the system prompt.
rationale_c: A behavioral policy is not a tool, and tools are the first segment of the cache prefix — changing the tool definitions invalidates the entire cache after that point. This both misuses the tool mechanism and destroys exactly the cache savings you were told to protect.
choice_d: Leave the cached system prompt byte-for-byte unchanged and append the new operator instruction as a regular message at the end of the existing conversation, so the stable system prefix still matches and keeps producing cache_read hits.
rationale_d: Prompt caching is a prefix match rendered in the order tools→system→messages. Appending volatile, per-conversation content to the message tail leaves the entire tools+system prefix byte-identical, so the cached prefix still matches and cache_read continues. Transient operator instructions belong in the volatile tail, not the frozen prefix.
guide_section: Appendix — Out of scope for the exam (drill-only)

stem: MeridianClaims, a property-insurance back office, is building a Claude-powered pipeline that extracts ~40 structured fields (policy number, loss date, claimed amount, adjuster notes) from inbound claim packets, where each packet is 30-80 pages of scanned correspondence and policy schedules. Compliance requires that every extracted field be traceable to the exact span of source text it came from, so that an auditor can later verify the extraction without re-reading the whole packet, and the team has seen Claude occasionally paraphrase or fabricate a quoted figure when asked to "show your sources" in prose. The architect wants Claude to return, alongside each extracted value, a machine-readable reference to the specific source location in the supplied documents, using the platform's first-party Citations capability rather than a home-grown quote-matching layer. The packets are passed as document content blocks in the Messages API, and the extraction prompt and JSON schema are otherwise already working. Which approach correctly turns on source-anchored citations for this extraction?
anti_pattern: Treating a deterministic, API-level configuration (enabling the Citations feature on the document content block) as something achievable through a natural-language system-prompt instruction or a misplaced top-level flag. Citations grounding is a structural feature of the document block; asking the model in prose to "cite sources" is advisory and does not produce the verifiable cited_text spans the architecture requires.
choice_a: Set "citations": {"enabled": true} on each document content block that is passed in the messages, so that Claude returns cited_text spans referencing the exact locations in those documents alongside its extracted output.
rationale_a: The platform's Citations feature is enabled per-document by adding the citations.enabled flag to the document content block; with it on, Claude emits machine-readable citation references (cited_text plus source locations) grounded in the supplied documents, which is exactly the verifiable traceability the auditors require.
choice_b: Define a tool named cite_source with a strict input schema and use tool_choice to force Claude to call it for each field, passing the page number and quoted text as tool inputs that the harness records.
rationale_b: Forcing a tool call still relies on the model to hand-author the quoted text and page, so it reintroduces the same fabrication risk and reimplements a home-grown quote layer -- the opposite of using the first-party Citations capability that returns grounded cited_text directly.
choice_c: Add a top-level "citations": {"enabled": true} parameter to the Messages API request body (a sibling of model and messages) and keep the document blocks as-is, so the setting applies globally to every document in the packet.
rationale_c: The objective flaw: citations.enabled belongs on each document content block, not at the top level of the request body. There is no top-level citations field, so this configuration does not turn the feature on, Claude returns no cited_text spans, and the compliance traceability requirement is unmet.
choice_d: Keep the document blocks unchanged and append a strict instruction to the system prompt -- "For every field you extract, you MUST quote the exact source sentence verbatim and never paraphrase" -- relying on the model to self-report its sources.
rationale_d: A natural-language prompt instruction is advisory and is precisely what already produced paraphrased and fabricated quotes; it yields no machine-readable, verifiable source spans, so it cannot satisfy auditable traceability the way the structural Citations feature does.
guide_section: Appendix — Out of scope for the exam (drill-only)

stem: Meridian Claims is building a batch pipeline that reads 240,000 scanned insurance forms per night and extracts a fixed record per form: a policy_id string, a coverage_tier enum, a claim_amount number, and a list of named line_items (each with a label and an amount). They call the large, most capable model tier with structured output so every record is machine-parseable on the first pass. An architect drafts a JSON schema and wants it to (a) reliably constrain the model to exactly these fields with no extras, (b) guarantee none of the four top-level fields are silently omitted, and (c) model the nested line_items, while also clamping claim_amount to the range 0-10,000,000 directly in the schema so out-of-range OCR misreads (e.g. a 12,000,000 claim) are rejected before they reach downstream systems. The team is debating how to express the schema so the platform's structured output actually enforces the shape rather than treating parts of it as decorative. Which schema-authoring approach is correct?
anti_pattern: Authoring a structured-output JSON schema that leans on JSON Schema validation keywords (numeric min/max bounds, recursive `$ref` self-references) and forgets the two constraints the platform's structured output actually enforces — `additionalProperties:false` plus a complete `required` list — assuming a richer-looking schema yields stronger guarantees when in practice the unsupported keywords are ignored and the missing required pieces let the model emit partial objects.
choice_a: Set `strict: true` on the tool definition, include `additionalProperties: false` and a complete `required` array on every object, and model line_items as a bounded array of label/amount objects rather than a self-referential recursive type; enforce the 0-10,000,000 claim_amount bound in application code after parsing, since numeric min/max constraints are not enforced by structured output.
rationale_a: The platform's structured output reliably enforces object shape via `additionalProperties:false` and a complete `required` list, with `strict:true` placed on the tool definition (sibling of name/description/input_schema). It does NOT enforce unsupported validation keywords like numeric min/max ranges or recursive `$ref`, so value bounds belong in post-parse application validation and nesting should be expressed as a concrete bounded structure the model can satisfy.
choice_b: Place `strict: true` inside the `tool_choice` object and omit `additionalProperties` and `required` from the schema, relying on `strict` mode alone to forbid extra fields and require all four top-level fields, then use a recursive `$ref` self-reference for line_items to model arbitrary nesting depth.
rationale_b: `strict` belongs on the tool definition (sibling of name/description/input_schema), not on `tool_choice`, and it works together with `additionalProperties:false` plus an explicit `required` list rather than replacing them — omitting those removes the very constraints that enforce shape. Recursive `$ref` self-references are also an unsupported constraint, so the nesting would not be enforced.
choice_c: Skip structured output entirely; instead prefill the assistant turn with an opening `{` and instruct the model in the system prompt to fill in the four fields, clamp claim_amount, and nest line_items, then parse whatever JSON comes back.
rationale_c: Assistant-turn prefill is removed on the current model generation and returns a 400 error, so this approach cannot even run; the supported mechanism for guaranteed-parseable output is structured output via `output_config.format` / a strict tool schema, and value clamping still has to happen in code.
choice_d: Set `strict: true` on the tool definition, add `additionalProperties: false` and a complete `required` array to every object, express line_items as a flat bounded array of label/amount objects, and add `"minimum": 0, "maximum": 10000000` to claim_amount so the schema itself rejects out-of-range values during generation.
rationale_d: Everything here is correct except the single load-bearing detail: relying on `minimum`/`maximum` to reject values during generation. Numeric min/max are unsupported keywords that structured output silently ignores rather than enforces, so the bound is decorative — a 12,000,000 OCR misread would still parse cleanly and flow downstream. The exact same `strict`/`additionalProperties`/`required` shape is right; the range must be enforced in post-parse code, not the schema.
guide_section: D4 §4.3 — Enforce structured output using tool use and JSON schemas

stem: You are architecting a customer-support assistant on the Messages API for a SaaS company called Larkfield. Each support conversation runs 30-60 turns and reuses the same 6,000-token system prompt (policies, tone, and a fixed 11-tool definition list). Because the Messages API is stateless, your backend resends the entire transcript on every turn, and your finance team is alarmed that per-conversation token cost climbs steeply as transcripts grow. An engineer proposes enabling prompt caching to cut the cost of re-sending the unchanging early content. The team needs the request assembled so that, turn after turn, the large stable portion is served from cache while the newest user message is processed fresh. Which approach correctly reduces the re-sent-history cost?
anti_pattern: Treating the stateless Messages API as if cost grows unavoidably with conversation length, and "fixing" it by mutating the front of the request (refreshing a timestamp, re-summarizing and prepending, re-sorting tools) — which silently invalidates the prompt-cache prefix so every turn pays full write/uncached input price instead of the ~0.1x cache-read price.
choice_a: Move the conversation to the Message Batches API, which is 50% cheaper, and submit each turn as a batch request keyed by custom_id so the cumulative transcript cost is halved across the conversation.
rationale_a: Message Batches are an asynchronous bulk-processing mechanism (results returned in any order, may take up to 24h) for independent requests, not interactive multi-turn chat, and they do not address re-sent-history cost the way prefix caching does.
choice_b: Stop resending the transcript entirely: send only the newest user message each turn and rely on the Messages API to retain the prior turns server-side from the previous request, since the model already saw them. Add cache_control to that single message to persist the conversation state between calls.
rationale_b: This is wrong because the Messages API is stateless and retains nothing between requests — omitting the transcript loses all prior context, and cache_control is a cost optimization for re-sent content, not a server-side conversation-state store.
choice_c: Keep the request assembled in the order tools -> system -> messages with the stable content first: place a cache_control breakpoint (type:"ephemeral") at the end of the system prompt (and optionally after the last stable early turn), and append each new user turn at the END of the messages array. Leave the tool definitions and system prompt byte-identical every turn so the cached prefix matches, and confirm it is working by checking that usage.cache_read_input_tokens is non-zero (cache reads bill at roughly 0.1x input).
rationale_c: The Messages API is stateless so the full history must be resent each turn, but prompt caching does a prefix match: keeping the tools+system+early-turn prefix byte-identical and appending new turns at the end lets the stable prefix be read from cache at ~0.1x, and usage.cache_read_input_tokens verifies the hit. Tools render before system before messages, so the most stable content sits at the front of the cacheable prefix.
choice_d: Assemble the request as tools -> system -> messages and place a cache_control breakpoint (type:"ephemeral") at the end of the system prompt, appending each new user turn at the end of the messages array. To keep the model aware of the current time, prepend a freshly generated "Conversation context as of {datetime.now()}:" line to the very top of the system prompt on every turn.
rationale_d: This gets the order, the breakpoint placement, and the append-at-end mechanics exactly right, which is why it tempts. The single objective flaw: prompt caching is a prefix match, so injecting a changing datetime.now() string at the TOP of the system prompt alters a prefix byte and invalidates the entire cache from that point onward — every turn then pays uncached/write price, defeating the savings. Volatile content (timestamps, per-request IDs) must go LAST, after the stable cached prefix, never first.
guide_section: Appendix — Out of scope for the exam (drill-only)

stem: At Lumen Archival, you are architecting a batch document-extraction pipeline on the balanced mid-tier model that pulls structured fields from scanned insurance contracts. Each job stuffs a long, varying set of OCR'd pages plus a fixed 9-page extraction rubric into a single prompt, and the team needs a pre-flight check that rejects any assembled prompt before it would overflow the model's context window. A junior engineer has already shipped a guardrail that estimates prompt size by running the assembled text through another provider's `tiktoken` library (cl100k_base) and adding a 5% safety margin, reasoning that "a token is a token" and that the local library avoids a network round-trip per job. In a recent run, 14 of 320 jobs that the guardrail had marked "well under the limit" were nonetheless rejected by the API for exceeding the context window, and the lead suspects the local size estimates are systematically too low. Which approach should the architect adopt for the pre-flight size check so it is accurate for this model?
anti_pattern: Using a foreign tokenizer (tiktoken) as a proxy for Claude's token count, or trusting a fixed character-to-token ratio, to gate prompt assembly against a context window. Both produce systematically wrong counts for Claude because tiktoken is another provider's BPE tokenizer and undercounts Claude tokens, and char-ratios ignore model-specific tokenization, so the guardrail silently lets oversized prompts through.
choice_a: Replace the tiktoken estimate with the platform's `count_tokens` endpoint, called with the same model id and the exact messages/system/tools payload the job will send, and gate on the returned count against the model's context window.
rationale_a: Token counts are model-specific, so the authoritative measure of a Claude prompt's size is the platform's `count_tokens` endpoint invoked with the same model id and the same messages/system/tools payload that will actually be sent; tiktoken is another provider's tokenizer and does not reflect Claude's tokenization, so only the model-specific count can correctly gate against the context window.
choice_b: Estimate size with a fixed heuristic of 4 characters per token over the assembled string, since that ratio is close enough for English prose and needs no library or API call.
rationale_b: A fixed character-per-token ratio ignores model-specific tokenization and varies widely with content (OCR artifacts, JSON, punctuation), so it is not an authoritative size measure; the only reliable count for a Claude prompt comes from the model-specific `count_tokens` endpoint called with the actual payload.
choice_c: Drop the pre-flight size check entirely and instead inspect `stop_reason` on each response: if it returns `max_tokens`, automatically resend the job with the input split into two prompts.
rationale_c: This is reactive rather than a pre-flight guard, and it misreads the signal: `max_tokens` indicates the generated output was truncated against the output cap, not that the input overflowed the context window, so it would not detect or prevent the oversized-input condition the team needs to catch before sending.
choice_d: Keep the local tiktoken (cl100k_base) estimate to avoid the per-job network call, but raise the safety margin from 5% to a larger fixed buffer so the systematic undercount is absorbed before the prompt is sent.
rationale_d: The objective flaw is that it still relies on tiktoken, another provider's tokenizer, which undercounts Claude tokens; a larger fixed margin cannot correct a model-mismatched tokenizer because the undercount is not a constant offset but varies with content, so the estimate remains unauthoritative for Claude regardless of buffer size.
guide_section: Appendix — Out of scope for the exam (drill-only)

stem: At Meridian Claims, an invoice-extraction agent runs the balanced mid-tier model in an agent loop. On each turn the model emits a tool_use block named `record_line_item` whose input contains fields like `{"sku": "...", "qty": <int>, "unit_price": <float>, "memo": "..."}`. The original Python harness pulled values out of each tool call by running regex over the raw serialized input string (e.g. searching for `"qty":\s*(\d+)`), and for months it worked across ~40,000 invoices. After the team enabled prompt caching and bumped `effort` to `high`, on-call engineers noticed that roughly 3% of line items now record `qty` as null or capture the wrong `unit_price`, and a handful of `memo` fields that contain escaped quotes or non-ASCII vendor names are silently truncated. The harness already correctly loops while `stop_reason == "tool_use"` and returns one `tool_result` per `tool_use_id`. What is the correct architectural fix for the value-extraction step?
anti_pattern: Treating the model's serialized tool_use.input as an opaque string and reading arguments out of it via substring/regex matching, rather than parsing it as a JSON object and reading typed fields. The serialization is non-canonical (key order, whitespace, escaping, unicode, and numeric formatting can all vary), so string matching is brittle and silently misreads or misses values.
choice_a: Keep parsing each tool_use block's `input` as JSON, but additionally set `disable_parallel_tool_use: true` so the model emits exactly one tool call per turn, which is what eliminates the misread `qty` and `unit_price` values.
rationale_a: Parsing the input as JSON is correct and is the real fix, but the stated cause is wrong: the misreads come from string-matching a non-canonical serialization, not from parallel tool calls. disable_parallel_tool_use only caps the number of tool_use blocks per turn; it does nothing to make field extraction reliable, and even a single tool call would still be misread by regex when keys reorder or values contain escaped quotes/unicode. The objective flaw is attributing the fix to disable_parallel_tool_use rather than to JSON parsing.
choice_b: Lower `effort` back to `medium` and disable prompt caching, since enabling caching and raising effort is what changed the serialization and introduced the misreads.
rationale_b: Prompt caching is a prefix-reuse cost optimization and effort controls thinking/tool-call depth; neither defines or stabilizes the byte-level serialization of tool_use.input, so reverting them is a superstitious fix. The regex extraction was always fragile and would eventually misread values regardless of caching or effort settings.
choice_c: Add `strict: true` and `additionalProperties: false` to `tool_choice` so the model is forced to emit canonically-serialized JSON with stable key ordering, then continue using the existing regex extraction unchanged.
rationale_c: strict goes on the tool definition (sibling of name/description/input_schema), not on tool_choice, and strict mode constrains which fields the model emits — it does not guarantee a canonical serialization that regex can rely on. Keeping regex over the serialized form preserves the original anti-pattern, so escaped quotes and non-ASCII memos would still be truncated.
choice_d: Stop regex-matching the serialized input. Read each tool_use block's `input` as a parsed JSON object (e.g. via `json.loads`/the SDK's already-deserialized `input` dict) and access typed fields like `input["qty"]` and `input["unit_price"]` directly.
rationale_d: tool_use.input is a JSON object whose serialized form is non-canonical — key order, whitespace, numeric formatting, escaped quotes, and non-ASCII characters all vary — so the only reliable contract is to parse it as JSON and read typed fields, never to string-match the serialized text. Public guidance is explicit: parse tool inputs with json.loads/JSON.parse and never raw-string-match the serialized input.
guide_section: D4 §4.3 — Enforce structured output using tool use and JSON schemas

stem: At Meridian Ledger, an invoice-processing agent runs on a balanced mid-tier model and has access to a `lookup_vendor_tax_id` tool backed by an internal vendor database. The agent processes ~3,400 incoming invoices per week. On roughly 22% of invoices where the vendor's tax ID field is missing or malformed, the agent simply transcribes the blank or garbled value into the output instead of calling `lookup_vendor_tax_id` to resolve it. When it DOES call the tool, the result is correct ~99% of the time, so the tool itself works; the problem is that the model often does not decide to call it. The current tool description reads, in full: "Looks up a vendor tax ID." The team wants to raise the rate at which the agent correctly calls the tool on missing/malformed IDs, with the smallest, most robust change. Which approach best fits how Claude selects tools?
anti_pattern: Reaching for a deterministic harness-enforcement mechanism (a hook that force-injects a tool call) to fix what is really a tool-selection problem. The model is not reliably DECIDING to call the tool, so the right lever is making the tool's own description prescriptive about when to call it; jumping to a hook conflates 'help the model choose the tool' with 'remove the model's choice entirely', which is heavier and brittle.
choice_a: Add a Claude Code hook that intercepts the agent's output and, whenever the tax ID field is missing or malformed, deterministically force-injects a `lookup_vendor_tax_id` call before the response is finalized.
rationale_a: Claude Code hooks gate, block, or add feedback around tool calls the model has ALREADY decided to make (e.g. a PreToolUse hook fires on a call the model initiated); they cannot synthesize a tool call the model never chose. The stated problem is a should-call-rate gap — the model failing to decide to call `lookup_vendor_tax_id` — and the documented fix is a clearer, prescriptive tool description telling the model when to use it. A hook cannot manufacture the missing decision, so it solves a different problem (force a call) than the one stated (help the model decide).
choice_b: Lower the model's effort setting in output_config so it spends fewer tokens deliberating, since faster responses will make the agent more likely to reach for the tool.
rationale_b: Effort controls how much thinking/tool-calling budget the model spends; lowering it reduces deliberation and would, if anything, make the model LESS likely to recognize the missing-ID condition and call the tool. It does not change the tool's selection signal and is the wrong direction.
choice_c: Rewrite the `lookup_vendor_tax_id` tool description to be prescriptive about WHEN to call it — e.g. state that it must be called whenever a vendor tax ID is absent, empty, or fails the expected format, describe the inputs/outputs, and note it should not be called when a valid ID is already present.
rationale_c: Tool selection is driven primarily by the tool's name and description; a terse description like 'Looks up a vendor tax ID' gives the model no signal about the triggering condition. Making the description prescriptive about when (and when not) to invoke the tool is the documented, lowest-cost lever to raise the should-call rate, and it keeps the model in charge of the decision so valid IDs are left untouched.
choice_d: Switch the agent from the mid-tier model to the small, fast tier so the cheaper, faster model processes more invoices, which will incidentally improve tool-calling accuracy.
rationale_d: Downgrading to a smaller, faster model is a speed/cost move for simple high-volume work; it does not address the tool description that is causing the under-calling, and a less capable model is generally not more reliable at deciding when to invoke a tool. Throughput is unrelated to the should-call-rate problem.
guide_section: D2 §2.1 — Design effective tool interfaces with clear descriptions and boundaries

stem: You are architecting a long-running multi-agent research orchestrator named "Atlas" that investigates regulatory filings. A lead agent runs a single session that can span 40+ turns; the bulk of its context growth comes from a `fetch_filing` tool that returns 30–80KB of raw document text per call, and the lead typically issues 60+ such calls before it begins synthesizing. The lead's own chain of reasoning across turns — which sub-questions it has resolved, which leads it has discarded, and why — is load-bearing for the final synthesis and must stay intact. The team has observed that the session reliably approaches the context window only because of accumulated tool output, not because the reasoning narrative itself is long. They are deciding between proactively compacting the history and using context editing, and want the mechanism whose loss profile best matches where the bloat actually lives.
anti_pattern: Conflating the two distinct context-management mechanisms — compaction (lossy summarization of the running conversation history) versus context editing (clearing stale tool-result/thinking blocks while preserving the reasoning narrative) — by applying the wrong one to the situation's actual cost driver, or by treating the cleared content as a free, reversible operation when it is not.
choice_a: Use context editing to clear the old `fetch_filing` tool-result blocks, since this is fully reversible — the cleared raw document text can be transparently re-expanded on demand later in the same session if the synthesis step turns out to need it.
rationale_a: The mechanism choice (context editing on stale tool results) is correct and well-matched to where the bloat lives, but the stated justification is false: clearing a tool-result block removes that content from the context — it is not a transparent, on-demand reversible expansion within the session. If the synthesis later needs that filing's text, the agent must re-fetch it, not un-clear it. The only flaw is the invented reversibility behavior.
choice_b: Use context editing to clear the old `fetch_filing` tool-result blocks once they have been consumed, because the bloat is concentrated in stale tool output and this preserves the lead's intact turn-by-turn reasoning narrative that the synthesis depends on.
rationale_b: Context editing removes specific stale tool-result (and thinking) blocks — exactly the accumulated raw-document bloat identified here — while leaving the conversational reasoning trail untouched, which matches the requirement that the load-bearing narrative survive. It targets the actual cost driver (tool output) rather than lossily summarizing everything.
choice_c: Do nothing proactively and rely on the harness's reactive `/compact` to trigger automatically when the window fills, since that defers any lossy operation until it is strictly necessary.
rationale_c: Reactive compaction is itself lossy and is not a substitute for proactively isolating the verbose work; waiting until the window fills still applies a lossy summary to the load-bearing narrative rather than targeting the actual bloat in the stale tool output.
choice_d: Proactively compact the session history into a summary, because compaction reduces total tokens and a summary will faithfully retain the lead's full turn-by-turn reasoning narrative while also discarding the raw document text.
rationale_d: Compaction is lossy summarization of the running history, so it cannot be relied on to faithfully retain the full load-bearing reasoning narrative — it is the wrong fit precisely because the requirement is to keep that narrative intact, and it also collapses more than just the stale tool output.
guide_section: Appendix — Out of scope for the exam (drill-only)

stem: Helios Research runs a multi-agent literature-review system on a large, frontier-tier model. Every agent request reuses the same ~180K-token block: a frozen 9-page system prompt plus a sorted, deterministic dump of 40 source documents, followed by the agent's varying sub-question. To cut cost they add a single cache_control:{type:"ephemeral"} breakpoint at the end of the document dump. After deploying, finance reports the bill barely moved and usage.cache_read_input_tokens is 0 on nearly every call, while cache_creation tokens stay high. An engineer discovers the system prompt is assembled with an f-string that interpolates datetime.now().isoformat() ("retrieval performed at …") and a fresh uuid4() request trace ID near the top, both ahead of the breakpoint. Which change will actually restore cache hits and the ~10x read discount without losing per-request traceability?
anti_pattern: Placing volatile content (a per-request timestamp, request ID, or unsorted serialization) inside the cached prefix, which changes a byte in the prefix on every request and silently invalidates the entire cache after it — so the team pays the write premium repeatedly and never gets the 0.1x read discount, even though a cache breakpoint is present.
choice_a: Remove the timestamp and UUID from the cached prefix entirely: keep the frozen system prompt and the sorted document dump (which are byte-identical across requests) before the breakpoint, and move the per-request timestamp and trace ID into the volatile user message after the breakpoint. Confirm the fix by checking that usage.cache_read_input_tokens is non-zero on the second and later requests.
rationale_a: Prompt caching is a prefix match: any byte change before a breakpoint invalidates everything after it, so a datetime.now()/uuid4() in the prefix breaks the cache every request — the documented silent invalidators. Moving volatile content into the suffix keeps the prefix byte-identical, restoring the ~0.1x read cost, and cache_read_input_tokens is the documented way to verify hits.
choice_b: Lower the request effort to "low" and enable disable_parallel_tool_use so fewer tokens are processed per agent turn, which will bring the cached-context cost back down.
rationale_b: Effort and parallel-tool settings affect thinking/tool behavior, not cache prefix matching; they neither make the volatile prefix stable nor produce cache reads, so the 0.1x discount stays unattainable.
choice_c: Keep the timestamp and trace ID where they are at the top of the prefix, but add a second cache_control breakpoint immediately after them so the large document dump sits between two breakpoints and is cached independently of the changing header.
rationale_c: The objective flaw: caching is a strict prefix match, so a breakpoint only caches the contiguous prefix up to it — content after a changed byte cannot be reused regardless of how many later breakpoints you add (you may declare up to 4). Because the timestamp/UUID change every request, the prefix is invalidated at byte one and every downstream breakpoint (including the one before the documents) misses too.
choice_d: Leave the prefix unchanged and switch the ephemeral breakpoint to ttl:"1h" so the cached prefix survives long enough for later agent requests to read it instead of rewriting it.
rationale_d: A longer TTL only extends how long a valid cached prefix lives; it does nothing when the prefix bytes change every request, because each new timestamp/UUID writes a brand-new cache entry that the next differing request still cannot match.
guide_section: Appendix — Out of scope for the exam (drill-only)

stem: You are architecting a multi-agent research orchestrator on the Messages API. Every sub-agent request shares an identical 40,000-token prefix: a frozen system prompt plus a deterministically sorted tool list, followed by a small per-request research sub-question (~300 tokens). A teammate proposes placing a single cache_control breakpoint at the end of the shared 40,000-token prefix, and estimates the fleet will issue roughly 5,000 sub-agent requests per hour against it. During review, another engineer notices that the current system-prompt template begins with a line that injects datetime.now() and a fresh run UUID at the very top of the prompt, ahead of the frozen instructions. The team must decide whether the proposed caching design will actually pay off, and what (if anything) must change first.
anti_pattern: Reasoning about cache break-even purely from request volume while ignoring that a volatile prefix (a per-request timestamp or run ID placed before the cached content) invalidates the cached prefix on every request, forcing a full 1.25x write each time and never delivering the 0.1x read — so the cache is a permanent net loss no matter how many requests are sent.
choice_a: Caching is the wrong tool here because the per-request sub-question (~300 tokens) is the part that varies, and cached content must be identical across requests; since each request differs, no cache breakpoint can ever match. Drop caching and instead reduce cost by switching the sub-agents to the small, fast model tier.
rationale_a: This misunderstands prefix caching: only the cached PREFIX must be byte-identical, and the 40K shared prefix qualifies while the varying 300-token sub-question correctly sits after the breakpoint, so caching is exactly the right tool and the premise is false. Switching models also invalidates the cache entirely, so it does not address the design at all.
choice_b: Break-even depends on the requests/hour rate: with 5,000 requests/hour against a 5-minute TTL, you must issue at least 5,000 reads within each 5-minute window before the write is amortized, otherwise the cache expires unused. Since the window is too short for that volume, switch the breakpoint to a 1-hour TTL (~2x write) to guarantee amortization.
rationale_b: This invents a per-window volume threshold that does not exist — break-even is per-reuse (each hit saves ~0.9x), not a count of reads within the TTL window — and moving to a 1-hour TTL raises the write to ~2x without fixing the real blocker, the volatile leading prefix.
choice_c: Correct that the leading datetime.now()/UUID must move below the breakpoint first — a prefix match means that volatile byte invalidates the whole cached suffix on every request. Once the 40K prefix is byte-stable, the design pays off, and the math is comfortable: a 5-minute write costs ~1.25x and each read costs ~0.1x, so the ~1.25x premium paid on the write is recovered after about 2 cached reads (1.25 / 0.9 round up). At 5,000 requests/hour you clear that within seconds, so make the one edit and ship it.
rationale_c: This choice correctly identifies and fixes the real blocker (relocating the volatile leading prefix), which makes it tempting — but its break-even arithmetic treats the full ~1.25x write as the 'extra' paid. The extra cost versus an uncached request is only the ~0.25x premium (1.25x minus the 1x you would pay anyway), so recovery is ~0.25/0.9 — within the first reuse, not ~2 reads. The mechanism is right and the number is close, but it overstates the premium by 1x, so the stated break-even is wrong.
choice_d: The break-even is not the problem; the prefix volatility is. As written, the leading datetime.now()/UUID changes the very start of the prefix every request, and because cache matching is a prefix match, any byte change invalidates everything after it — so every request pays a full ~1.25x cache write and never gets a 0.1x read, making the cache a net loss at any volume. Move the volatile timestamp/UUID out of the cached prefix (to the end, after the breakpoint, alongside the per-request sub-question) so the 40K prefix is byte-stable. Once stable, the ~0.25x premium paid on the first write is recovered within roughly the first reuse, and every subsequent hit costs ~0.1x.
rationale_d: Cache lookup is a prefix match, so any byte change in the prefix — including a leading datetime/UUID — invalidates the entire cached suffix, forcing a 1.25x write every request and yielding zero 0.1x reads. The fix is to relocate volatile content after the breakpoint so the prefix is byte-stable; the extra paid versus an uncached request is only the ~0.25x write premium (1.25x write minus the 1x you would pay uncached), which each ~0.9x-saving read recovers within roughly the first reuse.
guide_section: Appendix — Out of scope for the exam (drill-only)

stem: Your team runs two Claude-based products that both reuse a large, byte-stable system prefix (a frozen system prompt plus a sorted tool list, roughly 38,000 tokens) cached with cache_control. Product A is "Atlas," a multi-agent research console where an analyst fires a dense burst of 12 to 20 follow-up queries against the same prefix within about 90 seconds, then goes idle for 30 to 60 minutes before the next burst. Product B is "Beacon," a continuously-staffed triage desk where agents hit the same prefix every 40 to 70 seconds, all day, with the prefix never changing between calls. A teammate proposes using the 1-hour cache TTL (ttl:"1h") for both products on the theory that "a longer TTL is always cheaper because the cache survives longer." You are asked to choose the TTL for each product and justify it on cache economics. Which choice is correct?
anti_pattern: Treating the 1-hour cache TTL as strictly better than the 5-minute TTL ("longer must be cheaper"), ignoring that the 1h TTL costs more to write (2x base vs 1.25x base) and only pays off when reuse gaps actually exceed 5 minutes. The trap is matching TTL to total session length rather than to the inter-request gap, and forgetting that any byte change in the cached prefix re-invalidates and re-writes regardless of TTL.
choice_a: Use the 1-hour TTL for Beacon and the 5-minute TTL for Atlas, since Beacon runs continuously all day and therefore needs the longest-lived cache, while Atlas only runs in short 90-second bursts and so needs only the short window.
rationale_a: This inverts the correct mapping by matching TTL to total session duration instead of the inter-request gap: Beacon's sub-minute reuse never needs more than the 5-min window (and pays a needlessly higher write), while Atlas's multi-minute idle gaps are precisely what require the 1h TTL to survive between bursts.
choice_b: Use the 1-hour TTL for Atlas (its idle gaps exceed 5 minutes) and the 1-hour TTL for Beacon as well, because the 1-hour TTL strictly dominates: it survives longer and the per-read cost is identical, so it can only ever save money compared to the 5-minute TTL.
rationale_b: The Atlas half and the equal-read-cost observation are both correct, but the claim that the 1h TTL "strictly dominates" is objectively false: the 1h cache write costs roughly 2x the base input tokens versus ~1.25x for the 5-minute write, so for Beacon — whose reuse gap is already under 5 minutes and never expires the short window — paying the higher write multiplier buys nothing and is strictly more expensive. The single flaw is the false dominance claim applied to Beacon.
choice_c: Use the 5-minute TTL for both products, because Atlas's intra-burst queries all arrive within 90 seconds — comfortably inside the 5-minute window — so the cache stays hot for every product and the cheaper write is always the right call.
rationale_c: This correctly reads the intra-burst timing but ignores the 30–60 minute idle gaps between Atlas bursts: a 5-minute cache expires long before the next burst, forcing a fresh full-price write at the start of every burst, which is exactly what the 1-hour TTL is meant to prevent.
choice_d: Use the default 5-minute TTL for Beacon (its 40–70s reuse gap is well under 5 minutes, so the cheaper 1.25x write is amortized over constant reads) and the 1-hour TTL for Atlas (its 30–60 minute idle gaps exceed 5 minutes, so without the 1h TTL each burst would start with a fresh, full-price cache write); within each burst the reads stay hot regardless of TTL.
rationale_d: Cache TTL should be matched to the inter-request gap, not the session length: the 5-min TTL keeps Beacon's prefix hot at the lower ~1.25x write cost because reuse always lands inside the window, while Atlas's multi-minute idle gaps would expire a 5-min cache between bursts, so the ~2x 1h write is justified to avoid repeated full-price re-writes. Cache reads are ~0.1x base regardless of which TTL is chosen, so the only economic lever between the two TTLs is the write multiplier weighed against whether reuse lands inside the window.
guide_section: Appendix — Out of scope for the exam (drill-only)

stem: At a fintech firm, an architect is building a Claude Code workflow that audits a large monorepo: for each of 47 microservices it must grep the codebase, read dozens of config files, and run a dependency scanner whose JSON output frequently exceeds 30K tokens per service. In early runs the orchestrating session's main context fills with raw grep hits, file dumps, and scanner JSON well before the audit reaches the last few services, and the final synthesis quality visibly degrades. The team only needs the orchestrator to retain a short per-service verdict (pass/fail plus the top issues), not the raw discovery output. The architect wants the main context to stay lean from the start, while the orchestrator still drives the overall audit and produces a final report. Which approach best fits documented Claude Code guidance?
anti_pattern: Treating reactive, lossy compaction (/compact) as equivalent to proactively isolating verbose work in a subagent. /compact summarizes the main thread AFTER the noise has already entered and been billed into context, and the summarization is lossy; it does not prevent the verbose intermediate output from ever touching the main window the way a subagent's return-a-summary boundary does.
choice_a: Dispatch the per-service discovery (grep, file reads, scanner run) to a subagent per service so the verbose intermediate output lives in the subagent's isolated context, and have each subagent return only a concise per-service verdict to the orchestrator's main context.
rationale_a: Subagents isolate verbose discovery in a separate context and return a concise summary, so the raw grep/file/scanner output never enters the orchestrator's main window — proactively keeping it lean from the start, which is exactly the documented purpose of subagents.
choice_b: Keep all discovery in the single main session but raise the orchestrator's effort to xhigh and enable adaptive thinking, so the model reasons well enough to compress the raw output as it goes.
rationale_b: Effort and adaptive thinking change how much the model reasons and how many tool calls it makes; they do not remove verbose tool output from the context window, so the main context still fills with raw grep/file/scanner data.
choice_c: Let the orchestrator run all discovery inline in its own context, and invoke /compact after each service so the accumulated grep output, file dumps, and scanner JSON are summarized down before the next service begins.
rationale_c: The objective flaw: /compact is reactive and lossy — it summarizes verbose output only AFTER it has already entered (and been billed into) the main context, and the summarization loses detail, so it is explicitly not a substitute for proactively isolating the verbose work in a subagent.
choice_d: Move the entire audit to the Message Batches API, sending one request per service, since batches are 50% cheaper and most finish within an hour.
rationale_d: Message Batches address async throughput and cost, not the per-session context-window problem; each request would still ingest its own verbose output, and batches do not provide an orchestrator that retains concise per-service verdicts.
guide_section: D5 §5.4 — Manage context effectively in large codebase exploration

stem: You are designing a long-running "competitive-landscape" research agent for a 9-person strategy team at a fictional firm, Meridian Analytics. Each weekday the agent is invoked in a fresh session to research one assigned competitor, so over a quarter it runs roughly 60 separate sessions covering 60 companies. Within a single session the agent fans out many web searches, reads sources, and reasons over them; across sessions it must remember durable conclusions — for example "Competitor Cygnus shifted to usage-based pricing in Q1, confirmed by two sources" — so that next week's session about a related competitor does not re-derive the same facts and can cross-reference them. The team has wired up the memory tool. An architect proposes: "Keep each session's intermediate search results, scratch reasoning, and source snippets in the conversation context as we go, and write only the verified, self-contained durable conclusions to memory so future sessions can retrieve them." Which assessment of this design is correct?
anti_pattern: Conflating in-conversation working state (which lives in the context window and is rebuilt each turn from the message history) with durable cross-session knowledge (which must be written to an explicit memory store keyed for later retrieval).
