The traps either try to persist across sessions using something that only survives within one conversation, or pick a mechanism that records the wrong granularity of fact.
choice_a: The design is flawed: durable cross-session facts should instead be kept by raising the effort level and relying on the 1M-token context window to carry all 60 companies' conclusions forward, so no separate memory store is needed.
rationale_a: A large context window and a higher effort setting only affect work within a single session; the window is rebuilt from that session's own message history and does not carry state into a future, independently-started session, so durable cross-session facts still require an explicit persistence mechanism like the memory tool.
choice_b: The design is sound, but to guarantee continuity the agent should also write each session's full intermediate search results and source snippets to memory, since the context window is cleared between sessions and those raw materials would otherwise be lost.
rationale_b: This choice is correct that the context window is per-session and that the design is otherwise well-formed, which makes it tempting — but it commits one specific objective error: it persists the full raw intermediate working state (search results and source snippets) to memory. That material is per-session scratch state that is meant to be discarded; writing it to the durable store records the wrong granularity, bloats later retrieval, and is not what cross-session memory is for. Only the verified, self-contained conclusions should be persisted, exactly as the original proposal already specifies.
choice_c: The design is sound: the conversation context naturally holds per-session working state (search results, scratch reasoning) because it is rebuilt from the message history each turn and is discarded when the session ends, while the memory tool is the correct mechanism for the small set of verified, self-contained conclusions that must outlive the session and be retrieved by future sessions.
rationale_c: Within-conversation working state belongs in the context window (it is reconstructed each turn from the message history and does not survive the session), whereas anything that must persist across separate, independently-started sessions must be written to a durable store like the memory tool and recalled later — these are two distinct mechanisms for in-conversation vs cross-session state, and the design correctly assigns each kind of data to the right one.
choice_d: The design is flawed: prompt caching should be used to persist the verified conclusions across sessions, because the cached prefix is retained and will be available to next week's session automatically.
rationale_d: Prompt caching only reduces the cost of re-processing an identical prefix within its short TTL (5 minutes by default, up to 1 hour) and is purely a latency/cost optimization, not a durable knowledge store; it does not give a future session access to facts derived in a prior one, so it cannot serve as cross-session memory.
guide_section: D5 §5.4 — Manage context effectively in large codebase exploration

stem: At Meridian Atlas, a five-agent research orchestrator runs long literature-synthesis sessions on a frontier-tier model with adaptive thinking enabled. Each lead agent commonly emits a turn containing a thinking block, two parallel tool_use blocks, and a short text block. To keep sessions under the context limit, the team built a custom compaction layer that summarizes older turns; when it reconstructs the running message history for the next API call, it stores each prior assistant turn as a single string built from the text blocks it parsed out of the response, then appends the matching tool_result blocks. After enabling compaction, lead agents began returning 400 errors mid-session and, when a call did succeed, produced noticeably less coherent follow-up reasoning. The architecture review must pin down what the compaction layer is doing wrong and how to fix history reconstruction. Which choice correctly diagnoses the flaw and states the right fix?
anti_pattern: Reconstructing message history from the extracted natural-language text of a prior assistant turn instead of appending the entire structured response.content array (thinking blocks, tool_use blocks, and text). Discarding the structured blocks breaks the tool_use/tool_result pairing and the model's carried reasoning, producing 400 errors or degraded follow-up reasoning on resume.
choice_a: The 400 errors mean the orchestrator is terminating the loop incorrectly. Inspect the model's text for words like "done" or "finished" to decide when an agent turn is complete, and only append turns to history once that text confirms completion; this prevents half-formed turns from corrupting the reconstructed history.
rationale_a: Loop termination must be decided by inspecting stop_reason (end_turn vs tool_use), never by parsing natural-language text for "done"/"finished"; this option both misdiagnoses the cause and prescribes a known anti-pattern that has nothing to do with the history-reconstruction bug.
choice_b: The compaction layer drops the structured blocks by storing only parsed text. Fix it by appending the full response.content array (thinking and tool_use blocks included) as the assistant message — but strip the thinking blocks before re-sending, since thinking is server-side-only and including it in history returns a 400.
rationale_b: The diagnosis and the core fix (append the full response.content) are right, but the claim that thinking blocks must be stripped from history is the objective flaw: when interleaving extended/adaptive thinking with tool use, prior thinking blocks must be passed back unmodified in the assistant turn so the model can resume its reasoning — stripping them degrades the resumed reasoning, and it is not what prevents a 400 (the 400 here comes from the text-only reconstruction breaking tool_use/tool_result pairing).
choice_c: The problem is that the two parallel tool_use blocks are being answered together. Return each tool_result in its own separate user message, one per call, so the history stays unambiguous, and continue building each assistant turn from the parsed text as before.
rationale_c: This is backwards: all tool_results for parallel tool_use blocks in one assistant turn must be returned in a single user message — splitting them across messages degrades parallel tool use — and it leaves the actual bug (text-only reconstruction) unfixed.
choice_d: The compaction layer is reconstructing each assistant turn from only the parsed text, discarding the thinking and tool_use blocks the model actually emitted. When appending a prior assistant turn to the message history you must append the full response.content array (thinking, redacted_thinking, tool_use, and text blocks) as the assistant message, then return each tool_result in a following user message keyed by its tool_use_id. Preserving the complete content blocks keeps the turn structure intact so the loop resumes coherently.
rationale_d: An assistant turn is the structured response.content array, not just its text; the tool_use blocks (and thinking blocks) must be appended verbatim so the matching tool_result tool_use_ids resolve and the model's prior reasoning is preserved — extracting only the text breaks both continuity and the tool_use/tool_result pairing.
guide_section: D5 §5.1 — Manage conversation context to preserve critical information across long interactions

stem: At Larchmont Systems, a code-generation service uses the Claude Python SDK to ask a large, frontier-tier model to emit large multi-file scaffolds, setting max_tokens to 100000 to leave room for the full output. The service runs behind a corporate reverse proxy that closes any connection idle for more than 60 seconds. The team finds that long generations intermittently fail: sometimes the SDK itself raises a ValueError before the request is even sent, and other times the proxy drops the connection partway through and the client surfaces a connection error. They want a single approach that reliably delivers the complete generated output without hitting either failure, and they don't need to react to individual token events along the way. Which approach is correct?
anti_pattern: Treating an HTTP-timeout symptom as a timeout-duration problem: raising the client read timeout (or expecting the SDK's auto-scaled timeout to save you) instead of streaming. A long non-streaming generation holds one socket open with no bytes flowing until the entire response is assembled, so idle-connection killers (proxies, load balancers) terminate it regardless of how high the configured timeout is; only streaming keeps bytes flowing.
choice_a: Keep the non-streaming client.messages.create(...) call but raise the client read timeout well above the model's worst-case generation time (for example client.with_options(timeout=1800.0)), so the request has enough time to finish and the SDK no longer refuses the large-max_tokens request.
rationale_a: Raising the timeout does suppress the SDK's own ValueError guard for large max_tokens, but the objective flaw is that it does nothing about the idle connection: a non-streaming request sends no response bytes until the full generation is assembled, so the 60-second-idle proxy still closes the socket mid-generation. Only streaming keeps the connection active; a higher timeout addresses the wrong failure.
choice_b: Leave the request non-streaming and instead lower max_tokens to about 8000 so the response returns fast enough to beat the proxy's idle limit, splitting the scaffold across several smaller sequential requests.
rationale_b: This abandons the requirement to deliver the large output in one reliable approach, adds orchestration complexity, and still risks idle drops on any single chunk that takes over 60 seconds; streaming is the supported answer for large outputs and is unnecessary to fragment around.
choice_c: Keep the non-streaming call and add retry-on-timeout logic by raising max_retries, treating the proxy disconnect as a transient error the SDK will automatically recover from.
rationale_c: Retrying re-runs the same non-streaming request that idles the connection again, so it reproduces the same drop and wastes a full generation each attempt; it never keeps bytes flowing, which is the actual fix.
choice_d: Issue the request with the streaming helper (client.messages.stream(...)) and read the assembled result with stream.get_final_message() after the stream completes. Streaming begins emitting bytes as the model generates, so the connection never sits idle long enough for the proxy to close it, and the SDK does not raise its large-max_tokens guard for streaming requests.
rationale_d: The platform documentation's guidance is to default to streaming for any request with long output or high max_tokens precisely because it prevents request timeouts; the stream keeps bytes flowing so idle-connection killers don't fire, and get_final_message() returns the complete response without needing to handle per-token events.
guide_section: Appendix — Out of scope for the exam (drill-only)

stem: You maintain the orchestrator for "Lattice," a multi-agent research system on a frontier-tier model (1M-token context, 128K max output). A lead agent fans out to sub-agents, each of which accumulates web-fetch results, prior tool outputs, and a growing transcript across many turns; the orchestrator drives a manual agentic loop and re-sends the full message history each turn. On long runs you hit two distinct failures from the same sub-agent. (A) Some non-streaming calls return a successful HTTP 200 whose `stop_reason` is `max_tokens` mid-thought. (B) After enough turns accumulate, the very next `messages.create` call raises `anthropic.BadRequestError` (HTTP 400, `invalid_request_error`) before any tokens are generated, because the input messages alone exceed the model's context window. Your loop currently handles only `end_turn` and `tool_use`, and for everything else logs "unexpected stop" and aborts. Which change correctly handles BOTH (A) and (B)?
anti_pattern: Conflating an output-cap stop (max_tokens, an in-band stop_reason on a 200) with a context-window-overflow (a request-time 400 error), and "fixing" both by raising max_tokens — when one needs the output ceiling raised (or a continuation) and the other needs the input shrunk. A related trap is treating the 400 as an in-band stop_reason to branch on, or treating max_tokens as an exception to catch.
choice_a: Drop the `stop_reason` branching for (A) and the exception handling for (B) entirely; instead inspect the assistant's final text — when it says it was "cut off" or "cannot continue," summarize and retry — to avoid hardcoding stop-reason strings and exception types that may change between models.
rationale_a: Wrong because correct loop control reads the structured `stop_reason` field and the SDK's typed exceptions, not natural-language prose. The model's text is unreliable, may not mention truncation at all, and a 400 overflow (B) produces NO assistant text to parse — the request fails before generation — so a text-based check can never detect it.
choice_b: Treat both (A) and (B) as in-band stop reasons: add branches for `stop_reason == "max_tokens"` and `stop_reason == "model_context_window_exceeded"`, and in each branch raise `max_tokens` and retry the same call, since both failures mean the model needs more room to finish.
rationale_b: Wrong on the mechanism. `model_context_window_exceeded` IS a documented stop_reason — but it is returned in-band on a 200 when generation reaches the window, so it does not fire for failure (B), where the input alone is too large and the API rejects the request up front with a 400 (no response body to branch on). And raising `max_tokens` is the opposite of the fix for (B): a larger output reservation pushes the request further over the context window, not under it.
choice_c: For (A), add a branch on the 200 response's `stop_reason == "max_tokens"` that raises the output ceiling (or continues the truncated turn) so the model can finish the response. For (B), wrap `messages.create` in a try/except for `anthropic.BadRequestError`: the overflow is a request-time 400 error, not an in-band stop_reason, so on catch you compact or split the sub-agent's accumulated history (summarize old tool results, drop or offload stale turns) to shrink the input, then retry the call with the smaller history. Branch (A) on the structured `stop_reason` field; recover (B) in the exception handler — they are categorically different (output-side vs input-side).
rationale_c: `max_tokens` is an in-band stop_reason on a successful 200 (output cap hit) — handled by reading the `stop_reason` field and raising the ceiling or continuing. Failure (B) is different: when the input messages ALONE exceed the window, the API rejects the request up front with a 400 `invalid_request_error`, surfaced by the SDK as `anthropic.BadRequestError`, which the loop must catch and remedy by shrinking the input. (Distinct third case: when the input fits but generation reaches the window, the API returns the `model_context_window_exceeded` stop_reason in-band on a 200.) Conflating (B) with just raising `max_tokens` makes it strictly worse — a larger `max_tokens` reserves more output room and pushes the request further over the window.
choice_d: Handle (A) by branching on `stop_reason == "max_tokens"` and raising the output ceiling. Handle (B) by catching `anthropic.BadRequestError`, then shrinking the history; but instead of retrying by re-sending the rebuilt message list, resume by sending one new user message whose content is the literal string "continue" so the model picks up where it left off.
rationale_d: The output-side half and the catch-the-400 half are both correct, which makes this tempting. The single objective flaw is the (B) resume step: after compacting you must retry the call with the rebuilt (smaller) message history — appending a literal "continue" user turn is wrong. "continue" is specifically the documented anti-pattern for resumption (e.g. for `pause_turn` you re-send the prior assistant content, never the word "continue"), and here it injects an unrelated user instruction into a conversation that simply needs less input, leaving the history malformed.
guide_section: D5 §5.1 — Manage conversation context to preserve critical information across long interactions

stem: You maintain a long-running research orchestrator built on the Messages API. The shared prefix is large and stable: a frozen ~30K-token system prompt plus a deterministically sorted set of 22 tool definitions, with a `cache_control` breakpoint on the last system block, and it stays warm across hundreds of turns of one investigation (verified via non-zero `cache_read_input_tokens`). Two changes are requested for the next release: (1) for cheap, high-volume sub-tasks like fanning out link triage, the team wants to run those sub-tasks on the small, fast model tier instead of the orchestrator's large-tier model to cut cost; (2) the tool library is growing to ~60 tools, but any single turn only needs a handful, and the team wants to stop paying to keep all 60 schemas resident in the prefix. The lead's constraint is explicit: do not regress the cache-read rate on the main orchestration loop. Which approach satisfies both requests without invalidating the main loop's cached prefix?
anti_pattern: Treating prompt caching as if it survives a mid-session change to the model or the tool definitions. The model believes that as long as the rendered bytes of the system/tools prefix are reused, the cache will hit — ignoring that the cache is keyed per-model and that tool definitions render at position 0, so switching the model or adding/removing/reordering tools invalidates the entire cached prefix.
choice_a: Keep one continuous conversation on the orchestrator. For the cheap sub-tasks, issue them as nested calls on the small-tier model that reuse the orchestrator's exact `system`, `tools`, and `messages` prefix verbatim so the small-tier calls read the already-warm cache; and adopt the tool search tool so the 60 schemas are appended on demand rather than loaded up front.
rationale_a: The objective flaw: prompt caches are keyed per-model, so a call on the small-tier model cannot read a cache written by the large-tier orchestrator no matter how byte-identical the reused prefix is — the small-tier call pays a full cold cache-write. Reusing the verbatim prefix is the correct discipline for a same-model fork, but it does not transfer cache across a model switch. (The tool search half is correct.)
choice_b: Stay on the orchestrator model and full tool set, but protect the cache by switching `tool_choice` to `{"type": "any"}` on the cheap turns and toggling `thinking` off for them, and rely on the 1-hour TTL so the prefix survives the cheaper turns; skip tool search entirely since the schemas are already cached.
rationale_b: Toggling `tool_choice` or `thinking` does not invalidate the tools+system cache, so this preserves the prefix — but it does nothing to satisfy either request: it never runs anything on a cheaper model (no cost cut) and never reduces the resident tool schemas (the 60 tools still sit in the prefix every request).
choice_c: Keep everything in one loop on the orchestrator model. Switch the request to the small-tier model only for the triage turns (then switch back to the large tier), and for the library growth, add the ~38 new tools to the existing sorted tool array up front. The system prompt and the `cache_control` breakpoint are unchanged, so the cached prefix is preserved across both changes.
rationale_c: This is the anti-pattern stated outright: switching the model mid-session invalidates the whole cache (caches are model-scoped), and adding tools to the array changes the position-0 tools prefix, which also invalidates the entire cache. Leaving the system text and breakpoint unchanged does not help once the model or tool set changes.
choice_d: Keep the orchestrator's model and its full tool/system prefix fixed for the entire session, and run the cheap sub-tasks in a separate subagent (a distinct API call on the small-tier model with its own prefix); for the growing library, adopt the tool search tool so tool schemas are discovered and appended on demand rather than all loaded up front. Because the main loop never switches model and never swaps its tool set, its cached prefix stays intact.
rationale_d: Caches are model-scoped and tool definitions render at position 0, so the only safe way to use a cheaper model for a sub-task is to isolate it in a separate call (a subagent) and keep the main loop on one model; tool search appends discovered schemas instead of swapping the tool set, which preserves the existing prefix. Both documented agent workarounds are applied to the right place.
guide_section: Appendix — Out of scope for the exam (drill-only)

stem: A research-orchestration team at Halberd Analytics runs a "lead" call on the large, most capable model tier with a 31KB frozen system prompt and a deterministically sorted set of 9 tools; this prefix is reused across roughly 1,800 requests/hour and reliably shows up as cache_read_input_tokens at the ~0.1x rate. For each incoming report, the lead spawns a "verifier" sub-call to independently re-check the lead's claims in a fresh context. The architects want the verifier sub-call to reuse the lead's already-cached tools+system prefix so the verifier pays only the cache-read rate on those 31KB rather than re-processing them at the full input rate. They are simultaneously under cost pressure and would like the verifier to be cheaper per token wherever it can be. Which configuration actually lets the verifier sub-call land on the lead's cached prefix?
anti_pattern: Assuming a forked sub-call reuses the parent's cached prefix while quietly changing one prefix-affecting variable (the model, or appending the sub-task into the system block) — any such change invalidates the prefix and forces a full cache write instead of a ~0.1x cache read.
choice_a: Issue the verifier sub-call with the byte-identical tools array and system prompt as the lead, on the same large-tier model the lead used, and place the verifier's differing instruction (the specific claims to re-check) in the messages array after the cached prefix; confirm the hit via usage.cache_read_input_tokens.
rationale_a: Prompt caching is a pure prefix match rendered in the fixed order tools→system→messages, so reuse requires a byte-identical tools+system prefix AND the same model; putting the only varying content (the claims to verify) in the trailing messages keeps the prefix stable and yields a cache read on the 31KB.
choice_b: Append the verifier's specific claims-to-check to the end of the shared system prompt (so the verifier has all instructions up front), keep the same tools and model, and rely on the prefix match to hit the lead's cache for everything before that appended text.
rationale_b: Editing any byte inside the system block changes the prefix at that point, and because caching is a prefix match, everything from the edit onward is invalidated — the appended per-task text breaks reuse rather than preserving it. Per-request varying content must live in the trailing messages, never inside the cached tools+system prefix.
choice_c: Declare the same 9 tools but let the verifier's code assemble them in whatever order it builds them, keep the same system prompt and model, and set tool_choice to {type:"any"} on the verifier so it is forced to use the tools and reuse the cache.
rationale_c: An unsorted/reordered tools array changes the serialized tools prefix byte-for-byte and invalidates the cache from that point on. tool_choice is also the wrong lever: toggling tool_choice does not invalidate the tools+system cache, but neither does it cause or preserve a hit — so this option both breaks the prefix and misattributes the mechanism.
choice_d: Keep the lead's tools array and system prompt byte-identical and pass the verifier's claims-to-check in the messages after the prefix, but run the verifier on the cheaper small, fast model tier to cut per-token cost while still reusing the lead's cached prefix.
rationale_d: Everything about prefix placement here is correct — the tools+system bytes are unchanged and the varying content is in trailing messages — so this tempts a candidate optimizing for cost. The single objective flaw is the model swap: caches are model-specific, and the cache key includes the model, so a small-tier call cannot read a prefix that a large-tier call wrote. Switching the model is a documented silent invalidator; the verifier would pay the full write rate, not the read rate.
guide_section: Appendix — Out of scope for the exam (drill-only)

stem: You are building a long-running research agent named "Lumen" that fans out across roughly 40 documentation pages using a web_fetch tool, accumulating one tool_use/tool_result pair per page. After about 30 fetches the context window is dominated by the raw fetched page bodies in earlier tool_result blocks. Lumen's workflow is strictly sequential: it fetches a page, extracts the facts it needs from THAT page into a running notes block, and never refers back to a page once it has moved on. The agent does little reasoning between fetches, so thinking blocks are a small fraction of the window. You want to configure a context-editing strategy that reclaims the most window space without breaking the agent's ability to finish the page it is currently working on. Which configuration should you use?
anti_pattern: Reaching for a context-editing strategy by name recognition rather than matching the strategy — and its retention/threshold configuration — to the actual source of context bloat. The subtle form is choosing the right strategy family but configuring it to clear the wrong end of the history (evicting content still in use while keeping stale content).
choice_a: Enable the clear_tool_uses strategy, but configure it to keep the oldest tool_use/tool_result pairs and clear the most recent ones once total tool-result content exceeds the threshold, so the window stays bounded as new fetches arrive.
rationale_a: This picks the correct strategy (clear_tool_uses) and a real trigger (a tool-result content threshold), which is why it is tempting — and the stem's hint that thinking is small does not eliminate it. The single objective flaw is the inverted retention condition: clearing the MOST RECENT pairs would evict the page Lumen is currently extracting from, breaking the in-progress work, while preserving the long-finished pages the stem says it never refers back to. clear_tool_uses ages out the oldest content, not the newest.
choice_b: Enable prompt caching with a cache_control breakpoint on the earliest tool_result so the stale page bodies are read at the cheaper cache rate on every subsequent turn.
rationale_b: Caching only lowers the per-token cost of re-reading content; the stale page bodies still fully occupy the context window, so caching reclaims no window space the way a context-editing strategy does — and putting a breakpoint before volatile, growing tool results is a poor cache layout anyway.
choice_c: Disable context editing and instead lower the agent's effort setting to medium so it generates fewer tokens per turn and the window grows more slowly.
rationale_c: Effort controls how much thinking and tool-calling the agent does per turn; it does not remove already-accumulated tool_result page bodies from the window, so it cannot reclaim the space that is actually the problem and only slows the rate of growth at best.
choice_d: Enable the clear_tool_uses strategy configured to clear the OLDEST tool_use/tool_result pairs once they age past a recent-turns threshold, so the long-finished page bodies are evicted while the in-progress fetch and its result are preserved.
rationale_d: The bloat is accumulated tool_result content (the raw page dumps), so clear_tool_uses is the correct strategy family; configuring it to evict the OLDEST aged pairs matches Lumen's sequential, never-look-back access pattern — it reclaims the most space while keeping the current page's result, which the agent still needs to finish extracting. This is the principle of matching both the strategy and its retention boundary to the actual bloat source and access pattern.
guide_section: Appendix — Out of scope for the exam (drill-only)

stem: Northwind Mutual runs a Claude-based pipeline that extracts structured fields from three document types submitted by policyholders: 8,200 typed claim forms, 1,400 itemized medical invoices, and 250 handwritten incident statements per month. The architecture team built a 1,000-document labeled gold set (sampled proportionally to live volume) and a grading rubric that checks each extracted field against the label, then reports one number: overall field-level accuracy, currently 94.1%, which clears their 90% launch gate. A claims adjuster escalates that handwritten incident statements are "almost always wrong," but the dashboard still shows 94.1% and the team cannot reconcile the complaint with the metric. The lead architect must decide how to change the evaluation so the launch decision reflects reality. Which change best addresses the problem?
anti_pattern: Collapsing evaluation results into a single headline accuracy number, which masks systematically low performance on a minority document type behind a high-volume majority that performs well.
choice_a: Raise the overall launch gate from 90% to 97% and expand the gold set from 1,000 to 5,000 proportionally sampled documents, so the higher bar and larger sample force the aggregate to reflect the handwritten failures.
rationale_a: A higher threshold and a larger proportional sample still produce one blended number dominated by the 9,600 typed-and-invoice documents, so handwritten failures stay diluted; tightening the gate without disaggregating does not reveal which type is failing and may even reject a launch for the wrong reason.
choice_b: Compute and display field-level accuracy separately for each of the three document types on the dashboard so reviewers can see the handwritten breakdown, but keep gating the launch on the single overall accuracy number against the 90% threshold, since the per-type panels already give the team visibility into where the model is weak.
rationale_b: This adopts the correct mechanism — per-document-type reporting — and would let a human notice the handwritten failure, so it is genuinely close. Its one objective flaw is that the launch decision is still gated on the diluted aggregate: a 30% handwritten score next to two ~98% types still blends to ~94% and clears the 90% gate, so the launch ships despite the visible failure. The documented principle is that the gate, not just the report, must be per-category; surfacing the breakdown without gating on it leaves the launch decision exactly as wrong as before.
choice_c: Break the evaluation out and report field-level accuracy separately for each of the three document types (typed claim forms, medical invoices, handwritten statements), and gate the launch on each per-type number rather than on the single aggregate, since the handwritten type is a small fraction of volume and its failures are diluted in the blended 94.1%.
rationale_c: Published agent-design guidance is to report per-document-type / per-category accuracy rather than a single aggregate, because an aggregate masks failures on a minority category; here the 250 handwritten docs (~2.5% of volume) can be near-zero accuracy while the blend stays at 94.1%, so only a per-type breakdown that also gates per type surfaces and blocks the real failure.
choice_d: Move the per-field grading from the same evaluation session into a separate verification session run by a second Claude instance, so an unbiased grader recomputes the overall 94.1% accuracy without same-session reasoning bias.
rationale_d: Separate-session verification addresses self-review bias in grading, which is a real documented principle, but it is orthogonal to this problem: re-grading in a fresh context still yields a single aggregate that masks the handwritten failures, so it does not make the launch decision reflect reality.
guide_section: D5 §5.5 — Design human review workflows and confidence calibration

stem: Northwind Research runs a multi-agent literature-review system on a frontier-tier model (1M-token context window). The orchestrator currently loads all 1,400 indexed papers (~780K tokens) into the window on every turn, then asks a per-turn question against that corpus. The team reports rising per-turn cost and slow, sometimes unfocused answers as sessions get long. An engineer proposes: leave the full 780K-token corpus resident, add a cache_control breakpoint after it (stable corpus first, volatile question last), and rely on prompt caching so the resident corpus becomes "effectively free" and latency improves. The architecture review must decide whether that fixes the problem. Which choice correctly diagnoses the situation and states the right fix?
anti_pattern: Treating a 1M-token window as free to fill: loading the entire corpus into context on every turn and assuming prompt caching makes the resident bulk cost-free and latency-neutral. Caching discounts re-sending the same prefix (cache reads bill at ~0.1x per token, every turn, for the whole prefix); it does not avoid sending irrelevant context, and it does nothing for the attention dilution and latency of a saturated window.
choice_a: Keep the full 780K-token corpus resident and add the cache_control breakpoint after it (stable corpus first, volatile question last). This is the correct caching order, and because the corpus prefix is then cached, the resident corpus becomes effectively free and per-turn latency drops, which resolves both the cost and the slow-answer complaints.
rationale_a: The cache ordering (stable corpus first, volatile question last) is correct, but the objective flaw is the claim that this makes the resident corpus "effectively free" and fixes latency: cache reads are still billed at roughly 0.1x per token on every turn for the entire 780K-token prefix, and a saturated window's attention-dilution and latency cost is not removed by caching. So this discounts re-sending irrelevant context rather than avoiding it, leaving both symptoms only partly addressed.
choice_b: Keep the full corpus loaded and rely on /compact each turn to shrink the context, since compaction reduces the input the model has to process.
rationale_b: /compact (and server-side compaction) is reactive and lossy summarization of conversation history; it does not let you keep re-loading a 780K-token corpus cheaply, and it is not a substitute for retrieving only the relevant subset. It targets long conversation history, not a deliberately oversized per-turn document payload.
choice_c: Stop resident-loading the whole corpus. Retrieve only the papers relevant to each turn (e.g. via a search/retrieval tool that returns a small candidate set) and place just those in context, so the per-turn window is a few thousand tokens instead of 780K. Caching is a useful secondary optimization for any genuinely stable prefix, but the primary fix is to not put irrelevant context in the window at all.
rationale_c: The core principle is retrieve-only-what-you-need rather than filling the window: a saturated window costs tokens on every turn and dilutes the model's attention across mostly-irrelevant material, hurting both cost and answer focus. Shrinking the per-turn context to the relevant subset addresses both symptoms at the source; caching is complementary, not a substitute.
choice_d: Switch the orchestrator to the small, fast model tier to cut the per-token cost of the 780K-token corpus on every turn, trading some quality for cheaper high-volume processing.
rationale_d: Downgrading the model to make an oversized resident corpus cheaper treats a context-design problem as a model-cost problem; the small tier's 200K context cannot even hold 780K tokens, and the fix is to stop loading irrelevant context, not to process it more cheaply.
guide_section: D5 §5.1 — Manage conversation context to preserve critical information across long interactions

stem: At Meridian Analytics, a platform engineer named Priya is wiring a new long-document ingestion service that must pass entire 600,000-token legal contracts to Claude in a single request, so she needs the most capable model and has to hard-code its exact API model identifier and confirm its context window before the on-call review at 4 PM. Her team standardized on the provider's flagship large-tier model, version 4.8, because it is the most capable tier, and the config file requires the literal string the Messages API accepts in the "model" field plus the documented maximum context window. A teammate left four candidate config snippets in the pull request, each pairing a model-ID string with a context-window claim. Priya needs to pick the one snippet that is correct on BOTH the identifier and the context window. Which snippet should she choose?
anti_pattern: Recalling the marketing version ("4.8") correctly but transcribing it into the wrong API identifier format (dotted vs. hyphenated), or pairing the right model with a stale/smaller context-window figure carried over from an earlier model generation.
choice_a: model = "mid-4-6" (the balanced mid-tier model) with a 200K-token context window.
rationale_a: This snippet is wrong on both axes for Priya's requirement: the 4.6 mid-tier model is the speed/intelligence-balance tier, not the most capable model the team standardized on, and the context-window figure (200K) is also wrong for that mid-tier model, which is documented at 1M tokens. The correct config must name the most capable large tier (version 4.8) with its real 1M window.
choice_b: model = "large-4-8" with a 200K-token context window.
rationale_b: The identifier is correct, but 200K is the wrong context window for the large-tier 4.8 model, which is documented at 1M tokens. 200K is the context size of the smaller/cheaper fast tier, and a 200K limit could not hold a single 600K-token contract at all.
choice_c: model = "large-4.8" with a 1M-token (1,000,000) context window.
rationale_c: The context window is correct (1M tokens) and the model family/tier is right, but the identifier writes the version as a dotted "4.8" instead of the API-required hyphenated "4-8". The Messages API matches the literal model string and will reject "large-4.8" as an unknown model. The single objective flaw is the dot-versus-hyphen in the version segment of the model ID.
choice_d: model = "large-4-8" with a 1M-token (1,000,000) context window.
rationale_d: The Messages API identifier for the large-tier 4.8 model is the fully hyphenated string "large-4-8", and its documented context window is 1M tokens. A 1M window comfortably holds a 600K-token contract in a single request, so this snippet is correct on both axes.
guide_section: Appendix — Out of scope for the exam (drill-only)

stem: You are hardening the agent loop for "Lattice," an internal research assistant at a 40-person logistics firm. The loop calls the Messages API with a mix of client-side tools and the server-side web_search tool, and your reviewer insists the dispatcher have an explicit, exhaustive branch for every value the response's `stop_reason` field can take — no catch-all `else` that silently swallows an unhandled case. A teammate drafts the switch and asks you to confirm it covers the complete set of `stop_reason` values a single Messages response can return, so that nothing the API emits falls through. Which option lists exactly that complete set, with each value handled the way the platform documentation prescribes?
anti_pattern: Treating the agent loop's set of terminal/continuation signals as smaller than it really is — omitting a documented stop_reason (here pause_turn) so the harness has no branch for it and silently mishandles a resumable turn, or inventing non-existent values. Correct termination depends on enumerating the full stop_reason set the API can actually return.
choice_a: `end_turn`, `max_tokens`, `stop_sequence`, `tool_use`, and `refusal` — five values. Continue the loop on `tool_use`, treat `end_turn` as normal completion, and handle `max_tokens`, `stop_sequence`, and `refusal` as terminal cases that stop the loop.
rationale_a: The objective flaw is that this omits `pause_turn` (emitted when a server-side tool such as web_search hits its internal loop limit) and `model_context_window_exceeded`; with web_search enabled and long inputs the loop will receive stop_reasons it has no branch for, failing the reviewer’s no-fall-through requirement and never resuming the paused turn.
choice_b: `end_turn`, `max_tokens`, `stop_sequence`, `tool_use`, `pause_turn`, `refusal`, and `model_context_window_exceeded` — seven values. The loop continues on `tool_use` (execute tools, append results, re-call) and on `pause_turn` (re-send the user message plus the assistant content to resume the server-side tool loop, without appending a literal "continue"); it terminates on `end_turn`; and it handles `max_tokens`, `stop_sequence`, `refusal`, and `model_context_window_exceeded` (returned in-band on a 200 when the response fills the context window) as distinct terminal/recoverable cases.
rationale_b: The platform documentation (Handling stop reasons) defines the full stop_reason set as exactly {end_turn, max_tokens, stop_sequence, tool_use, pause_turn, refusal, model_context_window_exceeded} — seven values, all delivered in-band on successful 200 responses. `model_context_window_exceeded` is GA on 4.5-generation and newer models with no beta header. Correct loop termination inspects stop_reason and branches on every documented member, with pause_turn resumed by re-sending context rather than by an appended "continue" message.
choice_c: `end_turn`, `tool_use`, and `max_tokens` — three values; stop on `end_turn`, loop on `tool_use`, and retry with a higher cap on `max_tokens`. Detect any other situation by parsing the assistant's text for words like "done" or "finished."
rationale_c: This both undercounts the set (dropping stop_sequence, pause_turn, and refusal) and reverts to natural-language parsing of the model's text to decide termination — the explicit anti-pattern, since correct termination must inspect the structured stop_reason field, not the prose.
choice_d: `end_turn`, `max_tokens`, `stop_sequence`, `tool_use`, `pause_turn`, `refusal`, and `content_filter` — seven values, where `content_filter` is the terminal signal that a safety classifier blocked the output.
rationale_d: `content_filter` is not a Claude Messages API stop_reason; safety declines surface as `refusal` (with `stop_details`). The genuine seventh value is `model_context_window_exceeded`, not `content_filter`, so this set names a non-existent member and misrepresents how the API signals a safety block.
guide_section: D1 §1.1 — Design and implement agentic loops for autonomous task execution

stem: At Larkfield Logistics, an architect is wiring a Claude-powered shipment-status agent that has five tools available. For the first turn of every conversation, the team has a hard product requirement: Claude must always call exactly one of the available tools (it should never reply with plain prose on that turn), but the architect wants Claude itself to pick which of the five tools fits the user's request rather than hard-coding one. The architect is reviewing the Messages API request body and needs to set the tool_choice field correctly for this first turn. Which tool_choice value satisfies the requirement that the model is forced to use some tool while still choosing which one?
anti_pattern: Confusing the four tool_choice modes — especially conflating "model decides whether to use a tool" (auto) with "model must use some tool" (any), or mixing up which value forces a single named tool versus forbidding tools entirely.
choice_a: Set tool_choice to {type: "any"}, which forces Claude to use one of the available tools while letting the model decide which tool to call.
rationale_a: {type:"any"} requires the model to emit a tool_use block (it cannot answer with prose) but leaves the choice of which tool open to the model — exactly the stated requirement of forcing a call while letting Claude pick among the five.
choice_b: Set tool_choice to {type: "tool", name: "get_shipment_status"} so the model is required to use a tool on the first turn.
rationale_b: {type:"tool",name:...} forces one specific named tool, which hard-codes the choice — directly contradicting the requirement that Claude pick which of the five tools to use. It does force a call, but to a fixed tool, not a model-selected one.
choice_c: Set tool_choice to {type: "none"}, which makes the model select a tool on its own without being constrained to a specific one.
rationale_c: {type:"none"} prevents the model from using any tool at all (it must answer with prose), the opposite of forcing a tool call; the choice's description misstates what "none" does.
choice_d: Set tool_choice to {type: "auto"}, which lets Claude decide whether to use a tool and which tool to use among the five.
rationale_d: The single objective flaw: {type:"auto"} (the default) lets the model decide *whether* to call a tool at all, so it may reply with plain prose — it does not force a tool call, violating the "must call exactly one tool" requirement. It does correctly leave the tool choice to the model, which is exactly what makes it tempting; only the forcing semantics are wrong.
guide_section: D2 §2.3 — Distribute tools appropriately across agents and configure tool choice

stem: You are the architect for "LedgerLoom," an internal accounting-assistant agent built on the Claude Messages API. Each request reuses a large stable prefix: a 40 KB frozen system prompt plus a sorted toolset of 11 tool definitions, followed by a short per-user question that varies every time. Your team enabled prompt caching by adding cache_control to the end of the system block, and you confirmed cache hits via usage.cache_read_input_tokens during a load test where requests arrived every 30 to 60 seconds. During an overnight reconciliation batch, however, individual users sometimes pause for several minutes between turns, and you observe the prefix being re-written at full price instead of read cheaply. A teammate asks what governs how long the cached prefix survives between requests and whether anything can extend it. Which statement correctly describes the cache TTL behavior and the option available to you?
anti_pattern: The near-miss embodies the trap of conflating the cache-write cost multiplier with the TTL duration, or misremembering the default TTL — getting the right mechanism (cache_control with a ttl field) but stating a wrong, checkable value (the duration or the override key). It looks authoritative because the surrounding mechanism is correct.
choice_a: The default cache TTL is 1 hour, and the only shorter option is a 5-minute window set with ttl:"5m"; since your gaps are only a few minutes, the cache should already survive, so the recurring writes mean caching was never actually enabled.
rationale_a: This inverts the defaults — the default TTL is 5 minutes, not 1 hour — and misdiagnoses the symptom, since confirmed cache_read_input_tokens during the load test proves caching was enabled and the multi-minute gaps are exactly what expires the 5-minute window.
choice_b: The ephemeral cache defaults to a 5-minute TTL that is refreshed on each hit, so the multi-minute idle gaps in the batch let it expire and force a fresh write; you can opt into a longer-lived cache by setting ttl:"1h" inside cache_control for a 1-hour TTL, accepting the higher one-time write cost that the 1-hour breakpoint carries.
rationale_b: The platform documentation states the ephemeral cache has a 5-minute default TTL (refreshed on each cache hit) with a 1-hour option available via ttl:"1h". A several-minute idle gap is longer than the 5-minute window, which is exactly why the prefix is re-written rather than read — and the 1-hour TTL is the documented lever to keep the prefix warm across those gaps. For reference, the 5-minute write costs roughly 1.25x base and the 1-hour write roughly 2x base.
choice_c: Cache TTL is fixed at 5 minutes and cannot be changed; to survive longer idle periods you must instead send a tiny keep-alive request to the same prefix before each timeout to refresh the cache, since no longer-lived TTL exists.
rationale_c: This is wrong because a 1-hour TTL is available via ttl:"1h"; the claim that the TTL cannot be changed and that a keep-alive hack is the only recourse contradicts the documented configurable TTL (even though a keep-alive would, incidentally, also refresh a 5-minute window).
choice_d: The default TTL is 5 minutes and you can extend it to a 1-hour TTL by setting ttl:"1h" inside cache_control; because the cache write is a flat surcharge regardless of TTL (roughly 1.25x base in both cases), switching to the 1-hour window adds no extra write cost and is essentially free to enable.
rationale_d: The mechanism is exactly right — 5-minute default, ttl:"1h" override key, a real 1-hour TTL — which makes this tempting. It is wrong only on one minor, objective value: the 1-hour cache write is billed at roughly 2x base, not the roughly 1.25x of the 5-minute write, so the claim that the longer TTL is a flat surcharge with "no extra write cost" is false. The write multiplier scales with the chosen TTL.
guide_section: Appendix — Out of scope for the exam (drill-only)

stem: You are architecting a long-running support agent at a fictional firm called Larkfield Logistics. To maximize prompt-cache hits, the team wants to mark several stable prefix segments — the frozen system prompt, a sorted tool list, a static policy appendix, and a per-customer profile block — each with its own cache_control:{type:"ephemeral"} marker so the cache can be reused at multiple prefix boundaries. A junior engineer notes the markers default to a 5-minute ephemeral TTL and asks how many cache_control breakpoints the Messages API allows in a single request before they must consolidate segments. What is the correct limit?
anti_pattern: The traps embody confusion about the fixed ceiling on cache_control breakpoints per request — substituting a plausible-but-wrong cap (an off-by-one), conflating the breakpoint count with the default ephemeral TTL (5 minutes), or assuming the limit is unbounded.
choice_a: The relevant limit is 5 minutes, since each breakpoint may only persist for one default ephemeral TTL window.
rationale_a: This conflates the breakpoint count with the cache lifetime. 5 minutes is the default ephemeral TTL (extendable to 1 hour via ttl), not a limit on how many cache_control breakpoints a single request may contain.
choice_b: A single request may include at most 4 cache_control breakpoints.
rationale_b: The Messages API caps prompt caching at a maximum of 4 cache_control breakpoints per request. Larkfield's four planned stable segments fit exactly within that ceiling, so no consolidation is required; a fifth marker would not.
choice_c: A single request may include at most 5 cache_control breakpoints.
rationale_c: Off by one. The documented maximum is 4 breakpoints per request, not 5. The value is tempting because the team has a per-customer block they might want to mark as a fifth segment, but a fifth breakpoint exceeds the hard limit — they would have to consolidate that segment into an adjacent cached prefix instead.
choice_d: There is no fixed limit on breakpoints; you may mark as many stable prefix segments as you have, subject only to the minimum cacheable token size.
rationale_d: False. The API enforces a hard ceiling of 4 cache_control breakpoints per request regardless of how much stable content exists; the per-segment minimum-token requirement is a separate constraint and does not lift the breakpoint cap.
guide_section: Appendix — Out of scope for the exam (drill-only)

stem: Northwind Analytics runs an overnight enrichment job that classifies roughly 240,000 archived support tickets with the small, fast model tier. None of the results are needed in real time — the downstream dashboard refreshes once each morning — so the platform team wants to move the job off the synchronous Messages API and onto the Message Batches API to cut cost. A junior engineer is drafting the batching layer and asks you to confirm three things before they ship: how many requests can go in a single batch, what cost reduction batching provides versus synchronous calls, and how long the completed results stay retrievable so a failed morning ETL run can simply re-pull yesterday's output instead of re-submitting. Which statement correctly captures all three points for the Message Batches API?
anti_pattern: The near-miss and distractors embody the trap of half-remembering Message Batches facts: conflating the per-batch request-count ceiling with the byte-size ceiling, misstating how long results are retained, or claiming the discount requires a special parameter rather than being intrinsic to the endpoint. Each plausible answer pairs one true Batches fact with one specific wrong number or mechanism.
choice_a: A single batch may contain up to 100,000 requests (256 MB total), batching costs 50% less than synchronous Messages API calls, and completed results stay retrievable for 24 hours after the batch finishes — so the ETL must re-pull within that window or re-submit the batch.
rationale_a: The request count, size cap, and 50% discount are all correct, but the retention figure is wrong: results are kept for 29 days, not 24 hours. The 24-hour value is the maximum processing time (most batches finish under 1 hour), not the result-retention window — conflating the two is the objective flaw.
choice_b: A single batch may contain up to 100,000 requests (256 MB total) and results are retained for 29 days, but the 50% discount only applies when you set a discount_tier parameter on the batch request; otherwise batched calls are billed at the standard synchronous rate.
rationale_b: The 50% cost reduction is intrinsic to using the Message Batches API and requires no special parameter; there is no discount_tier flag, so conditioning the discount on it is fabricated.
choice_c: A single batch may contain up to 256,000 requests, batching costs 50% less than synchronous calls, and results are retained for 29 days; the per-batch limit is purely a request count with no separate byte-size cap.
rationale_c: This misstates the ceiling: a batch is limited to 100K requests AND 256 MB, not 256K requests, and the 256 MB byte cap is a real separate limit — so claiming there is no size cap is wrong.
choice_d: A single batch may contain up to 100,000 requests (and is also capped at 256 MB total), batching costs 50% less than the equivalent synchronous Messages API calls, and results remain available for retrieval for 29 days after the batch is created — so a failed ETL run can re-pull recent output without re-submitting.
rationale_d: The platform documentation states a batch holds up to 100K requests / 256 MB, costs 50% less than synchronous calls, and retains results for 29 days; all three values are stated correctly, so re-pulling yesterday's output is valid.
guide_section: D4 §4.5 — Design efficient batch processing strategies

stem: Northwind Analytics submits a nightly Message Batches job of 12,000 document-summarization requests, each carrying a unique custom_id like "doc-4471". A junior engineer writes the result-collection code so that after polling processing_status until it reads "ended", it zips the returned results array against the original input array by index — result[0] paired with input[0], and so on — then writes each summary back to the matching database row. Spot-checks show summaries are frequently attached to the wrong documents, and a handful of rows get no summary at all because a few requests errored. A teammate asks how the code should match each batch result back to its originating request. Which approach is correct?
anti_pattern: Assuming the Batches API returns results in the same order they were submitted, so an engineer pairs each result with its input by array position instead of by the custom_id that travels with each request.
choice_a: Match each result to its request using the custom_id carried on the result, building a lookup keyed by custom_id, because batch results can arrive in any order and are not guaranteed to follow submission order.
rationale_a: Message Batches process asynchronously: each request's processing is independent, results arrive in any order, and some entries may carry an errored result type. The custom_id you assigned travels with both the request and its result, so a dict/map keyed by custom_id is the only reliable join — and it stays correct even when a request errors or is missing, which is exactly the case the spot-check surfaced. Pairing by position is unsafe.
choice_b: Match by the tool_use_id returned on each result, building a lookup keyed by tool_use_id to associate each summary with its original document.
rationale_b: tool_use_id is the identifier that pairs a tool_use block with its matching tool_result within a single agent turn; it has nothing to do with joining Batches API results. There are no tools in this summarization job, so no tool_use_id exists — the batch join key is custom_id.
choice_c: Match each result to its request by its index position in the returned results array, but first re-sort the results array by custom_id so it lines up with a similarly sorted copy of the input array before zipping by position.
rationale_c: This is tempting because sorting both sides by custom_id appears to align them. The objective flaw: it still ultimately joins by array position, so the instant any request errors out, is dropped, or returns an extra entry — as the scenario's failed requests do — the two sorted arrays differ in length and every index after the gap pairs to the wrong document. A direct custom_id lookup has no such off-by-one failure mode. The documented mechanism is to key by custom_id, not to re-sort and zip.
choice_d: Match by submission order: the Batches API returns results in the exact order the requests were sent, so zipping the results array against the input array by index is the documented, reliable approach.
rationale_d: This is false — batch results arrive in any order, not submission order, which is precisely the bug producing the mismatched summaries. The API documents that you must key by custom_id rather than rely on position.
guide_section: D4 §4.5 — Design efficient batch processing strategies

stem: At a logistics startup, you are architecting a pre-flight budgeting layer for a support-summarization service that sends large ticket threads to the large, most capable Claude model tier. Before each call, the service must compute an accurate input-token count so it can reject any payload that would exceed a per-tenant 200K-token quota and so it can attribute spend precisely across roughly 40 tenants. A backend engineer proposes reusing the team's existing Python utility that wraps another provider's tiktoken tokenizer library, arguing it is already battle-tested, runs locally with zero network latency, and "tokenizers are basically interchangeable." Accuracy of the count is the hard requirement here, since an undercount lets oversized requests through and a miscount corrupts billing. Which approach should you adopt to obtain the token count?
anti_pattern: Reaching for a familiar third-party tokenizer (tiktoken) or a hard-coded character-ratio heuristic to estimate Claude token counts, instead of the provider's own model-specific count_tokens endpoint. Both shortcuts produce systematically wrong counts because they don't model Claude's actual tokenizer.
choice_a: Call the Claude API's count_tokens endpoint for the specific target model, passing the same messages, system prompt, and tools you intend to send, and use the returned count for both the quota check and billing attribution.
rationale_a: Token counts are tokenizer-specific, so accuracy requires the platform's own count_tokens endpoint scoped to the exact model. It reflects Claude's actual tokenization of the full request shape (messages, system, tools) rather than another vendor's scheme, satisfying the hard accuracy requirement for both the quota gate and spend attribution.
choice_b: Call the Claude API's count_tokens endpoint, but invoke it once for a single representative model and reuse that count for requests to the large, mid, and small tiers, treating the result as identical across the model family.
rationale_b: This correctly chooses the count_tokens endpoint over third-party tokenizers, which is why it tempts a prepared candidate, but it is wrong on one specific, checkable detail: count_tokens is model-specific, so the count must be requested per target model and cannot be computed once and reused across the large, mid, and small tiers.
choice_c: Skip a dedicated token API entirely and estimate the count locally with a fixed characters-per-token ratio (about 4 characters per token) applied to the serialized request body.
rationale_c: A fixed character-per-token heuristic is only a rough approximation and does not match Claude's actual tokenizer, so it fails the hard accuracy requirement for both quota gating and billing.
choice_d: Adopt the engineer's tiktoken-based utility for the count, since it is local, fast, and tokenizers across major LLM providers are close enough for quota enforcement and billing.
rationale_d: tiktoken is another provider's tokenizer and does not model Claude's tokenization, so it produces systematically wrong (commonly under-) counts. That violates the accuracy requirement, letting oversized requests slip past the quota while corrupting spend attribution.
guide_section: Appendix — Out of scope for the exam (drill-only)

stem: You are an architect at a fictional fintech startup, Larkspur Ledger, configuring a nightly batch job that runs an unusually gnarly reconciliation reasoning task across 3,200 ledger entries. You want to dial the model's reasoning to the absolute maximum the API allows, so you plan to set the effort level in output_config to its highest value. A teammate asks which effort value represents the ceiling and whether the model you've selected can actually accept it. You are currently routing the job to the large, most capable model tier. Which statement correctly describes the highest effort level and its tier support?
anti_pattern: Assuming every effort level is available on every model tier, when the highest effort level (max) is restricted to the largest, most capable tier — conflating the full enum of effort values with the subset any given model actually accepts.
choice_a: There is no named ceiling; effort is set with a fixed budget_tokens integer, so you should pass the largest budget_tokens value the model allows.
rationale_a: Fixed budget_tokens is deprecated on 4.6 and removed (400) on 4.7/4.8; effort uses named levels (low..max) in output_config, so budget_tokens is the wrong and removed mechanism.
choice_b: The highest effort level is "max", but it lives in tool_choice rather than output_config, so you must set it there for the large-tier model to use it.
rationale_b: Effort is configured in output_config, not tool_choice — tool_choice controls whether/which tool is used (auto/any/tool/none), so placing effort there is the wrong mechanism.
choice_c: The highest effort level is "max", and it is supported on the largest, most capable tier, so the configured large-tier model can accept effort: "max" in output_config.
rationale_c: Public guidance lists effort ∈ low|medium|high|xhigh|max in output_config, with "max" being restricted to the largest tier — so routing to the large-tier model lets you set effort: "max".
choice_d: The highest effort level is "xhigh", and since it was added on 4.7 it is available on the large-tier 4.8 model, so you should set effort: "xhigh" as the maximum.
rationale_d: The objective flaw: "xhigh" is NOT the highest effort level — "max" sits above it. xhigh was indeed added on 4.7 and the large-tier 4.8 model supports it, but it is not the ceiling, so this answer misnames the maximum.
guide_section: Appendix — Out of scope for the exam (drill-only)

stem: You are wiring a remote MCP server named "ticket-search" into a customer-support assistant built directly on the Claude Messages API (no Claude Code, no managed Agents). The server is reachable over HTTPS and your team has added a single block to the request: mcp_servers=[{type:"url", url:"https://mcp.acme.example/sse", name:"ticket-search"}]. On the first test call the API returns a 400 error instead of letting Claude call the server's tools, and a teammate insists the model should "just discover the tools from the server block." Your lead asks you to state exactly what the request is missing so the connector works on the very next call. Which configuration correctly enables the MCP connector?
anti_pattern: Declaring the MCP server block alone and assuming Claude can use its tools — forgetting that the connector also requires a matching mcp_toolset entry in tools, so the server is registered but no tools are ever exposed (a 400).
choice_a: Add a tools entry of type "mcp_toolset" alongside the server block, but set mcp_server_name:"ticket-search-sse" to point at the SSE endpoint variant of the server.
rationale_a: This correctly adds the required mcp_toolset entry, but the objective flaw is the mismatched name: mcp_server_name must equal the name declared in mcp_servers ("ticket-search"), not an invented variant, or the toolset references no declared server.
choice_b: Leave the request as-is; the mcp_servers block alone is sufficient and the 400 just means you must retry with tool_choice:{type:"any"} so Claude is forced to discover and call the server's tools.
rationale_b: Declaring the server alone is precisely the 400 cause; tool_choice controls whether a tool must be used but cannot expose tools that were never declared via a mcp_toolset entry.
choice_c: Keep the mcp_servers block and ALSO add a matching entry to tools: tools=[{type:"mcp_toolset", mcp_server_name:"ticket-search"}], where mcp_server_name equals the name in the mcp_servers block. Both the server declaration and the toolset reference are required together.
rationale_c: The Messages API MCP connector requires BOTH mcp_servers (declaring the server) AND a tools entry of type mcp_toolset whose mcp_server_name matches the server name; declaring the server alone is a 400.
choice_d: Remove the mcp_servers block entirely and instead attach the server through a vault by passing vault_ids on the Messages request, since MCP auth and servers live in vaults.
rationale_d: Vaults hold MCP auth for managed Agents, not for the Messages API connector; the Messages connector still requires mcp_servers plus a matching mcp_toolset tools entry.
guide_section: Appendix — Out of scope for the exam (drill-only)

stem: You are architecting a high-volume document-triage agent on the balanced mid-tier model for a logistics firm called Meridian Freight. Each request reuses an identical 22-tool toolset and a 9,000-token frozen system prompt of routing rules, then appends one short, always-different shipment-description question. The team enables prompt caching to cut cost, but after deploying they observe usage.cache_read_input_tokens sitting near zero on almost every call — the cache keeps missing. A reviewer notes that the request is being assembled by concatenating the per-request shipment question first (because it was treated as the "primary" input), followed by the system prompt, followed by the tool definitions, with a single cache_control breakpoint placed at the very end. Which change correctly explains the misses and fixes them so the stable prefix is reused?
anti_pattern: Treating the cache prefix as if components can be cached independently or in an arbitrary order, and placing volatile content (a varying user question, a per-request timestamp) ahead of stable content (tool definitions, frozen system prompt) — which silently invalidates the cached suffix on every request because prefix matching is order-sensitive and any byte change in the prefix busts everything after it.
choice_a: The order is wrong — reorder to tools → frozen system prompt → the varying shipment question — but the real fix is that each request needs a fresh cache_control breakpoint placed at the very end of the message (after the shipment question) so the newest content is always cached for next time.
rationale_a: The reorder is correct and this answer even identifies the right render order, which makes it tempting — but the breakpoint placement is the single objective flaw. A breakpoint marks the END of the prefix that gets cached, so putting it after the per-request shipment question means the cached prefix now includes a value that changes every call; the next request's prefix never matches byte-for-byte and can never produce a cache_read. The breakpoint must sit at the end of the STABLE region (after the system prompt, before the varying question) so the reusable prefix excludes the volatile text. 'Caching the newest content for next time' is the wrong mental model — caching reuses a stable leading prefix, it does not snapshot trailing content.
choice_b: Order does not matter for prompt caching because each component (tools, system, messages) is cached as an independent segment. The misses are caused by the default 5-minute TTL expiring between Meridian's bursty requests; switch the breakpoint to cache_control:{type:"ephemeral", ttl:"1h"} and the existing assembly order will start hitting.
rationale_b: Caching is prefix-based and order-sensitive, not per-segment-independent; a longer TTL cannot rescue a prefix whose leading bytes (the varying question) change every request, so this misdiagnoses the cause and the cache would still miss.
choice_c: The request is assembled in the wrong order: prompt-cache prefix matching is order-sensitive and renders tools, then system, then messages, so stable content must come first. Reorder assembly to tools → frozen system prompt → the varying shipment question, and put the cache breakpoint after the system prompt (before the volatile question). Because the volatile question was leading the prefix, any change to it busted the entire cache; with the question last, the tools+system prefix stays byte-stable and is served as cache_read.
rationale_c: Cache hits depend on a byte-stable PREFIX, and the canonical render order is tools→system→messages; placing stable content (tools, frozen system prompt) first and the varying question last keeps the prefix identical across requests so it is reused as cache_read.
choice_d: The toolset is the problem: 22 tools exceeds the prompt-cache limit of 4 cacheable components (you may only have 4 cache breakpoints), so the tool definitions overflow and cannot be cached. Reduce the agent to 4 tools or fewer and the cache will begin reading the prefix.
rationale_d: The "max 4" limit is on cache breakpoints, not on the number of tools, and tool count has no bearing on prefix stability; the real cause is the volatile question leading the prefix, so cutting tools would not fix the misses.
guide_section: Appendix — Out of scope for the exam (drill-only)

stem: Your multi-agent research system is asked to report on "the economic impact of remote work." Every subagent completes successfully: the web search agent returns relevant articles, the document analysis agent summarizes them accurately, and the synthesis agent produces a coherent, well-cited report. Yet the final report covers only commercial real estate — office vacancy rates, REIT valuations, and downtown retail foot traffic — with nothing on labor markets, productivity, or worker migration. The coordinator's logs show it decomposed the topic into three subtasks: "office vacancy trends," "commercial property values," and "downtown business activity." What is the most likely root cause?
choice_a: The web search agent's queries were not comprehensive enough and should be expanded to cover more economic sectors.
rationale_a: The search agent answered the queries it was assigned — all of which were real-estate subtopics. Expanding its queries beyond its assignment would mean the search agent second-guessing the coordinator, which breaks the delegation model; the assignments themselves were the constraint.
choice_b: The document analysis agent is filtering out sources about labor markets and migration due to overly restrictive relevance criteria.
rationale_b: There is no evidence of filtering: the logs show labor-market sources were never sought because no subtask asked for them. Blaming a downstream agent that operated correctly within its assigned scope misreads where the scoping decision was made.
choice_c: The coordinator's task decomposition was too narrow: its subtask assignments covered only one dimension of the topic, so downstream agents never investigated the missing domains.
rationale_c: The coordinator's logs show the failure directly: all three subtasks are real-estate angles on a topic that spans labor markets, productivity, and migration. Each downstream agent worked correctly within the scope it was assigned — the coverage gap was created at decomposition time, which is exactly the risk the coordinator role carries in hub-and-spoke systems.
choice_d: The synthesis agent lacks instructions for detecting coverage gaps in the findings it receives from the other agents.
rationale_d: Gap-detection instructions in synthesis are a useful safety net, but they treat the symptom. The synthesis agent can only work with findings it receives; here the gap originated upstream in the coordinator's subtask assignments, so fixing decomposition breadth is the root cause, not synthesis prompting.
guide_section: D1 §1.2 — Orchestrate multi-agent systems with coordinator-subagent patterns

stem: In your research pipeline, the web search subagent was wired to pass its results directly to the document analysis subagent to "save a round trip," and analysis passes directly to synthesis. In production you see intermittent reports with missing citations, but you cannot tell which handoff dropped them: there is no single place where inter-agent messages can be observed, and each subagent handles its neighbor's failures differently. Which architectural change addresses these problems?
choice_a: Merge the search and analysis subagents into a single agent so the fragile handoff between them disappears.
rationale_a: Merging removes one handoff but sacrifices specialization and parallelism, and the synthesis handoff — equally invisible — remains. The problem is the communication topology, not the existence of agent boundaries.
choice_b: Route all inter-subagent communication through the coordinator, so it can observe every handoff, apply consistent error handling, and control what information flows to each agent.
rationale_b: This is the hub-and-spoke pattern: the coordinator mediates all subagent communication, which gives one observation point for debugging dropped data, one place to implement retry and error policy, and controlled information flow. The symptoms described — invisible handoffs and inconsistent failure handling — are precisely what direct peer-to-peer wiring costs.
choice_c: Keep the direct agent-to-agent handoffs but add detailed logging inside each subagent so failures become visible.
rationale_c: Logging restores some observability but nothing else: error handling remains scattered across subagents with no consistent policy, no single component can retry or reroute a failed handoff, and information flow is still uncontrolled. It patches one symptom of the peer-to-peer topology instead of fixing it.
choice_d: Introduce a shared message bus that all subagents publish their outputs to, letting each agent subscribe to what it needs.
rationale_d: A message bus is still decentralized coordination: no component decides what flows where, error handling stays per-subscriber, and debugging requires reconstructing event orderings. It adds infrastructure without providing the coordinator's central routing, aggregation, and recovery role.
guide_section: D1 §1.2 — Orchestrate multi-agent systems with coordinator-subagent patterns

stem: You configure a Claude Agent SDK coordinator with AgentDefinitions for three subagents (searcher, analyzer, synthesizer), each with a clear description and system prompt. The coordinator's allowedTools is set to ["WebSearch", "Read"]. In testing, the coordinator never delegates: it attempts every research task itself using web search, and the subagents are never invoked. What is wrong?
choice_a: The subagent descriptions are not detailed enough for the coordinator to recognize when each subagent applies.
rationale_a: Description quality affects which subagent the coordinator selects, but it cannot explain zero delegation across all tasks: selection presupposes the ability to invoke, and here the invocation mechanism itself (the Task tool) is missing from allowedTools.
choice_b: Each subagent must be listed by name in the coordinator's allowedTools array alongside its tools.
rationale_b: Subagents are not tools and are not named in allowedTools; they are defined via AgentDefinition and invoked through the single Task tool. Listing subagent names in allowedTools is not a supported configuration.
choice_c: The coordinator's allowedTools must include "Task" — the Task tool is the mechanism by which a coordinator spawns subagents, and without it the definitions are unreachable.
rationale_c: Subagent invocation happens through the Task tool. AgentDefinitions describe what subagents exist, but the coordinator can only spawn them by calling Task, and a tool absent from allowedTools cannot be called. With only WebSearch and Read available, doing the research itself is the coordinator's only option.
choice_d: The coordinator's system prompt must enumerate step-by-step rules for when to call each subagent.
rationale_d: Prompt guidance can tune delegation frequency and quality, but the coordinator is not declining to delegate by judgment — it structurally cannot, because the Task tool is unavailable. No amount of prompt instruction makes an absent tool callable.
guide_section: D1 §1.3 — Configure subagent invocation, context passing, and spawning

stem: Your coordinator delegates a research topic to four independent subagents (news, academic papers, industry reports, regulatory filings). Each subagent takes about two minutes, and the four investigations share no dependencies, yet end-to-end latency is nine minutes. Traces show the coordinator emits one Task call, waits for the result, then emits the next in its following turn. How do you run the subagents concurrently?
choice_a: Design the coordinator to emit all four Task tool calls in a single response, which spawns the subagents in parallel.
rationale_a: Parallel subagent execution comes from the coordinator emitting multiple Task tool calls in one response rather than spreading them across separate turns. Since the four investigations are independent, spawning them together cuts wall-clock time to roughly the slowest subagent instead of the sum of all four.
choice_b: Have each subagent spawn the next subagent as its final action, forming a pipeline that relays results forward.
