rationale_b: Chained spawning is still fully sequential — each agent waits for its predecessor — and it routes coordination through workers, so the coordinator loses visibility into handoffs and error handling. It changes who waits without removing the waiting.
choice_c: Merge the four source-type subagents into two broader subagents so there are fewer sequential spawns to wait for.
rationale_c: Halving the number of sequential two-minute steps only reduces latency to roughly the sum of two longer investigations — the work is still serialized, and each merged agent now covers two source types with a larger, less focused scope. The independence of the tasks is what should be exploited, via parallel Task calls.
choice_d: Spawn the subagents from a background worker thread in your application code, outside the coordinator's agent loop.
rationale_d: Bypassing the agent loop abandons the SDK's orchestration: results are no longer wired back into the coordinator's conversation as Task results, so aggregation, error propagation, and observability all have to be rebuilt by hand. The supported concurrency mechanism is multiple Task calls in one response.
guide_section: D1 §1.3 — Configure subagent invocation, context passing, and spawning

stem: Your search and analysis subagents each complete with high-quality findings, but the synthesis subagent — prompted with "synthesize the research findings into a cited report" — produces a vague report with no source attribution, and its trace shows it asked for documents that were never provided. The coordinator assumed the synthesis agent could see what the earlier subagents produced. What must change?
choice_a: The coordinator must include the prior agents' findings directly in the synthesis subagent's prompt, using a structured format that separates content from metadata like source URLs and document names so attribution survives.
rationale_a: Subagents run with isolated context: they do not inherit the coordinator's conversation history or sibling outputs. Findings must be passed explicitly in the spawning prompt, and structuring them so each claim carries its source metadata is what lets the synthesis agent preserve attribution instead of citing nothing.
choice_b: Enable context inheritance in the subagent configuration so spawned agents automatically receive the coordinator's conversation history.
rationale_b: There is no automatic context inheritance to enable: isolated subagent context is a design property of the architecture, not a default setting. Treating it as a missing flag misunderstands why explicit context passing is required.
choice_c: Add explicit citation-formatting instructions to the synthesis subagent's system prompt so it knows references are required.
rationale_c: The synthesis agent cannot cite sources it never received. Citation instructions address output formatting, but the trace shows an input problem — the findings themselves were absent from its context — so the fix is passing them in, with source metadata attached.
choice_d: Give the synthesis subagent web search tools so it can re-gather the sources it needs while writing the report.
rationale_d: Re-searching duplicates work the search agent already did, may surface different sources than the ones actually analyzed, and pushes tools outside the synthesis agent's specialization — the misuse risk that scoped tool access exists to prevent.
guide_section: D1 §1.3 — Configure subagent invocation, context passing, and spawning

stem: Your support agent investigates a billing dispute across six turns — verifying the customer, pulling two orders, and identifying a duplicate charge of $84.30 — then correctly escalates because the refund exceeds its authority. The escalation message reads: "Customer has a billing problem, please assist." Human agents, who cannot see the agent's conversation transcript, restart the investigation from scratch, doubling resolution time. What should the escalation include?
choice_a: Grant human agents read access to the full agent conversation transcript so no information is lost in the handoff.
rationale_a: The raw transcript buries the four facts that matter under six turns of tool calls and intermediate reasoning, forcing the human to re-do the synthesis the agent already performed. Even where transcript access is feasible, a structured summary of conclusions transfers the investigation; a log dump transfers homework.
choice_b: A structured handoff summary: customer ID, the verified orders involved, the root cause found (duplicate charge), the disputed amount, and a recommended action.
rationale_b: Because the human lacks transcript access, the handoff must carry the investigation's conclusions in structured form — identity, root cause, amount, and recommendation — so the human starts from the agent's findings instead of re-deriving them. This is the structured handoff protocol for mid-process escalation.
choice_c: Configure the agent to escalate immediately when a billing dispute is detected, before investigating, so no investigation context can be lost.
rationale_c: Escalating before investigation throws away the agent's ability to resolve in-authority cases and hands humans every dispute cold — resolution time gets worse, not better. The problem is handoff quality, not that investigation happened.
choice_d: Have the agent attempt the refund in smaller installments under its authority limit so escalation becomes unnecessary.
rationale_d: Splitting a charge to slip under an authority threshold circumvents the policy the limit exists to enforce — a compliance violation, not an escalation strategy. Cases beyond the agent's authority must escalate, with a handoff good enough to be cheap.
guide_section: D1 §1.4 — Implement multi-step workflows with enforcement and handoff patterns

stem: Your support agent queries three backend systems through MCP tools: the CRM returns ISO 8601 dates, the billing system returns Unix epoch integers, and the shipping system returns numeric status codes (2 means "delivered"). In about 4% of conversations the agent mis-compares dates across systems or tells customers their order status is "2." You need every tool result normalized before the model reasons over it. What is the most reliable approach?
choice_a: Add a normalize_data tool the agent can call to convert timestamps and status codes whenever it detects a raw format.
rationale_a: This keeps normalization probabilistic: the agent must notice the raw format and choose to call the tool every time, so misses persist. It also adds a tool-call round trip per lookup for work that should happen unconditionally in the result path.
choice_b: Document each backend's formats in the system prompt and instruct the agent to convert Unix timestamps and translate status codes before responding.
rationale_b: Prompt-based conversion makes normalization a reasoning task the model must re-perform correctly in every conversation — the residual 4% failure mode stays, just at a lower rate. Format normalization is deterministic work that belongs in an interception hook, not in probabilistic instruction-following.
choice_c: Implement a PostToolUse hook that transforms each tool's results — converting timestamps to one format and mapping status codes to labels — before the agent processes them.
rationale_c: A PostToolUse hook intercepts tool results and applies deterministic transformation on every call, so the model only ever sees normalized dates and human-readable statuses. Data normalization done in code at the interception point removes the per-conversation chance of the model mishandling raw formats.
choice_d: Ask the teams that own the CRM, billing, and shipping systems to standardize their APIs on ISO 8601 dates and string statuses.
rationale_d: Upstream standardization across three separately-owned backends is a multi-team change you may never get, and the agent stays broken until it lands. The integration boundary you control — the hook layer — is where normalization can ship today without external dependencies.
guide_section: D1 §1.5 — Apply Agent SDK hooks for tool call interception and data normalization

stem: Company policy caps autonomous refunds at $500; anything above requires human approval. Your agent's system prompt states this rule in bold with examples, yet a quarterly audit finds 3 of 1,240 processed refunds exceeded the cap — one for $2,150. Compliance requires that an over-cap refund can never execute. What must you implement?
anti_pattern: Relying on system-prompt instructions — however emphatic — to enforce a hard business rule like a refund authority cap. Prompt compliance is probabilistic: over thousands of tool calls, a non-zero fraction will violate the rule, which is unacceptable when violations have financial or compliance consequences. Rules that must hold every time belong in programmatic enforcement (tool-call interception hooks or prerequisite gates), with prompts reserved for guidance the model may weigh.
choice_a: Remove the process_refund tool from the agent entirely and route every refund request to a human queue.
rationale_a: This enforces the cap by eliminating the agent's core capability: the overwhelming majority of refunds are under-cap and were being handled correctly. A threshold check in a hook enforces exactly the policy boundary without surrendering first-contact resolution.
choice_b: Strengthen the system prompt: move the refund cap to the top, phrase it as MUST NEVER, and add few-shot examples of correctly escalating over-cap refunds.
rationale_b: The audit already shows prompt compliance at roughly 99.8% — and that residual rate is the problem, because prompt instructions are probabilistic and financial rules need a zero failure rate. Better prompting can reduce violations but cannot make them impossible; only interception in code can.
choice_c: Run a nightly audit job that scans processed refunds and automatically reverses any that exceeded the cap.
rationale_c: Detection after execution means the money moved: reversals fail, customers are confused, and the compliance requirement — that over-cap refunds never execute — is still violated at processing time. Post-hoc auditing complements prevention; it cannot replace it.
choice_d: A tool-call interception hook that blocks any process_refund call above $500 and redirects the case into the human escalation workflow.
rationale_d: Business rules with guaranteed-compliance requirements need programmatic enforcement: a hook that intercepts outgoing tool calls evaluates the refund amount in code, so an over-cap call is structurally unable to execute regardless of what the model decides. Redirecting to escalation preserves the customer path while enforcing the cap deterministically.
guide_section: D1 §1.5 — Apply Agent SDK hooks for tool call interception and data normalization

stem: You are designing two agent workflows. Workflow A reviews every merged pull request against the same three aspects — style compliance, bug patterns, and test coverage — in the same way every night. Workflow B investigates open-ended reports like "checkout latency doubled since Tuesday," where the useful next step depends on what the previous step uncovered. How should each be orchestrated?
choice_a: Use dynamic adaptive decomposition for both, since it is the more general pattern and handles predictable workflows as a special case.
rationale_a: Adaptivity is capability you pay for: the nightly review gains nothing from re-planning what is identical every run, while losing consistency across runs, adding planning tokens, and making failures harder to reproduce. Matching the pattern to the task structure beats defaulting to the most general machinery.
choice_b: Run each workflow as a single comprehensive prompt so the model sees all context at once instead of fragmenting it across steps.
rationale_b: One monolithic pass invites attention dilution across the review's three aspects and gives the investigation no mechanism to gather new evidence between reasoning steps. Both workflows benefit from decomposition; they differ only in whether the decomposition is fixed or adaptive.
choice_c: Workflow A as a fixed prompt chain of sequential focused passes; Workflow B as dynamic decomposition where the plan adapts to intermediate findings.
rationale_c: Prompt chaining fits predictable multi-aspect work: the three review passes are known upfront, so a fixed sequence gives each aspect focused attention with consistent, debuggable behavior. Open-ended investigation cannot be pre-scripted — the subtasks only become known as evidence accumulates — so it needs adaptive decomposition that generates next steps from discoveries.
choice_d: Use fixed prompt chains for both, scripting the latency investigation as a standard sequence: check deploys, then database metrics, then external dependencies.
rationale_d: A pre-scripted diagnostic sequence assumes the cause sits on the scripted path. When the second step reveals, say, a cache-hit-rate collapse, a fixed chain cannot pivot to pursue it — the defining property of open-ended investigation is that subtasks emerge from findings.
guide_section: D1 §1.6 — Design task decomposition strategies for complex workflows

stem: On Monday your codebase-exploration agent spent a long session mapping the payments module, and you saved it as the named session payments-analysis. Overnight Wednesday, a large merged refactor split that module into three packages and renamed most of its public functions. Thursday morning you need the agent's help extending the refund flow. Resuming payments-analysis, the agent confidently references file paths and function signatures that no longer exist. What is the most reliable way to proceed?
choice_a: Resume payments-analysis and tell the agent which files changed so it re-reads them before answering.
rationale_a: Informing a resumed session about specific file changes is the right technique when prior context is mostly valid and the delta is small. Here the refactor invalidated most of the analyzed module — paths, packages, and signatures — so the stale majority of the context keeps competing with the corrections; the mostly-valid precondition does not hold.
choice_b: Fork the payments-analysis session so the original stays intact while you work against the current code in the fork.
rationale_b: Forking exists to explore divergent approaches from a shared, still-valid baseline. A fork inherits exactly the same pre-refactor analysis, so it starts just as wrong as the resumed session — preservation of the original is not the problem; staleness is.
choice_c: Resume payments-analysis and run a compaction pass first, so the summarized context is smaller and easier to override with new reads.
rationale_c: Compaction summarizes what is in the session — including the now-wrong paths and signatures — producing a compact digest of stale facts. It reduces volume, not staleness, and the summarized errors become harder to spot than the raw ones.
choice_d: Start a new session and inject a structured summary of the still-valid architectural conclusions plus a note describing the refactor, letting the agent re-explore what changed.
rationale_d: The resumed session's context is dominated by stale tool results — file reads and signatures from before the refactor — which the agent treats as current truth. When prior results are broadly invalidated, starting fresh with a structured summary of what remains true is more reliable than resuming, because the summary carries conclusions without the stale evidence.
guide_section: D1 §1.7 — Manage session state, resumption, and forking

stem: A senior engineer on your team has refined an extensive set of coding conventions for your service — commit message format, test fixture patterns, API error-handling rules — and Claude Code follows them perfectly in her sessions. A new teammate clones the repository, starts Claude Code, and none of these conventions are applied: Claude generates code that violates the team's error-handling rules and uses the wrong fixture patterns. You confirm the senior engineer's conventions live in her ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md. What is the correct fix?
choice_a: Have the new teammate copy the senior engineer's ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md into his own home directory so his sessions load the same instructions.
rationale_a: Copying works for one person one time, but it duplicates the conventions outside version control: future edits by the senior engineer never propagate, the copies drift, and every subsequent hire repeats the same manual step. It treats a project-scoping problem as a personal-configuration problem.
choice_b: Instruct the new teammate to paste the conventions into the chat at the start of each session until he has internalized the workflow.
rationale_b: Per-session pasting is exactly the repetition CLAUDE.md exists to eliminate: it is manual, easy to forget, consumes fresh input tokens every session, and leaves the team with no single version-controlled source of truth for the conventions.
choice_c: Add the conventions to .claude/settings.json in the repository, since settings files are shared with the team via version control.
rationale_c: settings.json carries configuration such as permissions, environment variables, and hooks — not natural-language instructions. Conventions written there would not be injected into Claude's context as instructions; CLAUDE.md is the mechanism for persistent project guidance.
choice_d: Move the team conventions into a project-level CLAUDE.md (./CLAUDE.md or ./.claude/CLAUDE.md) committed to the repository, so every teammate receives them via version control.
rationale_d: User-level ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md applies only to that user's own sessions and is never shared through the repository. Team-wide conventions belong at the project level, where they are version-controlled and automatically loaded for anyone who clones the repo — the exact configuration-hierarchy diagnosis the exam guide calls out in task 3.1.
guide_section: D3 §3.1 — Configure CLAUDE.md files with appropriate hierarchy, scoping, and modular organization

stem: Your monorepo's root CLAUDE.md has grown past 600 lines because it inlines the full coding standards for every package: the React frontend, the Go API, and the Terraform infrastructure. Maintainers complain that editing standards means hunting through one giant file, and package owners want to keep their standards docs next to their code in docs/ directories they already maintain. How should you restructure so each package's CLAUDE.md stays small while still loading the relevant standards?
choice_a: Consolidate all standards into the root CLAUDE.md under clearer per-package headers so Claude can infer which section applies to the files it is editing.
rationale_a: This preserves the monolith the team is trying to escape: every session pays the context cost of all packages' standards regardless of relevance, the file keeps growing, and relying on Claude to infer which section applies is weaker than loading only the applicable standards.
choice_b: Keep the standards in their existing per-package docs files and reference them from each package's CLAUDE.md with @path import syntax (e.g., @docs/api-standards.md), so the referenced files are expanded into context at launch.
rationale_b: The @path/to/import syntax exists precisely for this: CLAUDE.md files can import external files, which are expanded and loaded alongside the CLAUDE.md that references them. Maintainers keep editing the docs files they own, each package's CLAUDE.md stays a thin index, and the guide's task 3.1 names @import for selectively including the standards files relevant to each package.
choice_c: Copy each package's standards section out of the root CLAUDE.md and paste it into that package's directory-level CLAUDE.md file.
rationale_c: This fixes the monolith but duplicates content the maintainers already keep in docs/ — now every standards change must be made twice, and the copies will drift apart. Importing the existing docs files avoids maintaining parallel versions of the same rules.
choice_d: Delete the standards from CLAUDE.md entirely and rely on Claude to discover and read the docs/ files on demand when it needs them.
rationale_d: Nothing guarantees Claude reads a given docs file before generating code; conventions load reliably only when CLAUDE.md (or a file it imports) carries them. On-demand discovery turns deterministic instruction loading into a hope.
guide_section: D3 §3.1 — Configure CLAUDE.md files with appropriate hierarchy, scoping, and modular organization

stem: Claude Code reliably follows your database-migration conventions in sessions you start from the repository root, but a teammate reports that in his sessions Claude ignores those same conventions entirely, and behavior seems to vary between his own sessions depending on which subdirectory he launches from. Before rewriting any instructions, what is the most effective first diagnostic step?
choice_a: Ask Claude in the affected session whether it has read the migration conventions and to summarize them, to check what it knows.
rationale_a: Claude's self-report is an unreliable proxy: it may accurately summarize conventions from general knowledge without them being in context, or hedge when they are. /memory gives ground truth about what was actually loaded; asking the model gives a plausible-sounding answer either way.
choice_b: Rewrite the conventions in stronger, more emphatic language (bold text, MUST/ALWAYS) so Claude stops ignoring them.
rationale_b: Emphasis only helps if the instructions are in context at all. If the file isn't loaded in the teammate's sessions, no amount of stronger wording changes anything — this treats a loading problem as an adherence problem before diagnosing which one it is.
choice_c: Have the teammate reinstall Claude Code to reset its configuration state, since behavior differs between machines.
rationale_c: Nothing suggests a corrupted installation; the symptom pattern (varies by user and launch directory) points at which memory files get discovered and loaded. Reinstalling is a shot in the dark that skips the cheap, direct diagnostic.
choice_d: Run the /memory command in an affected session to see exactly which CLAUDE.md, CLAUDE.local.md, and rules files were loaded, and confirm whether the migration conventions file is present.
rationale_d: /memory lists every memory file loaded in the current session. Inconsistent behavior across users and launch directories is the signature of a loading problem — instructions living in a file that only some sessions pick up — and /memory verifies loading directly instead of guessing. The exam guide names /memory for diagnosing inconsistent behavior across sessions.
guide_section: D3 §3.1 — Configure CLAUDE.md files with appropriate hierarchy, scoping, and modular organization

stem: Before every release, engineers on your team paste the same 30-line release-verification prompt into Claude Code: check migration ordering, confirm feature flags default off, verify changelog entries. You want this available as a /release-check command to every engineer automatically as soon as they clone or pull the repository, with a single maintained definition. Where should the command live?
choice_a: As a section in the project CLAUDE.md titled 'Release checklist' that Claude applies whenever a release is mentioned.
rationale_a: CLAUDE.md content is always-loaded context, so the 30-line checklist would consume tokens in every session including the vast majority that have nothing to do with releases — and triggering on the word 'release' is discretionary rather than an explicit, on-demand /command invocation.
choice_b: In a docs/release-check.md file that engineers are instructed to reference with an @ mention when they need it.
rationale_b: A plain docs file creates no slash command; each use still depends on engineers remembering the ritual and pointing Claude at the right file. The .claude/commands/ mechanism exists precisely to package this as a first-class, discoverable /command.
choice_c: In the repository's .claude/commands/ directory (e.g., .claude/commands/release-check.md), committed to version control.
rationale_c: Project-scoped commands in .claude/commands/ are shared through the repository itself: everyone who clones or pulls gets /release-check with zero setup, and there is exactly one definition to maintain. This is the project-vs-user command scoping the exam guide tests in task 3.2.
choice_d: In each engineer's ~/.claude/commands/ directory, distributed once via the team chat channel.
rationale_d: ~/.claude/commands/ is user scope — personal commands that are not version-controlled. Distribution becomes a manual step for every engineer and every future edit, and the copies drift the first time someone updates the checklist. It works, but fails the 'automatically available and singly maintained' requirement.
guide_section: D3 §3.2 — Create and configure custom slash commands and skills

stem: Your team's /audit-deps skill walks the dependency tree, reads dozens of manifest and lockfile entries, and produces a long vulnerability report. Engineers complain that after running it, the main conversation is so full of dependency listings that Claude starts losing track of the feature work they were in the middle of. You want the skill to keep producing its full report without its intermediate output polluting the main session. Which change accomplishes this?
choice_a: Add argument-hint frontmatter so engineers invoke the skill with a narrower target and it produces less output.
rationale_a: argument-hint is an autocomplete affordance that shows users what arguments a skill expects; it neither constrains the skill's output volume nor isolates its context. The report is supposed to be comprehensive — the goal is isolation, not truncation.
choice_b: Add allowed-tools frontmatter listing only the tools the audit needs, so the skill runs with a restricted tool set.
rationale_b: allowed-tools governs tool permissions while the skill is active — it does not change where the skill executes or where its output accumulates. The dependency listings would still land in the main conversation's context.
choice_c: Add context: fork to the skill's SKILL.md frontmatter so the skill executes in an isolated forked subagent context and only its result returns to the main conversation.
rationale_c: context: fork is the frontmatter option for running a skill in an isolated sub-agent context: the skill's exploration and verbose intermediate output stay in the fork, and the main session receives the result. This is exactly the 'preventing skill outputs from polluting the main conversation' behavior the exam guide describes in task 3.2.
choice_d: Document that engineers should run /compact immediately after the skill finishes to shrink the conversation back down.
rationale_d: Reactive compaction is lossy summarization applied after the damage is done — details of the interrupted feature work can be degraded along with the dependency noise. Isolating the verbose work so it never enters the main context is strictly better than cleaning up afterwards.
guide_section: D3 §3.2 — Create and configure custom slash commands and skills

stem: Your platform has strict conventions for SQL migration files — forward-only, one DDL statement per file, mandatory rollback comment header — and migration files live in per-service directories scattered across the repo (services/billing/migrations/, services/auth/migrations/, and a dozen more, with new services added monthly). You want Claude to apply the migration conventions automatically whenever it edits any migration file, without loading them into sessions that never touch migrations. What is the most maintainable setup?
choice_a: Create one .claude/rules/migrations.md file with YAML frontmatter paths: ["**/migrations/*.sql"] so the rule loads only when Claude works with matching files, wherever they live.
rationale_a: Path-scoped rules in .claude/rules/ activate on glob-matched file paths regardless of directory location — one file covers every current and future service, loads only when relevant (saving context in unrelated sessions), and new services inherit the conventions with zero additional setup. This is the glob-pattern-over-directory-files advantage the exam guide tests in task 3.3.
choice_b: Package the conventions as a skill that Claude loads when it determines migration work is happening.
rationale_b: Skill loading on relevance is discretionary — Claude may or may not pull the skill in before editing a migration file. Conventions that must apply deterministically to matching files are what paths-scoped rules provide; a skill adds an invocation dependency where none is needed.
choice_c: Place a CLAUDE.md file inside each service's migrations/ directory containing the migration conventions.
rationale_c: Directory-level CLAUDE.md files do load when Claude works in those directories, but this means maintaining a dozen duplicated copies that drift apart, plus a manual step every time a service is added — the exact multi-directory maintenance burden path-scoped rules eliminate.
choice_d: Add the migration conventions as a section of the root CLAUDE.md so they are guaranteed to be in context for every session.
rationale_d: Root CLAUDE.md loads unconditionally, so every session — frontend work, docs edits, everything — pays the context cost of migration rules it will never use, and the requirement was specifically to avoid loading them when irrelevant.
guide_section: D3 §3.3 — Apply path-specific rules for conditional convention loading

stem: You have two tasks queued for Claude Code this morning. Task 1: replace the ORM across your API layer — roughly 60 model and repository files, with at least three viable migration strategies that differ in how transactions and lazy loading are handled. Task 2: a bug report with a stack trace showing a missing null check in one utility function. How should you run these two tasks?
choice_a: Use plan mode for the ORM replacement so Claude explores the codebase and commits to a migration strategy before editing, and direct execution for the null-check fix.
rationale_a: Plan mode is designed for large-scale, multi-file changes with multiple valid approaches and architectural implications — exploring first prevents costly rework from a strategy chosen blind. A single-file fix with a clear stack trace is exactly the well-scoped change direct execution is for. Matching mode to task complexity is task 3.4 of the exam guide.
choice_b: Use plan mode for both tasks, since planning before execution reduces risk regardless of task size.
rationale_b: For a bounded single-function fix with a stack trace pointing at the line, a planning phase adds latency and ceremony without reducing any real risk — the change has one obvious scope and approach. Plan mode's value is proportional to architectural uncertainty, which the small task lacks.
choice_c: Start both in direct execution and switch the ORM task into plan mode only if Claude encounters unexpected complexity.
rationale_c: The complexity is not unexpected — 60 files and three competing strategies are stated up front. Reactive switching means the first stretch of edits happens without a committed strategy, and work done before the switch may follow an approach the plan later abandons.
choice_d: Use direct execution for both tasks, giving the ORM migration comprehensive upfront instructions describing exactly how each service should be restructured.
rationale_d: Comprehensive upfront instructions assume you already know the right migration strategy without Claude having explored the dependency structure — the assumption plan mode exists to avoid. Discovering mid-execution that the chosen strategy mishandles transactions means large-scale rework.
guide_section: D3 §3.4 — Determine when to use plan mode vs direct execution

stem: You ask Claude Code to write a normalizer that converts scraped product dimension strings into structured millimeter values. Your prose spec says to 'handle common formats sensibly,' but across three attempts Claude handles the ambiguous cases differently each time: sometimes '4 x 6 in' becomes width/height, sometimes height/width; "5'" is parsed as feet in one attempt and as a data error in another. The function compiles and passes the happy-path tests every time. What is the most effective way to get consistent, correct handling of these edge cases?
choice_a: Generate five candidate implementations and keep the one that handles the most edge cases correctly.
rationale_a: Sampling more candidates from the same ambiguous spec doesn't define what 'correctly' means for the contested cases — you would be picking a winner by a judgment the spec still hasn't pinned down, and the next regeneration starts from the same ambiguity.
choice_b: Ask Claude to restate the specification in its own words and confirm it understands before regenerating the function.
rationale_b: A restated spec is a self-report, not a constraint: Claude can paraphrase the prose correctly and still resolve '4 x 6' order differently at generation time, because the ambiguity lives in the spec, not in Claude's reading of it.
choice_c: Expand the prose specification to several paragraphs precisely describing every dimension format and the rule for each.
rationale_c: Longer prose is still prose: each rule is one more sentence to interpret, and exhaustively enumerating formats in words is harder to get right than showing the mapping directly. The observed failure mode — inconsistent interpretation across attempts — is the signature of description-based specs, which more description dilutes rather than fixes.
choice_d: Add 2-3 concrete input/output example pairs to the request covering exactly the ambiguous formats — e.g., '4 x 6 in' → {width_mm: 101.6, height_mm: 152.4} — and ask Claude to regenerate against them.
rationale_d: When natural-language descriptions are interpreted inconsistently, concrete input/output examples are the most effective way to communicate the expected transformation — they resolve each ambiguity by demonstration rather than by prose that itself needs interpreting. This is the exam guide's task 3.5 iterative-refinement pattern.
guide_section: D3 §3.5 — Apply iterative refinement techniques for progressive improvement

stem: Your CI pipeline runs claude -p to review each pull request and must post the findings as inline PR comments, which requires each finding's file path, line number, severity, and description as separate fields. The current implementation asks Claude to 'end your review with a JSON block' and regex-extracts it from the response; roughly one run in ten fails because the JSON is malformed, wrapped in prose, or missing a field. What is the correct fix?
choice_a: Run claude -p with --output-format json and --json-schema pointing at a findings schema, then read the validated result from the structured_output field of the response.
rationale_a: --output-format json with --json-schema is the documented mechanism for enforcing schema-conformant structured output from CI-invoked Claude Code: the response carries the findings in a structured_output field that conforms to the supplied JSON Schema, eliminating the parse-from-prose step entirely. This is task 3.6 of the exam guide.
choice_b: Strengthen the prompt to 'Respond with ONLY valid JSON matching this exact format, no other text' and add a retry loop for malformed output.
rationale_b: Prompt-level format demands are probabilistic — this is the current failing approach with louder wording plus retries that add latency and cost. When the CLI offers schema enforcement, relying on instruction-following for machine-critical structure is the anti-pattern.
choice_c: Switch to --output-format json alone and parse the findings out of the response's result text field.
rationale_c: --output-format json structures the envelope (result, session ID, metadata) but the result field is still free-form text — the findings inside it remain prose that must be regex-extracted, so the one-in-ten malformed-JSON failure mode survives intact. The --json-schema flag is what constrains the payload itself.
choice_d: Use --output-format stream-json so each finding arrives as its own newline-delimited JSON event that the pipeline can parse incrementally.
rationale_d: stream-json emits transport events for real-time streaming — message deltas and lifecycle records — not schema-validated findings objects. The pipeline doesn't need incremental delivery; it needs the payload to conform to a schema, which is --json-schema's job.
guide_section: D3 §3.6 — Integrate Claude Code into CI/CD pipelines

stem: Your synthesis agent receives aggregated findings from six research subagents as one long input: roughly 80,000 tokens of raw findings concatenated in the order the subagents finished. QA review shows the final reports reliably incorporate findings that happen to sit near the beginning or end of the aggregated input, but findings from the middle sections are omitted from the report about a third of the time, even though they are present in the input. You need to change how the aggregated input is assembled. Which approach most directly mitigates this failure mode?
anti_pattern: Feeding a long undifferentiated concatenation of findings to a synthesis step and assuming every section gets equal attention, instead of placing key findings at the beginning and structuring the middle with explicit headers to counter the lost-in-the-middle effect.
choice_a: Increase max_tokens on the synthesis request so the model has enough output budget to cover every finding in the input.
rationale_a: max_tokens caps the response length; it has no effect on which parts of the input the model attends to. The reports were not running out of output budget — they were omitting middle-section findings, which is an input-position effect, not an output-length limit.
choice_b: Place a key-findings summary at the beginning of the aggregated input, then organize the detailed results under explicit section headers so critical information is anchored where the model processes it reliably.
rationale_b: This is the documented mitigation for the 'lost in the middle' effect: models reliably process information at the beginning and end of long inputs but may omit findings from middle sections. Leading with a summary puts every critical finding in the high-attention region, and explicit section headers give the model structural anchors for locating detail in the middle.
choice_c: Split the synthesis into six separate API calls, one per subagent's findings, and concatenate the six partial reports into the final document.
rationale_c: Per-subagent synthesis removes the long input but eliminates cross-source synthesis entirely — the synthesis agent can no longer compare, reconcile, or merge findings across subagents, which is the reason a synthesis stage exists. Concatenated partial reports are not a synthesized report.
choice_d: Sort the subagent findings by relevance score so the most important findings appear first, and truncate the input to the top 50% to keep it comfortably under the context limit.
rationale_d: Ordering by relevance helps the top findings but truncating discards half the research outright — the problem was omission of valid middle-section findings, and this approach guarantees their loss instead of fixing their placement. The input already fits in context; length is not the failure mode, position is.
guide_section: D5 §5.1 — Manage conversation context to preserve critical information across long interactions

stem: Your support agent handles long multi-issue conversations. To control context growth, older turns are progressively summarized. After the change, agents working late in a conversation start making errors on specifics: quoting a refund amount of "around $180" when the customer was told $187.50, and referencing "your recent order" when two order numbers (#88231 and #88764) are in play. The summaries describe the conversation accurately in broad strokes but the transactional specifics are getting blurred. What is the most effective fix?
anti_pattern: Letting progressive summarization be the only carrier of transactional specifics, so amounts, dates, and order numbers get condensed into vague summaries instead of being extracted into a persistent structured facts block outside the summarized history.
choice_a: Instruct the summarizer prompt to always preserve all numbers, dates, and identifiers exactly when condensing older turns.
rationale_a: This relies on probabilistic instruction-following inside repeated lossy compression: every summarization pass is another chance for a figure to be dropped or rounded, and once lost it cannot be recovered from the summary. Separating facts from the summarized narrative removes them from the lossy path entirely instead of asking the compressor to be careful.
choice_b: Lower the temperature on the support agent's requests so it reproduces figures from the summarized history more deterministically.
rationale_b: Temperature affects sampling variability, not information availability. The exact amount $187.50 is no longer present in the summarized context — no decoding setting can recover information that summarization already discarded.
choice_c: Stop summarizing and pass the full conversation history on every request so no detail is ever condensed.
rationale_c: Passing the full transcript restores the specifics but abandons context management: long multi-issue conversations grow without bound, costs rise with every turn, and very long histories reintroduce position effects. Summarization was added for a reason; the fix is protecting critical facts from it, not abandoning it.
choice_d: Extract transactional facts — amounts, dates, order numbers, statuses — into a persistent case-facts block that is included verbatim in every prompt, outside the summarized history.
rationale_d: Progressive summarization condenses numerical values, dates, and customer-stated specifics into vague prose — that is exactly the observed failure. A structured case-facts block carried verbatim in each prompt keeps the exact figures available regardless of how aggressively the narrative history is summarized.
guide_section: D5 §5.1 — Manage conversation context to preserve critical information across long interactions

stem: Reviewing transcripts of your support resolution agent, you find this pattern: a customer opens with "I've been going back and forth on this for a week. Please just connect me to a human." The agent responds by asking for the order number, runs get_customer and lookup_order, proposes a resolution, and only offers escalation after the customer repeats the demand a second time. Your escalation policy review flags this conversation. What should the agent have done?
anti_pattern: Attempting investigation and resolution before honoring an explicit customer request for a human agent, treating escalation as a last resort instead of respecting the stated preference immediately.
choice_a: Continue handling the case autonomously but adopt an empathetic tone, since the issue history suggests it is resolvable without human involvement.
rationale_a: Whether the issue is technically resolvable is not the deciding factor once the customer has explicitly asked for a human. Overriding a stated preference because the agent judges itself capable is exactly the behavior that erodes trust in automated support.
choice_b: Escalate to a human immediately on the explicit request, without first attempting investigation or resolution.
rationale_b: An explicit customer demand for a human is itself an escalation trigger. Honoring it immediately respects the customer's stated preference; forcing them through an investigation they already declined compounds frustration and delays the handoff they asked for.
choice_c: Run sentiment analysis on the message and escalate only if the frustration score exceeds the configured threshold.
rationale_c: No sentiment scoring is needed to interpret "connect me to a human" — it is an explicit request, not a signal to be inferred. Sentiment thresholds are also unreliable proxies for escalation in general; gating an explicit demand behind one adds a failure mode where the request is ignored.
choice_d: Acknowledge the frustration, briefly offer to resolve the issue since it may be within the agent's capability, and escalate if the customer reiterates the request.
rationale_d: Acknowledge-then-offer is the right pattern when the customer expresses frustration but has not asked for a human — there, offering resolution for a straightforward issue is appropriate, escalating only if they reiterate. Here the customer explicitly demanded a human up front, which calls for immediate escalation, not a counter-offer.
guide_section: D5 §5.2 — Design effective escalation and ambiguity resolution patterns

stem: Your support agent's get_customer tool accepts a name and email. A customer writes in as "Chris Martin" from a shared family email address, and the lookup returns two customer records: one with an open order for a jacket, one with an order delivered last month. The refund request says only "my order arrived damaged." Logs show the agent selected the record with the open order because "an open order is more likely the subject of a support request," and began the damage-claim workflow against it. What behavior should the agent have exhibited instead?
anti_pattern: Selecting one of several matching customer records by heuristic (recency, open orders, best score) instead of requesting an additional identifier to disambiguate before acting.
choice_a: Ask the customer for an additional identifier — such as the order number or billing ZIP — to disambiguate between the two matching records before proceeding.
rationale_a: When a lookup returns multiple matches, the agent must request additional identifiers rather than select a record by heuristic. Guessing wrong here means processing a refund against the wrong customer's order — a financial action taken on a misidentified account.
choice_b: Proceed with the most recently active record but add a confirmation step summarizing the selected order for the customer to approve before any refund is processed.
rationale_b: This still leads with a heuristic guess and puts the burden of catching the error on the customer, who may approve a plausible-looking summary without noticing it references the wrong record. Confirmation is a good safety net, but the correct move on ambiguity is to disambiguate first, not to guess and verify.
choice_c: Modify get_customer to return only the single best-scoring match so the agent never has to reason about multiple records.
rationale_c: This hides the ambiguity inside the tool instead of resolving it — the tool now silently makes the same heuristic guess, with the agent (and transcript) unaware an alternative match existed. Ambiguity should surface to be clarified, not be suppressed at the tool layer.
choice_d: Escalate to a human agent, since ambiguous customer identity makes the case too risky for autonomous handling.
rationale_d: Multiple matches from a shared email is routine ambiguity the agent can resolve itself with one clarifying question. Escalating every disambiguation wastes human capacity on cases well within the agent's capability and adds latency for the customer.
guide_section: D5 §5.2 — Design effective escalation and ambiguity resolution patterns

stem: An analyst runs a single extended agent session to survey 200 vendor security questionnaires. For the first hour the agent's answers cite specific documents ("Vendor 41's questionnaire discloses subprocessors in section 7"). Later in the session, answers drift: the agent describes what vendor questionnaires "typically contain" and gives inconsistent answers to questions it answered correctly earlier, even though the relevant documents were reviewed in-session. What practice most directly counteracts this degradation?
anti_pattern: Relying on ever-growing conversation context to retain discovered specifics across an extended session, until the model starts citing 'typical patterns' instead of the concrete findings it made earlier.
choice_a: Switch to a model with a larger context window so the full session history fits without any information loss.
rationale_a: A larger window delays the wall but does not address degradation within long contexts — inconsistent recall and 'typical patterns' answers appear in extended sessions even when history physically fits. Durable structured persistence of findings addresses the failure mode; window size only postpones it.
choice_b: Have the agent maintain a scratchpad file recording key findings as it works, and reference that file when answering subsequent questions instead of relying on earlier conversation context.
rationale_b: This is context degradation in an extended session: findings discovered early become unreliable to recall, and the model falls back to generic 'typical patterns.' A scratchpad file persists key findings outside the conversation window, giving later questions a durable, re-readable record of what was actually discovered.
choice_c: Raise max_tokens on each request so the model can produce more thorough answers that compensate for the fading detail.
rationale_c: Longer answers do not restore lost information — max_tokens governs output budget, and the failure is that discovered specifics are no longer reliably available in the input context. More verbose generic output makes the degradation harder to spot, not better.
choice_d: Periodically summarize the session so far and continue working from the summary in place of the older turns.
rationale_d: Summarization frees context but is lossy in exactly the dangerous way: which of hundreds of specific findings survive each compression pass is not controlled, and dropped specifics are unrecoverable. A findings file is deliberate, structured persistence; a rolling summary is best-effort compression of everything at once.
guide_section: D5 §5.4 — Manage context effectively in large codebase exploration

stem: Your research pipeline runs a coordinator with four subagents over multi-hour jobs. Last night the host was OOM-killed five hours into a six-hour run and all progress was lost; the rerun cost a full extra day. You are asked to make runs recoverable without keeping everything in one process's conversation context. Which design provides crash recovery?
anti_pattern: Keeping all pipeline progress inside living conversation contexts with no externally persisted state, so any crash discards hours of completed work.
choice_a: Move all subagent work into the coordinator's own session so there is only one context to protect, and checkpoint it by asking the model to summarize progress every 30 minutes.
rationale_a: Collapsing subagents into one session destroys the context isolation the architecture depends on and makes the single context the bottleneck. Periodic in-conversation summaries still live inside the process that crashes — they vanish with it unless exported, and as prose they are a lossy substitute for structured state.
choice_b: Persist every agent's full raw conversation transcript to disk after each turn, and on restart replay the transcripts through the API to reconstruct each agent's context exactly.
rationale_b: Raw transcripts capture everything but are the wrong recovery artifact: replaying hours of verbose tool results re-fills the context with the same bloat, costs a large re-ingestion bill, and much of the transcript is stale exploration rather than durable findings. A structured manifest of completed work and key findings is smaller, current, and directly actionable on resume.
choice_c: Have each agent export its state — completed work, key findings, pending items — to a known location as a structured manifest, and have the coordinator load the manifests on resume and inject the recovered state into agent prompts.
rationale_c: Structured state persistence for crash recovery: each agent writes its progress to a known external location, so a restarted coordinator can load a manifest of what was completed and resume from there instead of re-running the whole job. Prompt-injected recovered state rebuilds working context deliberately.
choice_d: Wrap the whole pipeline in automatic process-restart logic so a crashed run is immediately relaunched from the beginning with the same inputs.
rationale_d: Auto-restart without persisted state is exactly what the team is trying to escape — the six-hour job restarts from zero every time. Recovery requires knowing what was already completed, which only persisted state can provide.
guide_section: D5 §5.4 — Manage context effectively in large codebase exploration

stem: Your invoice-extraction system routes low-confidence extractions to human review and auto-accepts high-confidence ones. Leadership wants to cut review costs further by trusting high-confidence output entirely and reassigning most reviewers. The pipeline's error rate on auto-accepted extractions was last measured three months ago, before two vendor-format changes rolled out. What should you put in place before reducing human review?
anti_pattern: Reducing human review of auto-accepted extractions on the strength of a stale one-time validation, with no ongoing sampled measurement of the high-confidence stream's error rate.
choice_a: Re-run the validation study from three months ago on the current pipeline, and if accuracy still exceeds the target, approve the reviewer reduction.
rationale_a: A refreshed one-time study only certifies the pipeline against today's document mix; the vendor changes that already happened are exactly the kind of drift that will happen again after the study. The requirement is ongoing error-rate measurement, which a point-in-time re-validation cannot provide.
choice_b: Implement stratified random sampling of high-confidence extractions for ongoing human review, measuring the error rate continuously and surfacing novel error patterns as they emerge.
rationale_b: Auto-accepted output needs ongoing measurement, not a one-time validation: stratified random sampling across document types and fields keeps a continuous estimate of the high-confidence error rate and catches novel error patterns — like those introduced by vendor-format changes — before they silently accumulate.
choice_c: Raise the confidence threshold for auto-acceptance so that only the model's most certain extractions bypass review, making sampling unnecessary.
rationale_c: Raising the threshold routes more documents to a review pool you are trying to shrink, and still leaves the auto-accepted stream unmeasured — high model confidence does not exempt output from novel error patterns, which is why sampled measurement is needed regardless of where the threshold sits.
choice_d: Track the aggregate accuracy metric on the review queue weekly and reduce reviewers as long as it holds above 97%.
rationale_d: The review queue only contains low-confidence extractions, so its accuracy says nothing about the auto-accepted high-confidence stream — the population actually at risk. Aggregate metrics on the wrong population give false assurance while errors flow through unmeasured.
guide_section: D5 §5.5 — Design human review workflows and confidence calibration

stem: Your extraction pipeline has the model output a confidence score per extracted field, and routes fields below 0.8 to human review. Reviewers report the routing feels arbitrary: many flagged fields are fine, while a recurring error — transposed digits in account numbers — almost always arrives with confidence above 0.9 and skips review. You have 5,000 documents with ground-truth labels available. How should you fix the review routing?
anti_pattern: Routing human review on raw self-reported confidence scores without calibrating them against labeled data, leaving systematic high-confidence errors unreviewed.
choice_a: Raise the review threshold from 0.8 to 0.95 across all fields so that the transposed-digit errors above 0.9 start landing in the review queue.
rationale_a: A blanket threshold change is tuning an uncalibrated signal by anecdote: it floods reviewers with the many accurate 0.8–0.95 fields while still passing any error scored above 0.95, and it ignores that calibration differs per field type. Without measuring scores against labels you cannot know where any threshold should sit.
choice_b: Add an instruction to the extraction prompt telling the model to be more conservative and self-critical when assigning confidence scores.
rationale_b: Prompted humility does not produce calibration — the scores remain self-reported and unanchored to measured accuracy. The systematic blind spot (high confidence on transposed digits) reflects what the model cannot see about its own errors, which no phrasing instruction fixes.
choice_c: Calibrate the field-level confidence scores against the labeled validation set — measuring actual accuracy at each score level per field type — and set review thresholds based on the measured relationship rather than the raw scores.
rationale_c: Self-reported confidence is not inherently calibrated: a 0.9 does not mean 90% accurate, and calibration quality differs by field type. Labeled data lets you measure the true accuracy-versus-score curve per field and place thresholds where they route real risk — including discovering fields like account numbers where the scores are unreliable and review needs different triggers.
choice_d: Replace confidence-based routing with random sampling: send a fixed 20% of all extracted fields to human review.
rationale_d: Uniform random review spends 80% of reviewer effort on fields that were fine and lets 80% of genuine errors through unreviewed. Random sampling is a measurement tool; for routing limited reviewer capacity, calibrated risk-based routing concentrates review where errors actually live.
guide_section: D5 §5.5 — Design human review workflows and confidence calibration

stem: Your research system's subagents return prose summaries of their sources, and a synthesis agent merges them into a report. Clients now require every claim in the report to be attributable to a source. Spot checks show the synthesis stage is where attribution dies: subagent summaries mention sources inconsistently, and merged paragraphs blend multiple sources so no claim can be traced. Which change fixes attribution end to end?
anti_pattern: Compressing findings into prose summaries without structured claim-source mappings, so attribution is destroyed at each summarization and synthesis step.
choice_a: Skip synthesis for attribution-sensitive engagements and deliver each subagent's summary as a separate chapter with its own source list.
rationale_a: This abandons the product's value — a unified, cross-source synthesis — to dodge the provenance problem, and chapter-level source lists still don't map individual claims to sources within each summary. Structured claim-source mappings solve attribution without sacrificing synthesis.
choice_b: Instruct the synthesis agent to only include claims it is highly confident about, so unattributable statements are less likely to appear in reports.
rationale_b: Confidence filtering changes which claims survive, not whether surviving claims are traceable — a high-confidence claim with no source mapping is still unattributable. This addresses a different quality dimension and leaves the provenance pipeline broken.
choice_c: Have the synthesis agent add a bibliography section listing every source URL that any subagent consulted during the research run.
rationale_c: A run-level source list is not attribution: it tells the reader which sources existed, not which claim came from which source. The requirement is per-claim traceability, which needs claim-level mappings maintained through synthesis, not an appendix of everything consulted.
choice_d: Require subagents to output structured claim-source mappings — each claim paired with its source URL, document name, and relevant excerpt — and require the synthesis agent to preserve and merge those mappings when combining findings.
rationale_d: Attribution is lost during summarization when findings are compressed without preserving claim-source mappings. Making the mapping a structured, mandatory part of every subagent's output — and making its preservation an explicit synthesis requirement — carries provenance through the exact stage where prose blending destroys it.
guide_section: D5 §5.6 — Preserve information provenance and handle uncertainty in multi-source synthesis

stem: Your market-research pipeline flags "conflicting sources" for human adjudication. Analysts complain that most flagged conflicts are spurious: a typical case flagged "Source A says the market is $2.1B, Source B says $3.4B" — but Source A was published in 2023 and Source B in 2026, so both figures are correct for their time. The subagents' structured outputs currently capture the claim, source name, and URL. What change eliminates this class of false conflict?
anti_pattern: Collecting quantitative findings without publication or data-collection dates, so figures from different periods surface as false contradictions.
choice_a: Require subagents to include the publication or data-collection date in each structured finding, so downstream stages can interpret differing values as time-series points rather than contradictions.
rationale_a: Temporal differences get misread as contradictions when findings carry no dates. With publication/collection dates as a required structured field, the conflict detector and synthesis stage can distinguish a genuine disagreement (two sources, same period, different values) from an evolution over time — and can present dated figures as a trend.
choice_b: When sources disagree, have the pipeline keep the figure from the source with the higher domain-authority ranking and discard the other.
rationale_b: Arbitrarily selecting one value destroys correct information — both figures here are accurate for their periods. Conflicting values from credible sources should be annotated with attribution and, once dated, presented in temporal context; silent discards produce confidently wrong reports.
choice_c: Prompt the conflict-detection stage to use its judgment about whether two differing figures might both be true before flagging them for adjudication.
rationale_c: Without dates in the findings, the detector has nothing to judge with — it would be guessing at temporality from prose fragments, trading false positives for false negatives. The fix is supplying the missing metadata, after which the temporal check becomes a reliable comparison instead of an inference.
choice_d: Restrict research subagents to sources published within the last 12 months so all collected figures describe the same period.
rationale_d: A recency cutoff throws away historical data that research often needs (trend analysis, baselines) and still doesn't guarantee comparability within the window — a January figure and a December figure can differ materially. Capturing dates preserves all data and makes temporality explicit.
guide_section: D5 §5.6 — Preserve information provenance and handle uncertainty in multi-source synthesis

stem: Your CI pipeline runs Claude Code to review pull requests for inaccurate code comments. The prompt instructs: "Check that comments are accurate. Be conservative and only report high-confidence findings." Despite this, 40% of comment findings are false positives: docstrings flagged for stylistic wording, TODO comments flagged as inaccurate, and incomplete-but-correct comments flagged as wrong. Developers are losing trust in the review. What prompt change would most effectively improve precision?
anti_pattern: Controlling review precision with vague meta-instructions ("be conservative", "only high-confidence findings") instead of explicit categorical criteria that define exactly which issues to report and which to skip. The model cannot infer the intended decision boundary from an adverb.
choice_a: Run the review twice and only report findings that appear in both runs.
rationale_a: Consensus filtering suppresses real findings that are caught intermittently and doubles cost, while systematic false positives (the model consistently flagging TODOs) survive both runs. It treats the symptom without fixing the unclear criteria that cause misclassification.
choice_b: Add few-shot examples demonstrating the required output format for findings (location, issue, severity, suggested fix).
rationale_b: Format-focused few-shot examples improve output consistency, not decision precision. The failure here is which comments get flagged, not how findings are formatted — examples would need to demonstrate the accept/reject boundary, and the more direct first step is stating the explicit criteria.
choice_c: Replace the vague instruction with explicit categorical criteria, e.g., "Flag a comment only when its claimed behavior contradicts the actual code behavior. Do not flag style issues, TODO markers, or comments that are incomplete but not wrong."
rationale_c: Explicit categorical criteria define the decision boundary the model should apply: what to flag versus what to skip. General instructions like "be conservative" or "high-confidence only" fail to improve precision because they don't tell the model which findings are wanted — the exam guide calls this out directly ("flag comments only when claimed behavior contradicts actual code behavior" vs "check that comments are accurate").
choice_d: Add the instruction "Only report findings where you are at least 90% confident the comment is inaccurate" so low-confidence findings are suppressed.
rationale_d: Numeric self-assessed confidence looks rigorous but is still a vague meta-instruction: LLM self-reported confidence is poorly calibrated, and the model is already "confident" in the false positives it emits. It never defines which categories of finding are in or out of scope.
guide_section: D4 §4.1 — Design prompts with explicit criteria to improve precision and reduce false positives

stem: Your automated PR review reports findings in five categories. Measured precision: security 92%, correctness 89%, style 88%, documentation 85% — but the performance category runs at a 55% false-positive rate. Developers have started dismissing all review output without reading it, including accurate security findings. You need to restore trust while you improve the performance prompts. What should you do first?
anti_pattern: Leaving a known high-false-positive finding category live while iterating on it. High-FP categories undermine developer confidence in the accurate categories, so the whole review gets ignored.
choice_a: Keep the performance category enabled but downgrade all its findings to informational severity so developers can ignore them more easily.
rationale_a: Downgrading severity still delivers the noise — developers still see wrong findings attributed to the tool, and trust continues to erode. It also buries any genuine performance issues at a severity nobody reads, giving the worst of both worlds.
choice_b: Add "be more careful and conservative with performance findings" to the review prompt and monitor whether precision improves.
rationale_b: Vague carefulness instructions do not improve precision — the model lacks explicit criteria for what constitutes a real performance issue. Meanwhile the category keeps emitting 55% false positives in production, continuing to poison trust in the accurate categories.
choice_c: Require developers to dismiss each performance finding with a written reason so you can collect data while keeping the category live.
rationale_c: This shifts the cost of the tool's imprecision onto developers, accelerating the very disengagement you're trying to reverse. Dismissal-reason data is useful, but collecting it by forcing triage of known-bad output sacrifices trust for telemetry.
choice_d: Temporarily disable the performance category, keep the high-precision categories running, and re-enable performance only after refining its criteria with concrete code examples per severity level and measuring improved precision.
rationale_d: High false-positive categories undermine confidence in accurate categories — developer trust is a shared resource across the whole review. Temporarily disabling the offending category stops the trust damage immediately while the prompt is improved offline; explicit severity criteria with concrete code examples give the category a measurable path back.
guide_section: D4 §4.1 — Design prompts with explicit criteria to improve precision and reduce false positives

stem: Your support resolution agent handles returns with a detailed policy prompt: refunds within 30 days, replacements for verified damage, escalation for policy exceptions. On clear-cut cases it performs well. On ambiguous cases — a damage report on day 35 where shipping took 12 days, a return request for an item bought as a gift before a policy change — its decisions are inconsistent across runs, sometimes refunding, sometimes refusing the same fact pattern. The policy text is already thorough. What's the most effective improvement?
choice_a: Add 2-4 few-shot examples of ambiguous cases, each showing the decision and the reasoning for why that action was chosen over the plausible alternative.
rationale_a: Detailed instructions alone produce inconsistent results on ambiguous inputs; targeted few-shot examples that demonstrate the reasoning behind a judgment call teach the model the decision boundary and let it generalize that judgment to novel ambiguous patterns — exactly the ambiguous-case role the exam guide assigns to few-shot prompting.
choice_b: Route every ambiguous case to a human agent so the model only ever decides clear-cut cases.
rationale_b: Escalation is appropriate for genuine policy gaps, but routing all ambiguity to humans abandons a large share of resolvable volume and your first-contact resolution target. These cases are decidable once the boundary is demonstrated — teaching it with examples is the proportionate fix.
choice_c: Extend the policy prompt with explicit if-then rules covering every ambiguous situation observed so far (late damage with slow shipping, gift purchases across policy changes, etc.).
rationale_c: Enumerated rules only cover the pre-specified cases — the next novel ambiguity is again undefined, and the rule list grows without bound. Few-shot examples showing reasoning generalize to patterns you didn't anticipate; exhaustive rule enumeration does not.
choice_d: Add the instruction "When a case is ambiguous, use your best judgment and apply the policy consistently across similar cases."
rationale_d: The model has no memory of how it decided "similar cases" in other conversations, and "best judgment" is exactly what's producing inconsistency. Vague consistency exhortations give the model no new information about where the decision boundary lies.
guide_section: D4 §4.2 — Apply few-shot prompting to improve output consistency and quality

stem: You extract structured metadata (authors, sample size, methodology) from research papers. Papers vary widely: some cite inline, others use numbered bibliographies; some report sample sizes in tables, others in narrative prose like "we recruited roughly two hundred participants". Your prompt contains three paragraphs of prose rules describing how to handle each style, yet the model still misattributes bibliography entries as authors and fabricates a numeric sample_size when none is stated. What change is most likely to fix both behaviors?
anti_pattern: Piling more prose rules onto an extraction prompt when the model misreads varied document structures. Prose is interpreted inconsistently; few-shot demonstrations across the actual structural variety (including an absent-field-returns-null example) are the reliable way to communicate the transformation.
choice_a: Add a post-processing validation step that rejects sample sizes outside a plausible range and strips author names that appear in the references section.
rationale_a: Post-hoc filters catch only the errors you can express as rules, and a fabricated sample size of 200 passes any plausibility check. Validation is a useful safety net but doesn't teach the model to read varied structures correctly, so precision stays low.
choice_b: Add few-shot examples demonstrating correct extraction from each document structure — including one example whose source lacks a sample size and whose output correctly returns null for that field.
rationale_b: When prose descriptions are interpreted inconsistently, concrete input/output examples are the most effective way to communicate the expected transformation. Examples across varied structures (inline citations vs bibliographies, tables vs narrative) teach structure-specific reading, and an explicit absent-field example demonstrates returning null instead of fabricating — the exam guide names both as few-shot roles in extraction.
choice_c: Rewrite the prose rules to be more precise, adding a dedicated subsection for every document format you've encountered.
rationale_c: The prompt already fails through prose — more prose keeps the same failure mode. Detailed natural-language descriptions of transformations are interpreted inconsistently; demonstrations communicate the mapping directly and generalize to formats the rules didn't anticipate.
choice_d: Run each extraction three times and take the majority value for each field.
rationale_d: Self-consistency voting triples cost and doesn't help when the error is systematic: a model that reliably misreads bibliographies or fabricates a plausible sample size will produce the same wrong answer in all three runs. Voting addresses random variance, not learned misreading.
guide_section: D4 §4.2 — Apply few-shot prompting to improve output consistency and quality

stem: Your invoice extraction tool uses a JSON schema where vendor_tax_id and po_number are required string fields. In production, about 20% of invoices legitimately have no PO number, yet the extraction never returns an empty result — audit sampling reveals the model emits syntactically valid, entirely fabricated PO numbers for exactly those invoices. What schema-level fix addresses the root cause?
anti_pattern: Declaring schema fields as required when the source documents may not contain the information. A required field forces the model to fabricate a plausible value to satisfy the schema; nullable/optional design lets it truthfully return null.
choice_a: Add a validation-retry loop that rejects PO numbers not matching your PO format and re-requests extraction with the validation error attached.
rationale_a: Fabricated PO numbers are often format-plausible and sail through validation; when one is rejected, retrying cannot succeed because the information doesn't exist in the source. Retry-with-feedback fixes format and structural errors, not missing information.
choice_b: Make po_number (and any field that can be legitimately absent) optional/nullable in the schema, so the model can return null when the document doesn't contain the value.
rationale_b: A required field forces the model to produce some value for every document; when the source lacks the information, the only way to satisfy the schema is fabrication. Designing possibly-absent fields as nullable removes that pressure — the exam guide names nullable field design specifically as the guard against fabricating values to satisfy required fields.
choice_c: Lower the sampling temperature for extraction requests to reduce hallucination.
rationale_c: The fabrication is driven by the schema constraint, not sampling randomness — at any temperature the model must fill a required field. Temperature tweaks don't change the fact that the schema demands a value the document doesn't contain.
choice_d: Keep the field required but add a prompt instruction: "Never fabricate a PO number; output an empty string if the invoice has no PO number."
rationale_d: This pits a probabilistic prompt instruction against a hard schema constraint and overloads empty string as a sentinel the downstream system must special-case. The schema still declares the field required — the correct fix is to make the schema tell the truth about the data: the value is sometimes absent.
guide_section: D4 §4.3 — Enforce structured output using tool use and JSON schemas

stem: You classify contract clauses into a fixed enum of eight clause types. Two problems have emerged in production: genuinely ambiguous clauses get forced into whichever category is closest (with no signal that the call was a coin flip), and new clause types your team encounters roughly monthly get silently shoehorned into existing categories, so nobody notices the taxonomy is stale. How should you evolve the schema?
choice_a: Keep the schema unchanged and instruct the model to always select the closest existing category so downstream aggregation stays stable.
rationale_a: This institutionalizes the failure: ambiguous and novel clauses are silently mislabeled with full confidence, aggregation is stable but wrong, and taxonomy drift stays invisible. Stability of a miscategorized feed is not a virtue.
choice_b: Expand the enum to forty fine-grained clause types so every clause has a precise category.
rationale_b: A larger label set increases decision complexity and misclassification between near-neighbors, and it still can't represent next month's genuinely novel type — the extensibility problem is structural, not a matter of enum size.
choice_c: Add an "unclear" enum value for ambiguous clauses, plus an "other" enum value paired with a free-text detail field; periodically review "other" entries to promote recurring patterns into first-class categories.
rationale_c: The exam guide's schema-design guidance is exactly this pattern: "unclear" gives ambiguity an honest home instead of a forced guess, and "other" + detail keeps the enum's consistency for known categories while making novel types visible and reviewable — the detail field is the raw material for evolving the taxonomy.
choice_d: Replace the enum with a free-text category field so the model can describe any clause type it encounters.
rationale_d: Free text abandons the reason the enum exists: consistent, aggregatable labels. You'd get dozens of spellings and phrasings for the same category and lose the constrained-output guarantee for the 95% of clauses the taxonomy already covers.
guide_section: D4 §4.3 — Enforce structured output using tool use and JSON schemas

stem: Your contract extraction pipeline fails Pydantic validation on 18% of documents. Failure analysis shows two clusters: (a) format and structural errors — dates emitted as "Q3 FY24" instead of ISO 8601, line items that don't sum to the stated total; (b) contracts where termination_date is genuinely absent from the document. How should the retry strategy handle these clusters?
anti_pattern: Retrying extraction when the required information is absent from the source document. Retry-with-error-feedback fixes format and structural errors; it cannot conjure missing information, and repeated pressure invites fabrication.
choice_a: Retry both clusters with the same original prompt, since validation failures are usually transient sampling noise.
rationale_a: Without the validation errors in the follow-up, the model has no signal about what to fix and tends to repeat the same output. And absent information is not noise — no number of identical retries manifests a termination date the contract doesn't contain.
