choice_b: Retry both clusters up to three times, appending the validation errors to the prompt each time, and route documents that still fail to human review.
rationale_b: Correct mechanics for cluster (a), but for cluster (b) every retry is guaranteed wasted work — and pressuring the model repeatedly for a value the document lacks invites fabrication. Distinguishing retryable from non-retryable failures before retrying is the point.
choice_c: Skip retries entirely and send all 18% of failed documents to human review.
rationale_c: Format and structural errors are exactly what retry-with-error-feedback reliably fixes at machine cost. Sending the whole failure volume to humans forfeits that and swamps reviewer capacity with mechanically correctable cases.
choice_d: For cluster (a), retry with a follow-up request that includes the document, the failed extraction, and the specific validation errors; for cluster (b), don't retry — mark the field null (or route to review), since no retry can produce information absent from the source.
rationale_d: Retry-with-error-feedback works when the model can self-correct: format mismatches and structural inconsistencies are fixable given the specific error. Retries are ineffective when the required information simply isn't in the document — the exam guide draws exactly this line, and burning retries on absent data adds cost and fabrication risk.
guide_section: D4 §4.4 — Implement validation, retry, and feedback loops for extraction quality

stem: Developers dismiss about 30% of your automated review's findings, but the dismissals are spread across free-text finding descriptions, so the team can't tell which review criteria generate the false positives. You want to systematically identify and fix the prompt criteria responsible. What change to the findings schema enables this?
choice_a: Have the model output a confidence score with each finding and analyze whether dismissed findings cluster at low confidence.
rationale_a: Self-reported confidence is poorly calibrated, and even where it correlates, it tells you findings were uncertain — not which criterion generated them. You still can't trace dismissals back to a specific prompt rule to fix.
choice_b: Add a structured detected_pattern field recording which code construct or rule triggered each finding, so dismissed findings can be aggregated by pattern to reveal which criteria produce false positives.
rationale_b: Systematic false-positive analysis needs a stable, aggregatable key linking each finding to the criterion that produced it. The exam guide names the detected_pattern field for exactly this feedback-loop design: group dismissals by pattern, find the criteria with the worst dismissal rates, and fix those prompts specifically.
choice_c: Survey the development team quarterly about which kinds of findings they find least useful.
rationale_c: Surveys are anecdotal, lag by months, and aren't linked to individual findings, so they can't quantify per-criterion false-positive rates. The dismissal events already happening are the ground truth — they just need a structured key to aggregate on.
choice_d: Cap the review at ten findings per pull request so developers see fewer findings to dismiss.
rationale_d: A cap hides volume without improving precision — the surviving ten findings have the same false-positive rate, real issues beyond the cap are silently dropped, and you gain no information about which criteria are misfiring.
guide_section: D4 §4.4 — Implement validation, retry, and feedback loops for extraction quality

stem: A compliance team runs two Claude workloads: (1) a nightly re-extraction of ~5,000 archived contracts feeding a weekly audit report, and (2) an onboarding check that must validate a new customer's contract within 30 seconds while they wait. API costs are under pressure, and last night's pilot batch run had 112 documents fail on context-length errors. What's the right architecture?
choice_a: Keep both workloads on the synchronous API but schedule the nightly job during off-peak hours to reduce cost.
rationale_a: There is no off-peak discount on synchronous requests — time-of-day scheduling saves nothing. This forfeits the 50% batch discount on exactly the workload the Batches API is designed for: high-volume, latency-tolerant processing.
choice_b: After a batch run with failures, resubmit the full 5,000-document batch so the results stay consistent within a single batch.
rationale_b: custom_id exists precisely so you can correlate results and act per-document; there's no consistency requirement that results come from one batch. Resubmitting everything re-pays for ~4,900 successful extractions and re-fails the oversized documents unless they're also chunked.
choice_c: Use the Message Batches API for the nightly archive workload and keep the synchronous API for the onboarding check; for batch failures, identify failed documents by custom_id and resubmit only those, chunking the ones that exceeded context limits.
rationale_c: The Batches API trades latency for a 50% cost saving with up to a 24-hour processing window and no latency SLA — ideal for the overnight, latency-tolerant archive job and unacceptable for a 30-second blocking check. custom_id correlates request/response pairs, so failure handling resubmits only the failed documents with fixes (chunking oversized ones) rather than rerunning all 5,000.
choice_d: Move both workloads to the Batches API and poll aggressively for the onboarding batch, since batches often complete much faster than 24 hours.
rationale_d: "Often faster" is not a guarantee — the Batches API has no latency SLA, so a customer-facing 30-second requirement can't be built on it no matter how aggressively you poll. Blocking workflows need the synchronous API.
guide_section: D4 §4.5 — Design efficient batch processing strategies

stem: A framework-upgrade pull request touches 22 files. Your single-pass review (all files in one request) produces thorough feedback on the first several files and increasingly superficial notes on later ones, and it missed that a renamed API-client method broke three caller sites in other files. You have budget for a modest increase in request volume. How should you restructure the review?
choice_a: Run a focused per-file pass for local issues in each file, then a separate integration pass that examines cross-file concerns like the renamed method's call sites and data flow between changed files.
rationale_a: Attention dilutes when many files compete in one request — later files get superficial treatment, and cross-file reasoning gets crowded out entirely. Splitting into per-file local passes plus a dedicated cross-file integration pass gives every file consistent depth and makes inter-file data flow the explicit subject of its own pass, which is where the broken callers get caught.
choice_b: Run three independent full-PR review passes and report only findings that appear in at least two of the three.
rationale_b: Every pass suffers the same attention dilution, so the intersection is biased toward early-file findings, and majority voting actively suppresses real issues caught intermittently. Cost triples without any pass being asked to examine cross-file data flow.
choice_c: Keep a single pass but reorder the files so the riskiest ones come first and get the deepest attention.
rationale_c: Ordering decides which files get the superficial treatment, not whether any do — the dilution remains, just redistributed. And the missed breakage wasn't in any single file; it lives between files, which a single crowded pass still handles worst of all.
choice_d: Raise max_tokens substantially so the model can write a longer, more detailed review of all 22 files in one pass.
rationale_d: max_tokens caps output length; it doesn't improve how attention is allocated across a large input. You'd get a longer review with the same shallow coverage of later files and the same blind spot for cross-file interactions.
guide_section: D4 §4.6 — Design multi-instance and multi-pass review architectures

stem: Your team is adopting a GitHub MCP server for a developer-productivity agent. Every developer who clones the repository should get the server configuration automatically, but the GitHub token is per-developer and must never be committed to version control. Two developers also run personal, experimental MCP servers that the rest of the team should not inherit. How should you configure this?
anti_pattern: Managing shared MCP credentials by either committing secrets to version control or falling back to unshared per-user configuration, instead of separating team-shared server definitions (project .mcp.json) from per-developer secrets (environment variable expansion).
choice_a: Commit the .mcp.json with the token embedded directly, relying on the repository being private to protect the credential.
rationale_a: Committing a live credential to version control is a leak regardless of repository visibility: it persists in git history, is exposed to every collaborator and CI system, and cannot be rotated per developer. Environment variable expansion exists precisely so shared server config and private credentials can be separated.
choice_b: Have each developer add the GitHub server to their user-level ~/.claude.json with their token inlined, since user scope keeps credentials out of the repository.
rationale_b: User scope does keep tokens out of version control, but it abandons the requirement that the shared server arrive automatically with a clone: every developer must hand-configure it, configurations drift, and new teammates silently lack the integration. Project-scoped .mcp.json with env expansion achieves both goals at once.
choice_c: Document the server command and token setup in CLAUDE.md so the agent reads the instructions and connects to the server itself.
rationale_c: CLAUDE.md provides instructions and project context to the model; it is not a server registration mechanism. MCP servers are discovered from configuration files like .mcp.json or ~/.claude.json at connection time, not launched by the model reading prose documentation.
choice_d: Define the GitHub server in a project-level .mcp.json checked into the repository, referencing the token as ${GITHUB_TOKEN} via environment variable expansion, and have developers register their experimental servers at user level in ~/.claude.json.
rationale_d: Project-scoped .mcp.json is version-controlled and shared with everyone who clones the repo, while environment variable expansion like ${GITHUB_TOKEN} resolves credentials from each developer's environment at connection time so no secret is ever committed. User-level ~/.claude.json is exactly the scope for personal or experimental servers that should not leak to teammates.
guide_section: D2 §2.4 — Integrate MCP servers into Claude Code and agent workflows

stem: Your developer-productivity agent integrates with your issue tracker through a custom MCP server that exposes search_issues and get_issue tools. Session transcripts show the agent burning its first several turns on exploratory search_issues calls just to discover what projects, labels, and open epics exist before it can address the user's actual request. You want the agent to know what content is available up front without those discovery calls. What is the best change to the MCP server?
choice_a: Expose the catalog — project list, label taxonomy, and open epic summaries — as MCP resources that can be attached or read directly, keeping tools for targeted actions like fetching or searching specific issues.
rationale_a: MCP resources are the mechanism for exposing content catalogs (issue summaries, documentation hierarchies, database schemas) so the agent has visibility into available data without spending tool calls on exploration. Tools remain the right surface for parameterized actions; resources answer the standing question of what exists.
choice_b: Add a list_everything tool that returns all projects, labels, epics, and issue summaries in one response, and instruct the agent to call it at the start of every session.
rationale_b: This still spends an exploratory tool call every session and dumps the entire catalog into context whether or not it is relevant. It converts many discovery calls into one large one rather than eliminating discovery, which is exactly what exposing the catalog as MCP resources accomplishes.
choice_c: Add a system prompt instruction telling the agent to remember project and label names from previous sessions instead of re-discovering them.
rationale_c: Sessions do not share conversation state, so the agent has nothing to remember from prior sessions; the instruction invites the model to fabricate plausible-sounding project names. Availability of content must come from the server surface, not from imagined memory.
choice_d: Embed the current list of projects, labels, and epics into the search_issues tool description so the agent sees it whenever it inspects its tools.
rationale_d: Tool descriptions are static configuration meant to explain purpose, inputs, and boundaries. Stuffing live catalog data into them bloats every request, goes stale the moment the tracker changes, and abuses the description field as a data channel.
guide_section: D2 §2.4 — Integrate MCP servers into Claude Code and agent workflows

stem: Your Agent SDK productivity agent (with Read, Write, Bash, Grep, and Glob) is asked where the feature flag ENABLE_ASYNC_BILLING is handled in a large, unfamiliar monorepo, and how the value flows from configuration to the billing module. Which tool strategy should the agent use?
choice_a: Ask the user to paste the relevant configuration and billing files into the conversation, since they know the codebase best.
rationale_a: The premise is an unfamiliar codebase and an agent equipped with search tools; deferring discovery to the user defeats the purpose of codebase-exploration tooling and fails when the user does not know which files are relevant — which is the reason they asked.
choice_b: Grep for ENABLE_ASYNC_BILLING across the repo to find the entry points, then Read those specific files and follow their imports with further targeted Grep and Read calls to trace the flow.
rationale_b: Grep is the content-search tool, so it locates every file that references the flag regardless of file name. Building understanding incrementally — search for entry points, then Read only the implicated files and follow imports — traces the data flow without flooding context with irrelevant code.
choice_c: Glob for file names containing billing or flags (e.g., **/*billing*, **/*flag*), then Read each match to look for the constant.
rationale_c: Glob matches file path patterns, not file contents. The flag may be referenced in files whose names mention neither billing nor flags (config loaders, DI wiring, tests), so name-based discovery both misses handlers and pulls in files that never touch the constant. Content search is Grep's job.
choice_d: Read every file under src/ upfront so the agent has complete context before answering, then trace the flag from memory.
rationale_d: Reading a large repository wholesale exhausts the context window on code that is irrelevant to the question and degrades reasoning quality. The effective pattern is incremental: search first, read only what the search implicates.
guide_section: D2 §2.5 — Select and apply built-in tools (Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob) effectively

stem: Your agent must change the timeout in one specific handler inside a generated service file. The line timeout_ms = 5000 appears six times, and because the handlers are code-generated, the surrounding lines are byte-identical in every block. The agent's Edit call fails because the target text does not match uniquely. What should the agent do?
choice_a: Read the full file, construct the corrected content with only the intended handler's value changed, and Write the file back.
rationale_a: When Edit cannot anchor on a unique text match, Read followed by Write is the reliable fallback: the agent sees the whole file, can identify the intended block positionally, and writes back a complete, verified result. This is the documented recovery path for non-unique matches.
choice_b: Delete the file and Write a fresh copy from the agent's memory of its contents, incorporating the new timeout.
rationale_b: Reconstructing a file from the model's recollection rather than a fresh Read invites omissions and hallucinated details, and deleting first removes the safety net if the rewrite is wrong. The fallback is Read-then-Write, never write-from-memory.
choice_c: Use Bash to run sed -i 's/timeout_ms = 5000/timeout_ms = 30000/' on the file to make the change in one step.
rationale_c: An unanchored global substitution edits all six occurrences, changing the timeout of five handlers that must keep their current value. It trades a failed edit for a silent incorrect one.
choice_d: Retry Edit with a larger snippet that includes several surrounding lines, giving the match more context to latch onto.
rationale_d: Expanding the anchor is the right first instinct when nearby lines differ, but the stem states the surrounding lines are byte-identical across all six generated blocks, so a larger snippet is still non-unique and Edit fails the same way. That is precisely the situation where the Read + Write fallback applies.
guide_section: D2 §2.5 — Select and apply built-in tools (Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob) effectively
